confirmed.summary: gain-loss-asymmetry is served from the 'not yet in glossary.py' fallback even though glossary.py now HAS the entry, so the dashboard label (上下非対称) contradicts the glossary rendering (利得-損失非対称性) that every LLM prompt is instructed to use — violating the module's declared 'single source of truth = glossary.py' contract.
confirmed.evidence: _FACT_FALLBACK comment: "# Cont 2001 stylized facts not yet in glossary.py — add fallback labels" with "gain-loss-asymmetry": ("上下非対称 (gain-loss asymmetry)", ...). But glossary.lookup("gain-loss asymmetry") returns {'en': 'gain-loss asymmetry', 'ja_primary': '利得-損失非対称性', 'notes': '上向き運動と下向き運動の statistics が非対称な性質。...'}. Because _FACT_TO_GLOSSARY_EN (line 62-72) lacks the 'gain-loss-asymmetry' key, _glossary_entry() returns None and fact_label/fact_gloss silently use the stale fallback, including a fallback gloss that differs from the glossary notes.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: fact_label('gain-loss-asymmetry') == '上下非対称 (gain-loss asymmetry)' while glossary ja_primary == '利得-損失非対称性'. A view-A gap on gain-loss-asymmetry renders 上下非対称 in the dashboard, while gap→proposal LLM output (format_for_prompt mandates 'reject 訳は絶対に生成しないこと' with 利得-損失非対称性) uses the other term — the researcher sees two different Japanese names for the same fact across the pipeline, and a later glossary edit to this entry silently has no effect on the dashboard.
confirmed.suggestion: Add "gain-loss-asymmetry": "gain-loss asymmetry" to _FACT_TO_GLOSSARY_EN, demote the fallback entry to gloss-only (volume-volatility-corr genuinely has no glossary entry and legitimately stays), and fix the stale comment.

confirmed.summary: _copy_asset keys copied assets by basename only, so two different source PNGs with the same filename silently collapse onto one assets/<name> file and the mtime freshness check decides which image *both* figures display.
confirmed.evidence:
dst = assets / src.name
    if not dst.exists() or dst.stat().st_mtime < src.stat().st_mtime:
        shutil.copy2(src, dst)
    return f"assets/{src.name}" — the returned href ignores the source directory entirely, and copy is skipped when the second source's mtime is older, without any collision detection.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: _copy_asset(out, 'srcA/same.png') then _copy_asset(out, 'srcB/same.png') (B older) both return 'assets/same.png' and the file contains srcA's bytes — the second figure renders the wrong image with no warning. Latent today (the 7 hard-coded sources have unique basenames), but the moment someone adds e.g. notebooks/atlas_v5/atlas.png alongside notebooks/atlas_v4/atlas.png to compare versions, both figures show the same (mtime-newest) plot — a researcher comparing v4 vs v5 PCA maps sees two identical images and concludes 'no change'.
confirmed.suggestion: Namespace the destination (e.g. parent-dir prefix or short content hash: assets/atlas_v4__atlas.png), or raise/warn when a basename maps to a different resolved source than a previous call in the same build.

confirmed.summary: cmd_promote_proposal creates a dead-end idea row: status='judged' with judgment_json NULL, so the advertised next step (`plan --id N`) always refuses, and no CLI path can ever attach a judgment to an existing idea.
confirmed.evidence: idea_cli.py:280 `idea_id = insert_idea(args.db, idea_text=idea_text, status="judged")` passes no aspects/judgment (db.insert_idea stores NULL). Line 284 then prints `print(f"Next: `idea_cli --db {args.db} plan --id {idea_id}`")`. But cmd_plan line 90-91: `if not idea["judgment"]: print("idea has no judgment yet — run `judge` first.")` returns 1. The `judge` sub-command only accepts --idea/--idea-file and always inserts a NEW row (line 73), so there is no way to judge an existing idea id; grep confirms no other module calls update_idea with a judgment.
confirmed.failure_scenario: User runs `idea_cli --db x.db promote-proposal --proposal-id 7` (task #83's gap_mine→idea flow), then the printed next command `plan --id N`. plan exits 1 telling them to 'run judge first', which is impossible for that row without losing the proposal_ids provenance link the promote feature exists to create. The entire promote→plan→scaffold flow is broken 100% of the time. Reproduced on a temp DB: row has status='judged', judgment=None, and cmd_plan's guard fires.
confirmed.suggestion: Have cmd_promote_proposal run judge_idea() on the constructed idea_text before insert (or add a `judge --id N` mode that updates an existing row via update_idea).

confirmed.summary: scaffold_mechanism_combo/scaffold_new_method unconditionally overwrite module files at a path keyed only by snake(class name), silently destroying human-completed model code on re-scaffold or cross-idea name collision.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 259-261 `module_dir = os.path.join(packages_root, "abm_models", "abm_models", f"_idea_{snake}")` + `os.makedirs(module_dir, exist_ok=True)`; lines 309-312 `with open(init_path, "w") as fh: ... with open(model_path, "w") as fh: fh.write(model_body)`. No exists-check, no backup, and the dir name contains no idea_id. cmd_scaffold explicitly permits re-scaffold (idea_cli.py:130 allows status 'planned'/'scaffolded'/'executed'), and the workflow's contract is that a human fills in the `TODO(human)` in model.py in place. Repo already contains two near-collision dirs (_idea_self_organizing_speculation_game / _idea_speculation_game_self_organized from ideas #4 and #6).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Human completes the step logic in packages/abm_models/abm_models/_idea_self_organized_sg/model.py after scaffolding idea #4; later `scaffold --id 4` is re-run (allowed) or idea #9's LLM picks the same CamelCase class name (likely for similar ideas). Reproduced: file content after second scaffold call has 'human work survived: False' and the docstring now claims idea #9 — days of research code silently replaced by the TODO placeholder, with no git safety if the user hadn't committed.
confirmed.suggestion: Include idea_id in the module dir (`_idea_{idea_id}_{snake}`), and refuse to overwrite an existing model.py unless a --force flag is given (or write model.py.new).

confirmed.summary: scaffold_param_sweep validates only that required param keys are present — extra LLM-hallucinated keys and arbitrarily out-of-bounds values pass straight into the proposals table, guaranteeing execute-time failure or garbage runs polluting the atlas.
confirmed.evidence: Line 215: `missing = set(MODEL_BOUNDS[target_model].keys()) - set(params.keys())` — asymmetric: extras undetected, values never compared to bounds even though PLAN_SYSTEM_PROMPT line 118-119 promises 'params MUST ... stay within ±30% of the bounds'. Nothing downstream checks either: propose._validate_proposal (line 387) also only checks key presence, and execute_proposal → build_model does `cls(**p)`.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: plan with params {'N': 5000000, 'M': 4, 'S': 2, 'T': 2500, 'herding_gamma': 0.7} for minority_game (bounds N=(51,151)) is accepted by scaffold_param_sweep and inserted as a proposal. At execute (incl. `run --auto-execute` and CI), build_model raises `TypeError: MinorityGame.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'herding_gamma'` and execute_proposal permanently marks the proposal status='rejected'. Without the extra key, N=5,000,000 would instead launch a 5M-agent MG (memory/time blowup in CI), and any in-bounds-key/wild-value run gets recorded in the runs table, corrupting atlas novelty distances.
confirmed.suggestion: Reject unknown keys, and enforce the ±30% tolerance the prompt promises: `lo - 0.3*(hi-lo) <= v <= hi + 0.3*(hi-lo)` per key, with a clear error naming the offending param.

confirmed.summary: mechanism_combo/new_method scaffolds interpolate unvalidated LLM strings into Python source: non-identifier class names or triple-quotes in prose produce non-compiling modules, and base_method_a is never checked against REGISTRY — yet the scaffold reports success and the idea is marked 'scaffolded'.
confirmed.evidence: Line 252 `name = mc.get("new_class_name") or f"IdeaCombo{idea_id}"` — `name` is written raw into `from .model import {name}`, `__all__ = ["{name}"]` and `class {name}:` with no isidentifier() check; desc/behaviour go into the docstring via textwrap.fill with no `\"\"\"` escaping; line 304 generates `BaseA = REGISTRY["{base_a}"]` but base_method_a comes from candidate_methods, whose source table also contains garch11/levy_walk/regime_switch (methods.py lines 157-181) which are NOT REGISTRY keys. There is no py_compile/import validation anywhere in idea_plan.py.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: new_class_name='LLM-Sentiment Hybrid' (plausible LLM output) → both generated files fail py_compile; combination_strategy containing '"""' → model.py fails py_compile; base_method_a='garch11' compiles but the placeholder run() raises KeyError the moment the human tries it. In every case scaffold() returns success, cmd_scaffold sets status='scaffolded', and the breakage is only discovered later.
confirmed.suggestion: Validate `name.isidentifier()` (fall back to IdeaCombo{idea_id}), assert base methods ∈ REGISTRY (or emit a loud TODO), escape/strip triple quotes from prose, and py_compile the written files, recording failures on the idea row.

confirmed.summary: _filter_verdict_arxiv_ids assumes closest_literature_arxiv_ids is a list of strings: a bare-string value is iterated character-by-character (silently discarding a valid citation and persisting per-character 'hallucinated' warnings), and a null element crashes the whole judgment after both paid LLM calls.
confirmed.evidence: Line 296 `ids = verdict.get("closest_literature_arxiv_ids") or []` followed by `for cited in ids:` and line 311 `aid = _extract_arxiv_id(cited) or cited` (propose._extract_arxiv_id does `reference.strip()`). No isinstance checks on ids or elements. Also, contra the docstring ('hallucinated: id parses as arxiv but isn't anywhere in the DB'), non-parsing free-form citations like 'Lux & Marchesi 2000' are also dropped and labelled hallucinated, and the `except Exception: pass` at line 305 silently empties db_bases on any DB error, reclassifying real in-DB citations as hallucinated.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: verdict {'closest_literature_arxiv_ids': '2503.00320v2'} (a common LLM JSON slip) with that paper in candidates → verdict field replaced with [] and warnings.hallucinated = ['2','5','0','3','.','0','0','3','2','0','v','2'] persisted to the DB. verdict ['2503.00320v2', None] → AttributeError 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'strip'; cmd_judge catches it and returns 1 without inserting the idea, so both LLM calls are wasted and the judgment is lost.
confirmed.suggestion: Coerce a str value to [value], skip non-str elements with a warning bucket, and match classify_references' non_arxiv (unflagged) handling for free-form citations.

confirmed.summary: make_plan's `r.get("code_url")` is dead code: rank_methods' sibling rank_literature strips code_url from its output rows, so papers with a code_url but no cached snapshot never reach candidate_literature_code — partially defeating the repo-context-injection feature (tasks #39/#40).
confirmed.evidence: idea_plan.py:159 `url = r.get("code_url")` reads rows produced by idea_judge.rank_literature (lines 179-186), which emits only {arxiv_id, title, year, mechanism_summary, mechanism_tags, score, relevance_score} even though db.load_literature returns code_url (db.py:413). Hence `url` is always None and line 160 `if not (url or snap): continue` admits only snapshot-bearing papers; the fallback `"code_url": url or (snap and snap.get("code_url"))` never uses the literature row's URL.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: seeded a temp DB with a paper whose code_url='https://github.com/x/tribe' but no literature_code_snapshots row; it ranks #1 for the idea, yet the payload sent to the plan LLM has candidate_literature_code=[] — the plan loses the structural anchor the PLAN_SYSTEM_PROMPT (lines 95-100) tells the LLM to use.
confirmed.suggestion: Add code_url to rank_literature's output dict (it is already loaded), or fetch code_url via load_literature inside make_plan.

confirmed.summary: cmd_list crashes on legal DB states: IndexError for an empty idea_text and TypeError for an explicit null category/implementation_type, making `list` unusable until the row is hand-fixed.
confirmed.evidence: Line 298 `head = r["idea_text"].splitlines()[0][:60]` — `"".splitlines()` is [] (same bug class as the rank_proposals regression test at tests/test_idea.py:200). Line 299 `f"... {cat:<22s} {impl:<16s} ..."` — `verdict.get("category", "—")` returns None (not the default) when the LLM emitted "category": null, and `f"{None:<22s}"` raises TypeError.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced both: `judge --idea-file empty.txt` passes _read_idea (file path is truthy, content strips to ""), burns two LLM calls, stores idea_text="" → every subsequent `idea_cli list` raises IndexError. Similarly a stored verdict with category:null → TypeError: unsupported format string passed to NoneType.__format__. One bad row bricks the listing command for all ideas.
confirmed.suggestion: Guard: `head = (r["idea_text"].splitlines() or [""])[0][:60]`, `cat = verdict.get("category") or "—"`; also reject empty idea text in _read_idea.

confirmed.summary: Plan-stage references are stored into proposals.references verbatim with no hallucination filtering, unlike the judge stage (_filter_verdict_arxiv_ids) and the from-corpus path (classify_references) — an asymmetric trust boundary.
confirmed.evidence: Line 235 `references=plan.get("references") or []` passed straight to insert_proposal. PLAN_SYSTEM_PROMPT constraint 5 says 'Cite only arxiv_ids that appeared in candidate_literature. Do not fabricate references.' but nothing enforces it, whereas propose.propose_from_corpus runs classify_references and judge_idea runs _filter_verdict_arxiv_ids on the analogous fields.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: a plan with references=['arXiv:9999.11111'] scaffolds successfully and the fabricated id is stored in proposals.references_json with no warning — later consumers (dashboards, provenance review) treat it as a verified citation, exactly the failure mode task #26 built the citation validator to prevent.
confirmed.suggestion: Run classify_references (already exists in propose.py) over plan['references'] in make_plan or scaffold_param_sweep and drop/flag external_arxiv ids.

confirmed.summary: cmd_auto formats prediction_error / actual_novelty_distance with :.2f but both are Optional; a None value raises TypeError inside the per-proposal try block, so a successfully executed proposal is recorded as a FAILURE and the command exits 1.
confirmed.evidence:
try:
    r = execute_proposal(args.db, pid, seed=seed, verbose=False)
    results.append(r)
    err = r["prediction_error"]
    nov = r["actual_novelty_distance"]
    print(f"  ✓ ... pred_err={err:.2f} nov={nov:.2f} ...")
except Exception as exc:
    failures.append(...) — prediction_error is None whenever the LLM omitted predicted_fingerprint (which _validate_proposal explicitly allows: 'if "predicted_fingerprint" in p and ... is not None'), and actual_novelty_distance is None when the new run's fingerprint has a NaN or the DB has <=1 run.
confirmed.failure_scenario: REPRODUCED: a proposal without predicted_fingerprint passes validation, execute_proposal succeeds (DB shows status='executed', executed_run_id set), but the ✓-line print raises 'unsupported format string passed to NoneType.__format__'; cmd_auto reports 'executed: 1, failed: 1' for a single proposal (double-counted in both lists) and returns exit code 1 — a green run is reported red, and any CI invoking `auto` fails spuriously.
confirmed.suggestion: Format defensively: `err_s = f"{err:.2f}" if err is not None else "n/a"` (same for nov), and move the print outside the try or catch only around execute_proposal.

confirmed.summary: propose_next_k ranks by np.argsort(score)[::-1]; NaN acquisition scores (produced whenever an unlabeled run's fingerprint contains any NaN feature) sort to the END ascending and therefore come FIRST after reversal, so broken-fingerprint rows occupy the top-k labelling slots.
confirmed.evidence: score = ucb_acquisition(mu_pred, sigma_pred, novelty, ...)\n...\norder = np.argsort(score)[::-1] — model.predict() propagates NaN features into mu (Xa @ w_ with NaN input), novelty_score's np.nansum keeps novelty finite, so score is NaN exactly for NaN-fingerprint rows, and NaN sorts to the top after [::-1]. load_runs(labeled=False) applies no finiteness filter (db.collect_population exists but is not used here).
confirmed.failure_scenario: REPRODUCED: 4 labeled + 6 unlabeled rows where one unlabeled row has hill_tail_index=NaN → propose_next_k returned that row FIRST with mu=nan, acquisition=nan. In `label_cli next`, every session presents rows whose header reads '(NaN in features — fingerprint failed)' at the top; since skipping writes nothing, the same broken rows recapture the top-k slots forever, wasting the entire UCB labelling budget.
confirmed.suggestion: Filter unlabeled_rows to all-finite fingerprints before scoring (reuse db.collect_population), or replace NaN scores with -inf before argsort.

confirmed.summary: execute_proposal is not atomic and has no duplicate-run protection: insert_run commits before the prediction/novelty block, so any exception there (NaN IndexError, non-numeric predicted_fingerprint, Ctrl-C) leaves an orphan run row with the proposal still executable, and re-execution/duplicate proposals silently add repeated identical points to the atlas.
confirmed.evidence:
run_id = insert_run(db_path, ...)

# Compare predicted vs actual in standardised space.
all_runs = load_runs(db_path) ... update_proposal_status(db_path, proposal_id, status="executed", ...) — the run insert and the status update are separate sqlite transactions with fallible numpy code between them; there is no UNIQUE constraint or lookup on (model_name, params_json, seed), and cmd_execute's default `--seed 9000` (propose_cli.py:582) gives EVERY proposal the same seed despite the module docstring's promise 'run it with a fresh seed' (propose_cli.py:12).
confirmed.failure_scenario: REPRODUCED (see the NaN repro): first failed execute left runs=4 with proposal status 'proposed'; retry produced runs=5 — two identical orphan runs for one proposal. Independently, executing two proposals with near-identical params via `execute` uses seed 9000 for both, so the second run lands on top of the first (actual_novelty_distance ≈ 0) and duplicated points bias per-family centroids, sparse-region mining, and novelty calibration.
confirmed.suggestion: Wrap insert_run + measurement + update_proposal_status in one connection/transaction (or insert the run only after measurements succeed); check for an existing run with the same (model, params, seed) or an existing executed_run_id before inserting; derive the default seed from the proposal id as cmd_auto does.

confirmed.summary: _validate_proposal checks only key PRESENCE — no numeric-range check against MODEL_BOUNDS (despite the module docstring claiming 'Validates each proposal against MODEL_BOUNDS'), no numeric-type check on params or predicted_fingerprint values, and no list check on references — so malformed LLM output is stored and only explodes (or runs unbounded) at execute time.
confirmed.evidence: required_keys = set(MODEL_BOUNDS[p["target_model"]].keys())\nmissing_params = required_keys - set(p["params"].keys())\nif missing_params: return False, ... — values are never inspected. Verified: proposals with params {"N": 500000, "c": "high", "T": 250000}, predicted_fingerprint values as strings ("0.02"), and references given as a plain string all return (True, '') from _validate_proposal, even though the SYSTEM_PROMPT only licenses ±30% excursions outside [low, high].
confirmed.failure_scenario: (1) LLM proposes cont_bouchaud N=500000/T=250000 → accepted, and `auto` executes it unattended → a run 100x the intended budget stalls/OOMs the nightly job; a string param instead raises at build_model, which permanently marks the proposal 'rejected'. (2) String predicted_fingerprint values pass validation, then `np.array([...]) - mu_feat` in execute_proposal raises a TypeError AFTER insert_run → orphan run row (same corruption path as the NaN bug). (3) references-as-string is iterated character-by-character by classify_references and dump-md, producing one garbage 'reference' per character.
confirmed.suggestion: In _validate_proposal: coerce/verify every params value is numeric and within bounds*1.3 (matching the prompt contract), verify predicted_fingerprint values are finite floats, verify references is a list of strings, and verify predicted_novelty_distance is float-able before insert_proposal.

confirmed.summary: _format_proposal_full depends on the module-global _DB_PATH_FOR_SHOW pseudo-singleton; called without cmd_show having set it, classify_references('') raises sqlite3.OperationalError ('no such table') because sqlite3.connect('') opens a NEW anonymous temp DB per connection, defeating classify_references' own 'defensive' schema-creation.
confirmed.evidence: rv = classify_references(p["references"], _DB_PATH_FOR_SHOW.get("db", "")) — verified: classify_references(["arXiv:2208.13654"], "") raises OperationalError: no such table: literature_methods, because ensure_literature_schema('') creates the table in one temporary DB and load_literature's SELECT runs on a different one, contradicting the comment in propose.py:193-199 ('Create it (idempotent) so load_literature returns an empty list rather than raising OperationalError').
confirmed.failure_scenario: Any future caller (test, dashboard, dump-md enhancement) that formats a proposal with references without routing through cmd_show crashes with a misleading 'no such table' error instead of degrading to unverified-citation marks.
confirmed.suggestion: Pass db_path as an explicit parameter of _format_proposal_full (default None → skip classification), removing the singleton.

confirmed.summary: GCMG fingerprints are computed on a wrong series: the vectorized GCMG simulate() returns no 'actions' key, so series_for_fingerprint falls into the fallback that fabricates n_players with a mathematically wrong formula and ignores abstention, distorting all 12 production GCMG rows.
confirmed.evidence: adapters.py: `if "actions" in result and result["actions"].ndim == 2: ... else: n_players = int(2 * att.max() - att.min()) if att.size else 0 ... excess = 2.0 * att - n_players`. abm_models/gcmg/model.py simulate() returns `{"attendance", "active", "winner", "excess", "real_gain", "personal_final"}` — no "actions" — so the fallback always fires for GCMG. `2*att.max()-att.min()` is not N (for N=101 it yields 152; the correct heuristic is max+min), and in GCMG the true excess is `2*att - active(t)` (available directly as result["excess"]), not `2*att - const`, because agents abstain.
confirmed.failure_scenario: populate.py runs gcmg with production row 49 params (N=124, seed=1048): stored fp exactly reproduces the buggy series (vol 60.31). Against the true excess series the fingerprint differs materially: leverage flips sign (-0.035 vs +0.154), acf_absret_mean flips sign (+0.016 vs -0.026), acf_ret_l1 is 3x off (0.072 vs 0.023), agg_kurt_decay 0.47 vs 0.72. Every GCMG point in the atlas / inverse-ABM / novelty search sits at a wrong location; "which ABM is today's market closest to" answers involving GCMG are unreliable.
confirmed.suggestion: Use result["excess"] directly when present; for MG keep 2*att - actions.shape[1]. If a heuristic fallback must exist, use att.max()+att.min() and log a warning. Add a contract test asserting the adapter series equals the model-native excess.

confirmed.summary: _aggregational_kurt_decay divides by raw excess kurtosis, which is near zero for near-Gaussian/platykurtic series, producing unbounded feature values that violate the documented ~[-2,+1] range and turn single runs into spurious geometric outliers.
confirmed.evidence: `raw_k = _kurt_excess(r); if not np.isfinite(raw_k) or raw_k == 0: return float("nan") ... return float(1.0 - agg_k / raw_k)` — only exact zero is guarded. FEATURE_NAMES comment (line 31) claims `agg_kurt_decay ... ∈ ~[-2, +1]`. Production DB values: minority_game +21.355, zero_intelligence -12.743/-12.231, franke_westerhoff +10.869, garch11 -10.197; population std of this feature is 3.42, driven entirely by these blow-ups.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced production MG row 37 (N=87, M=8, seed=1036): raw excess kurt = -0.062, block kurt = +1.264, feature = 1-(1.264/-0.062) = 21.355 (matches DB exactly). After z-scoring, that run sits ~6 sigma out on this axis, so it dominates its own distances — novelty search would flag it as "most novel" purely from a 0/0-like ratio, and the inflated population std compresses the axis for every other run.
confirmed.suggestion: Guard |raw_k| below a threshold (return NaN or 0), or use a bounded transform such as (raw_k - agg_k) / (1 + |raw_k|), and clip to the documented range. Also note the docstring's interpretation assumes raw_k > 0; platykurtic attendance series invert its meaning.

confirmed.summary: compute_hill=False fabricates hill_tail_index = HILL_ALPHA_CAP (20.0) instead of a sentinel, so all MG/GCMG rows carry a fake 'maximally thin tail' measurement that systematically biases distances.
confirmed.evidence: `hill = hill_tail_index(r) if compute_hill else HILL_ALPHA_CAP` — populate.py passes compute_hill=(kind=="returns"), so every attendance_excess row stores fingerprint_json[2] = 20.0 exactly (verified in production DB: all 24 MG/GCMG rows have hill = 20.0, hill_raw = NULL). The value was never measured, yet it enters standardization and every L2 distance like a measurement.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Inverse-ABM query with a fat-tailed real market (hill ~3-4): MG and especially GCMG — the family whose hallmark is fat tails (excess kurtosis >> 0 per gcmg docstring) — are pushed away by a fixed multi-sigma penalty on an axis that carries no information for them, so they can effectively never be returned as nearest ABM regardless of their actual tail behaviour; conversely near-Gaussian capped price models (ZI) are pulled toward MG/GCMG on this axis. NaN would drop the rows entirely (collect_population requires all-finite), so the cap is currently the only way to keep them — a structural bias baked into the geometry.
confirmed.suggestion: Either compute Hill on the attendance-excess series (it is computable — the test suite already does), or store NaN plus a per-feature validity mask and use a masked distance normalized by shared feature count.

confirmed.summary: _load_returns_from_csv silently drops the first data point of BOM-prefixed CSVs and silently fingerprints the wrong column (e.g. Volume) for multi-column CSVs.
confirmed.evidence: `tok = s.split(",")[-1].strip(); try: vals.append(float(tok)) except ValueError: if first: first = False; continue`. strip() does not remove U+FEFF, so an Excel-exported UTF-8-BOM file's first numeric line raises ValueError and is consumed as a 'header'. And taking the last column means a Yahoo-style 'Date,Open,Close,Volume' file loads volumes.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: BOM file '﻿0.011\n-0.005\n0.003' loads [-0.005, 0.003] (first return silently lost); Yahoo-style CSV loads [5000000.0, 6000000.0] — the CLI then happily prints a nearest-ABM ranking computed from share volumes, with no error or sanity warning.
confirmed.suggestion: Open with encoding='utf-8-sig'; warn or error when lines have >2 columns; sanity-check magnitudes (|r| typically < 1 for log-returns).

confirmed.summary: validation_gate's failure path produces a gate JSON where "ok" is the string "False" (truthy on parse) and metrics are bare NaN tokens (invalid strict JSON).
confirmed.evidence: `"ok": np.isfinite(sil) and sil > 0 and inter > intra` — when sil is NaN, `np.isfinite(sil)` short-circuits and the expression evaluates to np.bool_(False), which json.dumps(..., default=str) serializes as the string "False"; intra/inter/sil NaNs serialize as bare NaN (json module non-strict output), which jq/JSON.parse reject.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: json.dumps({"ok": np.isfinite(nan) and ...}, default=str) -> '{"ok": "False"}'; bool(json.loads(...)["ok"]) is True. A degenerate population (e.g. single model family, >=4 rows) writes validation_gate.json whose 'ok' reads as truthy to any downstream consumer, and whose NaN fields break strict parsers in the atlas_refresh artifact chain.
confirmed.suggestion: Cast with bool(...) and replace non-finite metrics with None before returning; add allow_nan=False in main()'s json.dump to fail loudly.

confirmed.summary: distance_matrix's np.nansum silently treats NaN feature pairs as distance 0, so any NaN-containing fingerprint reads as artificially close to everything (latent — all current callers pre-filter NaNs).
confirmed.evidence: `return np.sqrt(np.nansum(diff ** 2, axis=-1))`, and standardize() uses nanmean/nanstd (signalling NaN tolerance) while nearest_abms_to_target line 138 repeats the same `np.nansum(diffs ** 2, ...)` pattern.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: rows [1, NaN, 3], [1, 99, 3], [1, 0, 3] give D[0,1]=D[0,2]=0.0 while D[1,2]=2.0 — the NaN row claims to be identical to two mutually distant rows. Currently unreachable because collect_population and the inverse_abm filters require all-finite vectors, but any future caller keeping partially-valid rows (the natural fix for the hill fabrication above) silently gets false proximity and wrong nearest-neighbour results.
confirmed.suggestion: Either assert np.isfinite(fps_std).all() at entry, or normalize by the count of shared finite features (masked distance).

confirmed.summary: A mistyped --db path creates a stray empty .db file as a side effect and then crashes with an uncaught sqlite3.OperationalError traceback (both subcommands; atlas.py main() too).
confirmed.evidence: `except (KeyError, RuntimeError, ValueError) as exc:` does not cover sqlite3.OperationalError; db.load_runs does `sqlite3.connect(db_path)` which creates the missing file before the SELECT fails with 'no such table: runs'.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: `... --db typo_abm_knowow.db nearest --target-run-id 1` -> uncaught OperationalError('no such table: runs') traceback, and a 0-byte typo_abm_knowow.db is left on disk (which can later shadow the real DB in scripts that check file existence).
confirmed.suggestion: In load_runs (or the CLIs) check os.path.exists(db_path) first, or connect with 'file:...?mode=ro' URI so missing files raise cleanly without creation.

confirmed.summary: int(round()) after LHS gives boundary integer values half the sampling weight of interior values, so documented bounds like M:(3,8) systematically undersample the endpoints.
confirmed.evidence: `v = float(lo + pts[i, j] * (hi - lo)); if name in _INT_PARAMS: v = int(round(v))` — the rounding bins [lo, lo+0.5) and (hi-0.5, hi] are half-width. Empirical (6000 samples, minority_game): M counts {3: 600, 8: 600, 4..7: 1200 each}; S:(2,4) gives {2: 1500, 3: 3000, 4: 1500}.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A researcher reading MODEL_BOUNDS assumes the atlas explores M=3..8 uniformly; in fact the regime corners (e.g. minority_game M=3, the most crowded-memory regime, or S=2 vs S=3 at half/double weight) are under/over-represented in every populate run, skewing per-family centroids and the coverage claims of the atlas.
confirmed.suggestion: Sample integers as int(lo + pts*(hi - lo + 1)) clipped to [lo, hi] (uniform strata over the integer grid), or document the endpoint halving.

confirmed.summary: Stale '6-vector / 6-D' documentation survives across the package even though FEATURE_NAMES has been 9 features since v4.
confirmed.evidence: __init__.py: 'one return series -> a 6-D vector of stylized facts (volatility, kurtosis, Hill tail index, return ACF, |return| ACF, leverage)'; db.py line 6: 'whose fingerprint_json is the 6-vector queried for atlas'; populate.py line 7: 'fingerprint(series) -> 6-vector'. fingerprint.FEATURE_NAMES has 9 entries and the DB stores 9-element vectors (verified: all 126 rows length 9).
confirmed.failure_scenario: A collaborator (or the LLM proposal prompts that quote these docstrings) builds against a 6-feature contract — e.g. hand-constructs a 6-element predicted_fingerprint for proposals — and hits shape mismatches or, worse, positional misinterpretation of the three v4 axes.
confirmed.suggestion: Replace hardcoded counts with len(FEATURE_NAMES) references or update the three docstrings.

confirmed.summary: The daily annotation workflow rebuilds its DB from seed on every run, so queue-next always returns the same first method (chiarella_iori) and the queue never advances.
confirmed.evidence: 'uv run python -m fingerprint_atlas.methods_cli --db "$DB_PATH" seed' with DB_PATH=data/abm_knowhow.db, followed by 'queue-next --n "$N_DRAFTS"'. .gitignore contains 'data/*.db' and no data/ directory exists in the repo, so the CI DB is freshly seeded (all commentary empty) every run. Human imports go into '../test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db' (PR body, line 162) — a local DB CI never sees. Nothing feeds merged methods_drafts/**.md back into the CI DB before queue-next.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Daily cron at 08:00 UTC: run 1 drafts chiarella_iori, PR merged, human imports locally. Run 2 seeds a fresh DB where all 11 methods are unannotated -> queue-next again prints chiarella_iori -> a new PR for the same method is opened every single day forever; the other 10 methods are never drafted. Reproduced by simulating two fresh-DB runs: both returned 'chiarella_iori'. Also, data/literature_methods.json never exists in the repo, so the 'literature snapshot' context step (line 56) is always skipped and every draft runs with n_relevant_literature=0.
confirmed.suggestion: Persist annotation state into the repo: either commit a snapshot the CI restores, or make queue-next skip methods that already have a file under methods_drafts/**/<name>.md, or import merged draft files into the CI DB before running queue-next.

confirmed.summary: cmd_import_md silently wipes all five commentary columns when given a file with no recognizable '## section' headers (wrong file, renamed draft, malformed edit), returning rc 0 and printing 'imported'.
confirmed.evidence:
vals = _parse_edit_file(text)
    update_method(db_path, name, **vals) — _parse_edit_file initializes out = {sec: "" for sec in SECTIONS} and only fills sections whose exact '## <name>' header matches; a file with zero matching headers yields five empty strings, which update_method writes unconditionally. There is no check that any section was found, nor that the file's '# methodology notes for: <name>' header matches the `name` argument.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: seeded a temp DB, hand-annotated minority_game (novelty_notes='hand-written precious notes', tags='keep:me'), then ran cmd_import_md(db, 'minority_game', <a plain README.md>). rc=0, output 'imported ... -> method minority_game', and all commentary columns including tags were cleared to ''. Same happens if a human reviewer typos a header in the PR (e.g. '## Mechanism Strengths' — space/capitals don't match the regex): that column is silently cleared and its text is misattributed to the previous section.
confirmed.suggestion: Abort (or require --force) when zero recognized sections are parsed; warn per-section when a previously non-empty column would be cleared; verify the file header name matches the `name` argument.

confirmed.summary: LLM draft text is injected unescaped into the edit-file format where any line matching '^## <ascii_word>$' is structural, so drafts containing markdown '## ' headers get truncated, misattributed, or destroy other sections in the rendered file.
confirmed.evidence: rendered = rendered[:body_start] + (draft[sec] or "") + "\n" + rendered[body_end:] (cmd_draft_to_file; identical code in cmd_draft at line 280). marker lookup uses rendered.find(marker) from position 0, and _parse_edit_file ends a body at the next '^##\s+[A-Za-z_]+$' line with last-match-wins (out[sec_name] is overwritten by later duplicates).
confirmed.failure_scenario:
Reproduced two ways with a stubbed draft: (1) novelty_notes containing 'point one
## summary
this is still novelty text' -> after draft-to-file + import-md, DB novelty_notes == 'point one'; everything after the '## summary' line silently dropped. (2) novelty_notes containing a literal '
## tags
' line -> the injection loop found that string before the real '## tags' template header and spliced there; the rendered file lost the mechanism_strengths, mechanism_weaknesses, and research_questions sections entirely, and import-md then cleared those three DB columns. gpt-4o-mini/gpt-oss producing '## ' markdown headings inside free-form Japanese note fields is a realistic daily-cron event; the corruption is silent.
confirmed.suggestion: Escape lines matching the section-header regex when rendering bodies (e.g. prefix with '\\' or an extra '#') and unescape on parse; in the injection loop, locate markers via the parsed section spans of the template rather than substring find over the mutated string.

confirmed.summary: _find_authors_repo regex-searches a hard-sliced 120-char window, so a repo URL straddling the window boundary is returned truncated mid-repo-name instead of in full.
confirmed.evidence:
window = text[hint.end():hint.end() + _PROXIMITY_WINDOW]
        m = _REPO_HOST_RE.search(window) — the slice can cut through the repo segment; the regex then matches the surviving prefix, _clean_repo_match accepts it, and the truncated URL is returned without ever consulting the full text.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: 'Source code is available <65 chars of filler> at github.com/quant-lab/agent-based-market-simulator together with configs.' -> extract_github_from_text returned 'https://github.com/quant-lab/agent-based-market' (window ended mid-name). The wrong URL is persisted as code_url with source='abstract'; fetch_repo_snapshot then 404s (status 'error' cached permanently) or, worse, fetches a different real repo whose README is fed into the idea-plan LLM context.
confirmed.suggestion: Search the full text from hint.end() and accept the match only if m.start() < _PROXIMITY_WINDOW, so the URL itself is never clipped.

confirmed.summary: _REPO_HOST_RE has no left boundary before the host, so it matches inside longer hostnames and subdomain paths, fabricating wrong repo URLs.
confirmed.evidence: r"(?:https?://)?(github\.com|gitlab\.com|bitbucket\.org)/" — nothing prevents a preceding 'gist.', 'api.', or 'my'.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: 'gist.github.com/johndoe/9f2c1' -> 'https://github.com/johndoe/9f2c1' (nonexistent repo); 'api.github.com/repos/torvalds/linux' -> 'https://github.com/repos/torvalds' (org='repos' — a live wrong account); 'mygithub.com/acme/tool' -> 'https://github.com/acme/tool'. Any abstract/PDF mentioning a gist or an API path yields a false code_url stored in literature_methods.
confirmed.suggestion: Add a boundary such as (?<![\w.-]) before the host group.

confirmed.summary: The 'open[- ]source' entry in _AUTHORS_REPO_HINTS fires on the generic adjective ('open-source model X'), promoting cited base-model/tool repos to 'authors-own-code' — the exact misattribution the proximity heuristic was built to prevent.
confirmed.evidence: r"open[- ]source(?:d)?(?:\s+at)?" — unlike the other hints ('our code', 'we release'), this matches attributive uses with no possessive context, and the '\s+at' is optional.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: 'We fine-tune the open-source model LLaMA (github.com/meta-llama/llama3) to build trading agents...' -> extract_github_from_text returns 'https://github.com/meta-llama/llama3' via the high-trust proximity path, even if the authors' real repo appears later in the abstract. For the LLM-agent corpus this package targets, 'open-source model/LLM' phrasing is extremely common, so base-model repos get stored as the paper's code_url and their READMEs get injected into idea-plan prompts as the paper's implementation.
confirmed.suggestion: Require the 'at' ('open[- ]sourced?\s+at') or a possessive ('our open-source'), or drop the hint entirely.

confirmed.summary: Collapsing '/\n' during URL de-wrapping welds the next line's first word onto an org-only URL, inventing a repo; the in-code comment claiming false positives 'just produce a wrong URL that 404s instead of a fake link that points at someone else' is wrong.
confirmed.evidence: text = re.sub(r"/\n", "/", text) — applied to the whole text, not just inside URL-like substrings, despite the docstring saying 'Collapsing newlines ... inside URL-like substrings'. Comment at lines 54-57 asserts FPs only 404.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: 'Our code is available at github.com/quantlab/\nMarkets exhibit fat tails in our experiments.' -> returns 'https://github.com/quantlab/Markets'. Any abstract/PDF line ending with an org-level or trailing-slash URL produces org/<FirstWordOfNextLine>, which can be a live third-party repo (e.g. common words like 'We', 'The', 'Data' exist as repo names); the proximity hint then blesses it as the authors' repo.
confirmed.suggestion: Only collapse '/\n' when the preceding context matches a host/org pattern, or validate the resulting URL (HEAD request) before persisting.

confirmed.summary: The Papers with Code API is dead (paperswithcode.com now 302-redirects to huggingface.co/papers/trending), so fetch_pwc_repo silently returns None forever and the documented 'PWC fallback' stage of resolve_code_url can never fire.
confirmed.evidence: _PWC_PAPERS_URL = "https://paperswithcode.com/api/v1/papers/" and docstring 'Papers with Code public API. Free, no auth.' Verified live: GET https://paperswithcode.com/api/v1/papers/?arxiv_id=1906.01034 -> 302 -> https://huggingface.co/papers/trending. urllib follows the redirect, gets HTML, json.loads raises JSONDecodeError, which _http_get_json swallows into None.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Every backfill-code / ingest run performs one useless HTTPS round trip per paper (plus sleep) for the PWC stage and reports 'pwc=0' with no signal that the source is permanently defunct; module docs, cmd_backfill_code help ('useful when ... PWC indexes a new paper'), and the source='pwc' plumbing are all stale.
confirmed.suggestion: Remove or replace the fallback (OpenAlex primary_location / Semantic Scholar externalIds / HF papers API) and treat an HTML/redirect response as a loggable 'source dead' condition rather than None.

confirmed.summary: All scratch-file/output I/O omits encoding='utf-8', so on Windows (a platform the code explicitly supports via the notepad fallback) cmd_edit/draft/draft-to-file crash with UnicodeEncodeError because SEED mechanisms and the mandated-Japanese drafts are not cp1252-encodable.
confirmed.evidence: with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile("w", suffix=f"_{m.name}.md", delete=False) as fh: ... fh.write(initial) — locale-default encoding; likewise open(path) reads at 209/291, open(out, "w") at 344, open(path) at 362. _resolve_editor contains 'if platform.system() == "Windows": return ["notepad"]', so Windows is in-scope.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Windows user (locale cp1252) runs 'methods_cli edit speculation_game': _render_edit_file emits 'history μ' / 'Erdős-Rényi' / 'β·(ED + μ)'; fh.write raises UnicodeEncodeError before the editor ever opens. Verified: 8 of 11 SEED mechanism strings fail .encode('cp1252'). The Japanese LLM drafts ('Write in Japanese' is a HARD prompt constraint) fail the same way in draft/draft-to-file.
confirmed.suggestion: Pass encoding='utf-8' to every NamedTemporaryFile/open in methods_cli.

confirmed.summary: _has_commentary counts 'tags' as commentary while cmd_draft_all does not, so tagging an unannotated method silently removes it from the queue-next annotation queue even though it still has no notes.
confirmed.evidence: _has_commentary: 'return any(getattr(m, sec).strip() for sec in SECTIONS)' where SECTIONS includes "tags"; cmd_draft_all checks only the four note fields ('has_notes = any(getattr(m, sec) for sec in ("novelty_notes", "mechanism_strengths", "mechanism_weaknesses", "research_questions"))').
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: after 'methods tag chiarella_iori --add todo' on a freshly seeded DB, queue-next no longer lists chiarella_iori (returns cont_bouchaud, ...) although all four note columns are empty — the daily annotation workflow will never draft it, while draft-all would. Using tags as a triage mechanism (their obvious use) permanently hides methods from the queue.
confirmed.suggestion: Exclude 'tags' from the queue-next emptiness check (share the four-field predicate with cmd_draft_all).

confirmed.summary: The `techniques` table that knowhow_techniques.py documents as living in this exact DB ('test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db') does not exist there, and load_techniques() swallows the OperationalError and returns [], so the idea_plan 'candidate_techniques' prompt injection is a permanent silent no-op in production.
confirmed.evidence: Docstring lines 3-4: 'The 15-technique knowhow DB lives at test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db (built by test/knowhow/seed_knowhow.py)'; lines 25-26: `except sqlite3.OperationalError: return []`. Production DB table list = {literature_methods, methods, runs} — no `techniques`. idea_plan.py:101 tells the LLM 'candidate_techniques : 15-row knowhow table', and its output contract (lines 78-87) requires the model to pick from it or justify an empty list.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Every idea_plan run against the production DB injects zero techniques while the prompt claims a 15-row table exists; the LLM either hallucinates technique names or always emits techniques_skip_reason, and the calibration/validation know-how layer (task #41 'techniques injection') never actually reaches any generated plan — with no log line to reveal it.
confirmed.suggestion: Log a warning when the table is missing (row count 0 is fine, missing table is not) and run seed_knowhow.py against the production DB or drop the injection claim from the prompt.

confirmed.summary: lux_marchesi family card ships arxiv_id 'cond-mat/9810262', which is a materials-science paper ("Dynamic Fracture in Single Crystal Silicon"), not Lux-Marchesi 1999 — a known-bad id that was fixed in subfields.py but not here.
confirmed.evidence: "arxiv_id": "cond-mat/9810262", on the Lux–Marchesi card. Fetching https://arxiv.org/abs/cond-mat/9810262 returns title "Dynamic Fracture in Single Crystal Silicon". subfields.py lines 48-53 already document the correction: "Lux-Marchesi 1999 ... is journal-only — no arxiv preprint. Anchor via the OpenAlex W-id" (W1537415400, which I verified via OpenAlex resolves to the real Nature paper, DOI 10.1038/17290).
confirmed.failure_scenario: dashboard.py _abm_family_card renders this id as a link https://arxiv.org/abs/cond-mat/9810262 labelled Lux–Marchesi; a user clicking it lands on a silicon-fracture paper, and anyone running `ingest-ids cond-mat/9810262` to "plug the Lux-Marchesi gap" pollutes literature_methods with an off-topic paper tagged as canon.
confirmed.suggestion: Set arxiv_id to None (like franke_westerhoff/chiarella_iori) or use the OpenAlex id 'oa:W1537415400'; add a cross-file test asserting no catalog reuses ids that subfields.py documents as journal-only.

confirmed.summary: chartist_fundamentalist_mix cites the same wrong id 'cond-mat/9810262' (silicon-fracture paper) as its defining Lux-Marchesi reference.
confirmed.evidence: "ref_papers": ["cond-mat/9810262"], under key 'chartist_fundamentalist_mix' ("Chartist–fundamentalist mix (Lux-Marchesi)"). Verified via arxiv.org: that id is "Dynamic Fracture in Single Crystal Silicon". techniques.py's own header says ref_papers are ids that "define or canonically use this technique" and are "matched against literature_methods.arxiv_id when surfaced in dashboard".
confirmed.failure_scenario: gap_finder view C joins ref_papers against the literature DB (gap_finder.py:252-262); this ref can only ever match if the fracture paper is ingested, so the technique's overlap row is structurally 0 and the cell is surfaced as a spurious 'no one has applied chartist-fundamentalist mixing in subfield Y' research gap, which gap_propose then turns into LLM proposals.
confirmed.suggestion: Replace with the real Lux-Marchesi anchor ('oa:W1537415400') or with an actual arxiv-hosted chartist-fundamentalist paper.

confirmed.summary: order_arrival_poisson's sole reference '1502.03003' is "Attraction and Repulsion in Conformal Gravity" — a gravity paper, not a Poisson/Hawkes order-flow paper.
confirmed.evidence: "ref_papers": ["1502.03003"], on 'Poisson order arrival'. https://arxiv.org/abs/1502.03003 returns title "Attraction and Repulsion in Conformal Gravity". The intended reference is almost certainly Bacry–Mastromatteo–Muzy "Hawkes processes in finance" (arXiv:1502.04592) — same year/month, transposed digits.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Dashboard technique card displays a physics id as the canonical order-arrival citation; gap_finder view C can never register literature overlap for this technique even after the Hawkes literature is ingested, generating a permanent false 'unexplored' signal for order-arrival × every subfield.
confirmed.suggestion: Change to 1502.04592 and add a title-vs-id check on the catalog.

confirmed.summary: abc_rejection's sole reference '1903.04279' is a ternary-Boltzmann-equation math-physics paper, not an ABC-for-ABMs paper.
confirmed.evidence: "ref_papers": ["1903.04279"], on 'Approximate Bayesian Computation (ABC) for ABMs'. https://arxiv.org/abs/1903.04279 returns "Rigorous derivation of a ternary Boltzmann equation for a classical system of particles".
confirmed.failure_scenario: Same as the other hallucinated ids: wrong citation rendered in the dashboard catalog, and the technique permanently shows zero literature overlap in gap-mine view C, producing spurious gap proposals for ABC calibration.
confirmed.suggestion: Replace with a real ABC/ABM-calibration reference (e.g. Platt 2020's calibration survey or Grazzini-Richiardi-Tsionas), verified by title lookup.

confirmed.summary: DOI-format ref_papers (hill_estimator, pickands_estimator, stationary_bootstrap, event_driven_lob) can never match anything in gap_finder view C, because the join keys are arxiv ids and OpenAlex W-ids only and literature_methods has no doi column.
confirmed.evidence: techniques.py documents and _looks_like_ref() blesses DOI refs ("ref_papers": ["10.1214/aos/1176343247"]), but gap_finder.py:218-233 builds by_key only from arxiv_id base forms and the W-id inside oa_paper_id, and db.py's literature_methods schema (line 218-241 plus migrations at 272-276) stores no DOI. A pre-arxiv classic like Hill 1975 can only ever enter the DB as an OA ingest keyed 'oa:W…', which the DOI string can't join to.
confirmed.failure_scenario: After the user ingests the Hill/Pickands/Politis-Romano/PAMS canon via canon_ingest, view C still reports 0 overlap for those four techniques across all 25 subfields; with 19/30 techniques having empty ref_papers, view C's 'zero overlap = nobody crossed X×Y' salience mostly measures catalog sparsity, and gap_propose spends LLM budget proposing 'gaps' that are join artefacts.
confirmed.suggestion: Either store 'oa:W…' ids in ref_papers for journal-only references, or add a doi column to literature_methods and include normalised DOIs in by_key.

confirmed.summary: format_for_prompt's domain filter treats a missing 'domain' as 'general' in one clause but not the other, so an entry added without a domain silently disappears from every domain-scoped LLM prompt while appearing in unscoped ones.
confirmed.evidence: items = [e for e in GLOSSARY if (domain is None or e.get("domain", "general") == domain or e.get("domain") == "general")] — the third clause omits the "general" default used by the second clause and by all_domains()/cmd_glossary. Reproduced: appending {"en": "zzz-test-term", "ja_primary": "ZZZ", "notes": "x"} → included in format_for_prompt() but excluded from format_for_prompt(domain="financial-abm").
confirmed.failure_scenario: The module docstring invites extension 'by editing this file'; a hand-added entry without domain never reaches gap_propose/idea prompts (which pass glossary_domain='financial-abm'), so the LLM keeps generating the very rendering the user added the entry to prevent — with no error anywhere.
confirmed.suggestion: Use e.get("domain", "general") in the third clause too (or normalise domain at catalog-load time).

confirmed.summary: The speculation_game card's fact count is self-contradictory ('reproduces 5 of 6 main stylized facts (everything except leverage effect + gain/loss asymmetry)' — six minus two is four) and the JSSC citation year is wrong (2020; actually published online 2021, issue 35(1) 2022).
confirmed.evidence: Lines 193-195: "reproduces 5 of 6 main stylized facts (everything except leverage effect + gain/loss asymmetry)" — two exclusions from six cannot leave five; i18n.py:43 mirrors "5/6スタイル化事実". Lines 186-187: "Katahira & Chen (2020), J. Syst. Sci. Complex. 35(1), 221-244" — Crossref returns "Heterogeneous Round-Trip Trading and the Emergence of Volatility Clustering in Speculation Game", JSSC 35(1) 221-244, issued 2021-06-28.
confirmed.failure_scenario: This is the lab's own flagship model card; a thesis committee member checking the citation finds the year wrong, and downstream text generated from the card propagates an arithmetic that doesn't survive a first read ('5 of 6 ... except two').
confirmed.suggestion: Decide whether gain/loss asymmetry is inside or outside the 'main 6' and fix the count consistently here and in i18n.py; correct the JSSC year to 2022 (online 2021).

confirmed.summary: The module docstring advertises extension 'via the `glossary add` CLI', but the glossary CLI implements only list/lookup/search/prompt — there is no add subcommand.
confirmed.evidence: glossary.py:18-19: "Extend by editing this file or via the `glossary add` CLI". arxiv_cli.cmd_glossary (line 1132+) handles subs 'list', 'lookup', 'search', 'prompt' and returns "unknown subcommand" for anything else.
confirmed.failure_scenario: User runs `... glossary add "drawdown" ...` per the docstring and gets 'unknown subcommand' (exit 2); the documented low-friction path for keeping the personal dictionary current doesn't exist, so new rejected translations accumulate unrecorded.
confirmed.suggestion: Implement `glossary add` (append to the file or a user overlay), or fix the docstring.

confirmed.summary: CI lint step is neutered with '|| true', and it is actively masking 76 ruff errors including an F821 undefined-name that is a real runtime crash bug.
confirmed.evidence: ci.yml line 22: `run: uv run ruff check packages experiments src tests || true`. Running the exact same command in the repo venv yields: "Found 76 errors" — 39 F401, 15 E702, 6 F841, and critically 1 F821: "Undefined name `os` --> packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/arxiv_cli.py:1349". There is no [tool.ruff] config anywhere, so CI's run is identical and has been silently red.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Any lint-detectable bug (like the F821 crash below) merges green. The F821 in question crashes the weekly atlas_refresh workflow's canon-atlas step during OpenAlex outages — ruff would have blocked it at PR time if the step could fail.
confirmed.suggestion: Drop '|| true'; if style noise is the concern, hard-fail on `--select F,E9` (correctness classes) and keep the rest advisory. 52 of the 76 are auto-fixable with `ruff check --fix`.

confirmed.summary: The 'end-to-end execute' test never calls the real executor — it hand-copies a simplified version and hardcodes prediction_error=1.0, so the pipeline's central research metric (prediction_error / actual_novelty computation in propose_cli.execute_proposal) has zero test coverage and its IndexError bug went undetected.
confirmed.evidence: test_end_to_end_execute_links_run_and_records_error (lines 303-335) comments "Simulate the CLI execute path inline" then re-implements build_model/run/fingerprint/insert_run and calls `update_proposal_status(db, pid, "executed", ..., prediction_error=1.0)` — the standardized-space L2 computation, novelty distance, NaN handling, and rejected-on-failure transition in execute_proposal (propose_cli.py:246-336) are all untested. The mirror can drift arbitrarily from the real executor and the test stays green (it did: see the reproduced IndexError finding).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Any regression in execute_proposal (indexing, standardization, np.nansum silently zeroing NaN dims, status transitions) ships silently; the analytics learning curves (prediction_error over time / by family) that drive the research feedback loop are computed by code no test exercises.
confirmed.suggestion: Replace the inline mirror with a call to propose_cli.execute_proposal on the temp DB; assert prediction_error against a hand-computed value and add a case with a pre-existing NaN-fingerprint run.

confirmed.summary: Nine of the 42 fingerprint_atlas modules have ZERO test coverage, including the analytics module that produces the project's headline learning-curve figures and the whole propose_cli executor.
confirmed.evidence: Grep of tests/ for module references: 0 test files touch analytics.py (168 lines: summarize, plot_prediction_error_over_time/by_family, plot_novelty_calibration), propose_cli.py (627), label_cli.py (360), populate.py (142), populate_refs.py (161), real_refs.py (150, live Yahoo fetch), inverse_abm_cli.py (130), knowhow_techniques.py (38), techniques.py (442 — only indirectly rendered via dashboard's _technique_catalog_html). Partially covered: arxiv_cli.py (2366 lines) has tests for only ~5 of its 25+ subcommands (strip-arxiv-versions, extract-untagged, stylized-fact-other, glossary, gap-mine); atlas.py only validation_gate (plot_atlas/plot_feature_box/main untested); openalex.find_canon_papers_by_seed (openalex.py:472) is only ever monkeypatched, never executed.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Concrete instance already found: the untested cmd_canon_atlas failure path crashes with NameError, and the untested execute_proposal crashes with IndexError. Same class of rot can exist undetected in label_cli's UCB labelling loop, populate_refs' real-data ingestion, and every analytics plot the dashboards embed.
confirmed.suggestion: Priority order for new tests: execute_proposal + analytics.summarize round-trip; a smoke sweep instantiating every cmd_* with a temp DB in dry-run mode; label_cli.propose-next logic (its math lives in preference.py which IS tested, but the CLI glue is not).

confirmed.summary: The find_gaps 'end-to-end' test asserts only `len(top) <= 5` while its own docstring promises a NON-EMPTY top list — a regression making rank_top_gaps return [] would pass.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 214-215 docstring: "find_gaps returns sensible views and a non-empty top list." Lines 232-233: `# Top list bounded by top_n, may be empty if no gaps satisfy thresholds` / `assert len(top) <= 5`. Empirically re-ran the exact fixture: len(top) == 5 and all five gaps come from view B (minority_game x fat-tails/vol-clustering/leverage/long-memory/aggregational-gaussianity) — so `assert len(top) == 5` plus a content check was available and stable.
confirmed.failure_scenario: rank_top_gaps regresses (e.g. a filter inversion drops every cell) -> find_gaps returns ([views], []) -> dashboard gap table and gap->proposal pipeline silently produce nothing, and this test still passes.
confirmed.suggestion: Assert len(top) == 5 (or >= 1) and that view 'B' rows for minority_game appear.

confirmed.summary: Tautological assertion: `n_relevant_literature >= 0` can never fail (it is a len()), while the comment above claims it verifies the seeded LLM-agent paper reached the context.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 106-107: `# context provided to LLM should include our LLM-agent paper` / `assert result["context_used"]["n_relevant_literature"] >= 0`. Empirically re-ran the fixture through draft_notes_for_method: n_relevant_literature == 1 and literature_cited_in_context == ['2604.18602v2'] — the intended strong assertion holds today but is not being made.
confirmed.failure_scenario: _select_relevant_literature regresses to always return [] (e.g. the relevance>=0.4 gate or tag-overlap scoring breaks) -> the LLM drafts notes with zero literature grounding -> hallucinated citations in methods notes; test stays green.
confirmed.suggestion: Assert n_relevant_literature == 1 and '2604.18602v2' in literature_cited_in_context.

confirmed.summary: EXTRACTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT is imported by the test module but never asserted against, leaving the stylized-fact vocabulary duplicated in two unguarded mirrors (taxonomy.CANONICAL_FACTS and the prompt literal) that can silently drift.
confirmed.evidence: test_arxiv_ingest.py:12-14 imports `EXTRACTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT, _coerce_relevance, extract_paper_structured` but EXTRACTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT appears nowhere else in the file (ruff F401 confirms). The prompt hardcodes the 10 fact slugs (arxiv_ingest.py:229-243) and taxonomy.CANONICAL_FACTS holds the same 10; verified in-sync today, but build_coverage silently DROPS any extracted fact not in the canonical list (pinned by test_build_coverage_counts_correctly line 52), so a rename/addition in taxonomy without a prompt edit means new extractions are silently discarded from every coverage matrix.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Someone adds 'volatility-persistence' to taxonomy.CANONICAL_FACTS but not to the prompt (or vice versa): the LLM keeps emitting the old vocabulary, coverage/gap views mis-count with no error anywhere, and no test fails.
confirmed.suggestion: Write the test the unused import implies: assert every CANONICAL_FACTS slug appears quoted in EXTRACTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT (and that no quoted fact in the prompt is missing from taxonomy).

confirmed.summary: Bot-created cron PRs receive zero CI: peter-evans/create-pull-request runs with the default GITHUB_TOKEN, whose PRs do not trigger `pull_request` workflows, so the literature-snapshot and proposal data that feed every downstream LLM context merge unvalidated (and lint in ci.yml is `|| true`, i.e. non-blocking, for human PRs too).
confirmed.evidence: PR #3 and PR #4 check-runs API both return `"total_count":0` while PR #7 (human-pushed) has a green 'test' run. ci.yml lint step: `uv run ruff check packages experiments src tests || true`.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A cron run writes a malformed data/literature_methods.json (e.g. an LLM extraction field that breaks the restore step's r["mechanism_summary"] access) and the PR merges green-looking with no checks; the next scheduled run then crashes at 'Materialise DB from snapshot' and the weekly pipeline silently stops producing PRs.
confirmed.suggestion: Give create-pull-request a PAT/App token so CI runs on cron PRs, or add a JSON schema-validation step before opening the PR.

confirmed.summary: tests/test_config.py and tests/test_rng.py are 0-byte placeholder files that collect zero tests, giving a false impression that config and RNG behavior are covered.
confirmed.evidence: `stat`: both files are 0 bytes; pytest collects 0 items from them (they do not appear in the 244-test collection listing). They predate this branch but remain in the suite it ships.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A reviewer scanning tests/ assumes config/RNG determinism (seed reproducibility of the populate/LHS pipeline) is tested; it is not — reproducibility regressions in sample_params_lhs seeding would be invisible.
confirmed.suggestion: Either delete them or fill them with the obvious contracts (same seed -> identical LHS params / identical populate fingerprints).

confirmed.summary: The bare `canon_atlas.html` gitignore pattern matches the generated notebooks/atlas/<run_id>/canon_atlas.html, so the atlas-refresh PR silently omits the canon atlas artifact its own body advertises.
confirmed.evidence: `git check-ignore -v notebooks/atlas/12345/canon_atlas.html` → `.gitignore:3:canon_atlas.html`. atlas_refresh.yml:107 writes `--out "$ATLAS_OUT_DIR/canon_atlas.html"` and the PR add-paths (line 118-119) is `notebooks/atlas/**`; peter-evans/create-pull-request stages via git add, which skips ignored files. The PR body (lines 139-143) says '**D. Canon atlas** — canon_atlas.html ... Open the HTML in a browser.'
confirmed.failure_scenario: Every Friday run: step D spends ~25 OpenAlex sweeps building canon_atlas.html, but the opened PR contains only A/B/C artifacts; the reviewer following the PR instructions finds no canon_atlas.html to open, and the work is discarded when the runner dies.
confirmed.suggestion: Scope the ignore pattern to the root file (`/canon_atlas.html`) or rename the workflow output.

confirmed.summary: propose.yml and atlas_refresh.yml depend on a live Yahoo Finance fetch from the CI runner (the cache dir is gitignored and thus always empty in CI), and populate_refs exits 1 on any fetch error, killing the whole weekly run before any LLM/PR step.
confirmed.evidence: `uv run python -m fingerprint_atlas.populate_refs --db "$DB_PATH" ... --cache-dir notebooks/yahoo_cache` — but `.gitignore:1` is `notebooks/yahoo_cache/` (confirmed untracked via git ls-files), so fetch_yahoo_closes (real_refs.py:36-61) always hits query.finance.yahoo.com from the runner. populate_refs.py:157: `return 0 if all(v.get("n_errors", 0) == 0 for v in out.values()) else 1`, and populate_real returns n_errors=1 on fetch failure (lines 95-100).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Yahoo rate-limits/blocks the GitHub-hosted runner IP (common for datacenter IPs) → populate_refs exits 1 → the 'Rebuild DB' step fails → propose.yml (Monday) and atlas_refresh.yml (Friday) produce no PR at all, week after week, with no retry or continue-on-error.
confirmed.suggestion: Commit the small yahoo cache JSONs (or vendor a fixture), or add `--skip-real` in CI, or make the step continue-on-error.

confirmed.summary: atlas_refresh.yml cannot succeed on the branch as committed: steps B and C call `arxiv_cli atlas`/`coverage`, which exit 1 when literature_methods is empty, and no data/literature_methods.json exists anywhere in the repo to restore.
confirmed.evidence: Step B runs `uv run python -m fingerprint_atlas.arxiv_cli --db "$DB_PATH" atlas --out-dir "$ATLAS_OUT_DIR"`; arxiv_cli.cmd_atlas (arxiv_cli.py:455-457) does `if not rows: print("no papers in literature_methods. Ingest first.", file=sys.stderr); return 1` (cmd_coverage:429-431 identical). `git ls-files data` is empty and the data/ dir does not exist on the branch, so the conditional snapshot restore in the 'Rebuild DB' step is always skipped. Empirically reproduced: `python -m fingerprint_atlas.arxiv_cli --db <fresh>.db atlas` → exit=1; `... coverage` → exit=1.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Friday 23:00 UTC cron fires on main (or workflow_dispatch): checkout has no data/literature_methods.json, DB rebuild seeds methods+runs only, step B exits 1, the job fails, and no atlas PR is ever opened — every week — until a separately-scheduled ingest_arxiv PR (which itself needs LLM API secrets) is merged first. Nothing guards the ordering or degrades gracefully (steps B–D have no `if: snapshot exists`).
confirmed.suggestion: Guard steps B/C/D with a snapshot-exists condition or make cmd_atlas/cmd_coverage exit 0 with a notice under an explicit --allow-empty flag used by the workflow.

confirmed.summary: Squash-merging PR #7 would break both open cron PRs: with a squash, the feature lineage embedded in their head branches is absent from main's ancestry, so PR #3 produces 6 merge conflicts and PR #4 produces 20, whereas a true merge commit lets both merge cleanly (verified by simulation) — so PR #7 must be merged with a merge commit, not squash/rebase.
confirmed.evidence: Scratch-clone simulation: (a) merge-commit path: main + PR#7 clean, then PR#3 head merges clean (adds only data/literature_methods.json) and PR#4 head merges clean (adds only proposals/28374237859/*.md). (b) squash path: `git merge --squash` PR#7, then merging PR#3 head -> 'CONFLICT (add/add) ... idea_cli.py / idea_judge.py / idea_plan.py / tests/test_idea.py; CONFLICT (content) ... ingest_arxiv.yml / arxiv_ingest.py' (6 files); PR#4 head -> 20 conflicts.
confirmed.failure_scenario: User clicks 'Squash and merge' on PR #7 (GitHub's default on many repos) and deletes the branch; PRs #3/#4 auto-retarget to main but now show 34-57 phantom commits and dozens of conflicts on files whose content is actually identical, and a rushed conflict resolution taking 'theirs' would silently revert 33 commits of arxiv_ingest/idea-pipeline fixes (e.g. the 'billing' false-positive fix 96e0a38, JA-tag leak fix 0f74588).
confirmed.suggestion: Use 'Create a merge commit' for PR #7. The 89 messages are high-quality provenance (only blemish: c0a648c 'a'); preserving them also keeps bisectability over 15.6k added lines.

confirmed.summary: Commit 14e24c3 ('gitignore dashboard build outputs') appended 'dashboard/*.html' onto the newline-less final '*.csv' line, producing the dead pattern '*.csvdashboard/*.html' — so neither *.csv nor dashboard/*.html is actually ignored, and merging PR #7 exports this corruption to main, silently destroying main's existing *.csv ignore rule.
confirmed.evidence: HEAD .gitignore last line: `*.csvdashboard/*.html`. Commit 14e24c3 diff: `-*.csv\ No newline at end of file` / `+*.csvdashboard/*.html`. Verified: `git check-ignore -v foo.csv dashboard/index.html` matches NOTHING (only dashboard/assets/ and canon_atlas.html match). The same commit had to `git rm` dashboard/index.html/markets.html/research.html because the ignore rule never took effect.
confirmed.failure_scenario: After PR #7 merges, any `uv run ... dashboard` rebuild followed by `git add -A` (or a future workflow using add-paths globs) commits regenerated dashboard/*.html again — exactly what commit c0a648c already did once by accident — and any stray .csv (e.g. literature_map.csv from the atlas step run locally) is committed to main, since main's *.csv rule is replaced by the corrupt line.
confirmed.suggestion: Before merging PR #7, split the line into two: `*.csv` and `dashboard/*.html` (add a trailing newline).

confirmed.summary: View B's real-market baseline silently falls back to std=1.0 when fewer than two finite real values exist for a dimension, so 'z-distance' becomes raw feature units while still being reported and thresholded as sigma.
confirmed.evidence: `real_std = {fn: (np.std(v) if len(v) > 1 else 1.0) for fn, v in real_dims.items()}` — 1.0 is in the raw units of whatever feature it replaces (kurtosis ~O(10), volatility ~O(0.01)). Downstream, `if real_std[fn] > 1e-9: devs.append(abs(fam_m - real_med[fn]) / real_std[fn])` treats it identically, rank_top_gaps applies the `val < 0.5` cut described as '0.5σ', and _gap_why prints 'deviates {val:.2f}σ from real-market median'.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: with a single real run in the DB (a realistic fresh-setup or feature-migration state, since len(fp)!=len(feature_names) rows are silently skipped), a family differing from real by 20 raw kurtosis units reports 'deviates 10.00σ' while a volatility mismatch of 0.002 raw (huge in market terms) scores 0.001 and is filtered as 'not really a gap'. Columns become mutually incomparable and the sigma language in the dashboard is fabricated.
confirmed.suggestion: Require a minimum count of finite real values per dimension (e.g., n>=4); emit NaN and skip the dimension otherwise, and record in the GapView description when a fact was skipped for insufficient baseline data. Never substitute a unitful 1.0.

confirmed.summary: The salience score log(row_total+col_total+1)-log(cell+1) is additive in marginals, so it ranks weak-evidence gaps (tiny row x huge column) above strong-evidence gaps (dense row x moderate column), inverting the surprise ordering implied by independence-expected counts.
confirmed.evidence: `sal = np.log(row_tot + col_tot + 1) - np.log(M + 1)`. For zero cells this is log(row+col+1), which is not monotone in the expected count lambda = row*col/N: with N=500, a 0-cell at (row=3, col=200) scores 5.32 with P(empty by chance)=exp(-1.2)=0.30, while (row=50, col=30) scores 4.39 with P(empty)=exp(-3.0)=0.05 — the 6x-less-surprising cell ranks higher. There is also no normalisation by row size anywhere (no per-row share, no expected-count residual).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced numerically (values above). In production, popular fact columns (fat-tails col_total ~100+) drag every near-empty subfield's zero cells to the top of the top-15/top-5 lists, so gap_propose spends its LLM proposals on subfields with 1-3 papers of evidence while statistically far stronger gaps in dense subfields rank lower.
confirmed.suggestion: Score zero cells by Poisson surprise under independence: lambda_ij = row_i*col_j/total (salience = lambda, or -log P(X=0) = lambda), optionally with a chi-square/Fisher-exact variant for nonzero cells. This is a one-line change and is also the row-normalised view the external reviewer asked for.

confirmed.summary: min_row_total=3 counts multi-label cell entries, not papers, so under the new 2-4-facts-per-paper prompt a single paper qualifies its whole subfield row for gap ranking; view A also counts co-emitted 'other' facts into row totals, inconsistently with coverage.py which suppresses them.
confirmed.evidence: `if row_tot < min_row_total or col_tot < min_col_total: continue` where `row_tot = float(v.matrix[i].sum())` sums fact-entries; _build_view_a increments a cell for every fact including 'other' (`for f in paper_facts: if f in facts: M[i, facts.index(f)] += 1`) while coverage.build_coverage drops 'other' when >=1 real fact exists ('other' is only excluded from gap columns via _GAP_NOISE_COLS, not from row totals or salience).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: one paper tagged (fat-tails, vol-clustering, other) makes its subfield row_total=3, and 'Minority Game x leverage' is then ranked as the TOP gap with why-text 'subfield has 3 mechanism×fact entries total but 0 targeting leverage' — a gap claim resting on a single paper, outranking a 4-paper row, with 1 of its 3 evidence entries being the catch-all 'other'. min_col_total=1 similarly lets a column supported by one entry corpus-wide count as 'dense enough'.
confirmed.suggestion: Track distinct papers per row (as coverage.py now does with row_papers) and threshold on that; apply the same other-suppression rule in view A as in build_coverage, or exclude 'other' from row totals used for salience/filtering; raise min_col_total.

confirmed.summary: _acf_decay_slope takes abs() of noise-level ACF values, so its docstring claims ('values are negative-or-zero'; 'magnitude near 0 means heavy long memory') are false for series without volatility clustering — no-clustering and long-memory series are confounded on this axis.
confirmed.evidence: `acfs = [max(1e-6, abs(_autocorr(absr, lag))) for lag in lags]` regresses log|ACF| on lag; for an IID series |ACF| is a flat ~1/sqrt(N) noise floor, so the slope is ~0 or positive. Docstring: 'Returns the slope of log|ACF| vs lag so values are negative-or-zero; magnitude near 0 means heavy long memory.'
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: IID normal (N=4000) gives slope +0.126 — positive, and larger in magnitude than a GARCH(1,1)'s -0.038 — while a genuinely long-memory series also sits near 0. Any consumer applying the documented reading (view B maps long-memory to this dim; feature-box interpretation) classifies zero-intelligence-style models as 'heavy long memory'; the 1e-6 floor can additionally inject log(1e-6)=-13.8 outliers into the regression.
confirmed.suggestion: Gate the feature on evidence of clustering (e.g., return NaN when mean |r| ACF over lags 1-5 is below the noise floor 2/sqrt(N)), and fix the docstring.

confirmed.summary: The cold-start docstring promises 'random if no labels yet — pure exploration cold start' but the implementation returns a deterministic fixed slice (all scores tie at 1.0, reversed stable argsort picks the last rows).
confirmed.evidence: Docstring: 'or random if no labels yet — pure exploration cold start'; code sets every score to 1.0 (`nov_finite = np.where(np.isfinite(novelty), ..., 1.0)`) then `order = np.argsort(score)[::-1]`.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: with zero labels, propose_next_k always proposes the same last-inserted rows (ids [13, 12] in the fixture) — the label CLI's zero-label path repeatedly shows the newest runs rather than a random exploration batch, biasing the first labels toward whatever model was inserted last (label_cli's separate cold-start command does randomize, so only the propose_next_k path via `next` is affected).
confirmed.suggestion: Use rng.permutation for the tie case or update the docstring to say 'most recently inserted rows'.

confirmed.summary: _validate_proposal only checks that predicted_fingerprint has all FEATURE_NAMES keys, not that the values are numeric, so a null feature value passes validation and later crashes execute_proposal AFTER a run row is already inserted.
confirmed.evidence: `missing = set(FEATURE_NAMES) - set(pf.keys())` accepts a key whose value is null. propose_cli.execute_proposal:307-310 then does `predicted_vec = np.array([p['predicted_fingerprint'][name] for name in FEATURE_NAMES]); predicted_std = (predicted_vec - mu_feat) / sd_feat` OUTSIDE the try/except that guards the model run (insert_run happens at line 286).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: pf={'volatility':None,...} passes the missing-keys check; np.array([...None...]) has dtype=object and `predicted_vec - mu_feat` raises TypeError. In execute_proposal the model run succeeded and insert_run already wrote a valid run row, but the TypeError propagates before update_proposal_status(status='executed'), so the proposal stays un-executed while an unlinked run is left in the runs table (shifting standardize()/novelty for all future computations). Re-executing duplicates the run.
confirmed.suggestion: Validate each predicted_fingerprint value is a real number (isinstance(v,(int,float)) and math.isfinite) in _validate_proposal, and move the prediction_error computation inside the guarded try in execute_proposal so a bad prediction can't orphan a run.

confirmed.summary: scaffold_mechanism_combo / scaffold_new_method write LLM-controlled new_class_name, base_method_a/b and combination_strategy verbatim into .py files with no identifier validation or docstring-escape handling.
confirmed.evidence: `class {name}:` and `from .model import {name}` / `__all__ = ["{name}"]` insert new_class_name raw; the combination_strategy / expected_behavior go into a `"""..."""` docstring via textwrap.fill without stripping embedded triple-quotes; base_method_a is interpolated into `REGISTRY["{base_a}"]`.
confirmed.failure_scenario:
Reproduced: a plan with new_class_name containing newlines/statements (e.g. 'Foo:
 import os
 os.system(...)
class Bar') and base_method_a containing '"] or __import__("os").system(...) #' is written straight into packages/abm_models/abm_models/_idea_*/model.py; a combination_strategy containing '"""' closes the docstring so injected statements become executable module code. abm_models/__init__.py does not auto-import _idea_* modules, so this is contained until a human imports the scaffold — but it is documented as review-before-running, so the surface is real when that review is skipped.
