confirmed.suggestion: Reject new_class_name unless name.isidentifier(); sanitize base_method_a/b against REGISTRY keys before interpolation; escape or strip triple-quotes in strategy/behavior/description text before embedding in a docstring.

confirmed.summary: Proposal status machine has no transition guards: `approve`/`reject` blindly overwrite any status including 'executed', re-opening the proposal so `execute` runs it a second time, inserting a duplicate run and silently repointing executed_run_id.
confirmed.evidence: cmd_approve (line 200-204) is `update_proposal_status(args.db, args.id, status="approved")` with no current-status check; db.py update_proposal_status (186-210) applies `SET status = ?` unconditionally and `executed_run_id = COALESCE(?, executed_run_id)` so a second execute's non-NULL run id replaces the first. execute_proposal's only gate is `if p["status"] not in ("proposed", "approved")` (propose_cli.py:261).
confirmed.failure_scenario: User executes proposal #7 (run 41 created, status='executed'), later runs `propose_cli approve 7` (e.g. batch-approving from a list) → status back to 'approved'. Next `execute 7` passes the gate, inserts run 42, repoints executed_run_id=42; run 41 remains, claiming proposal_id=7 in provenance_json. Reproduced end-to-end in a temp DB: two runs rows both with provenance {"proposal_id": 1}. prediction-error analytics and run counts double-count the proposal.
confirmed.suggestion: Enforce legal transitions in update_proposal_status (proposed→approved/rejected, proposed/approved→executed) or make cmd_approve/cmd_reject refuse when status='executed'.

confirmed.summary: strip-arxiv-versions renames/deletes only literature_methods rows: literature_code_snapshots keyed by the old vN id are orphaned (snapshot invisible to idea_plan, silently re-fetched), and the conflict branch DELETEs the vN row wholesale — losing its extraction, user_notes and user_tags without merging into the surviving base row.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 959-967 UPDATE/DELETE touch only literature_methods (`UPDATE literature_methods SET arxiv_id = ? ...`, `DELETE FROM literature_methods WHERE arxiv_id = ?`); no statement references literature_code_snapshots, whose rows are keyed by arxiv_id (db.py:427 `arxiv_id TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE`). Docstring admits "All metadata columns from the vN row are lost" but does not mention snapshots or user annotations. Same orphaning applies to cmd_delete_rows (500-502) and cmd_delete_low_relevance (828-832).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Row '2401.00001v2' has a cached repo snapshot; migration renames it to '2401.00001'. load_code_snapshots(db, ['2401.00001']) → {} (reproduced), so idea_plan loses the repo context and fetch-code-snapshots re-downloads from GitHub, leaving a permanently stale 'v2'-keyed row. Separately, delete-low-relevance --yes removes rows the user hand-annotated (user_notes) with no warning and leaves their snapshots orphaned; proposals.references_json entries pointing at deleted papers flip from '✓ in_db' to '⚠ unverified/hallucination-suspect' in `propose_cli show`.
confirmed.suggestion: Cascade the rename/delete to literature_code_snapshots (and warn when a to-be-deleted row has user_notes); in the conflict branch merge non-NULL columns into the base row before deleting.

confirmed.summary: The production DB (/home/user/test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db) contains ZERO literature_methods rows and no proposals/ideas/literature_code_snapshots/techniques tables, so the entire literature+proposal corpus that the branch's published figures and commit messages are built on does not exist anywhere in the audited environment.
confirmed.evidence: Query `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM literature_methods` -> 0. `SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table'` -> only {literature_methods, methods, runs}. `PRAGMA freelist_count` -> 0 and a `strings` scan of the raw file finds no deleted-row remnants (only 3 arxiv-ish hits, all from methods.references_json and the schema text), so the corpus never lived in this file or was fully vacuumed away. Yet commit 11e9d59 (2026-07-02, 'coverage: tag normalisation + other-suppression + honest paper counts') quotes live corpus counts — "'minority-game (9)' and 'Minority-Game (10)' appeared as separate rows", "Hawkes 12/28, LOB 12/26" — and mg_tree.html embeds '133 papers, 179 citations'. The workflow PR body (line 158-159) claims 'The JSON snapshot under `data/literature_methods.json` is the source of truth for the literature DB', but that file does not exist and `git log --oneline -- data` returns nothing (never committed on this branch).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Every one of the assigned hygiene checks is unanswerable: mechanism_tags case-duplicates, stylized_facts enum violations, arxiv_id vN/oa:W hygiene, relevance_score<0.3 survivors, extraction_attempts parking, NULL-abstract placeholders — all vacuous on 0 rows. The retagged ~120-paper corpus behind the coverage matrix, canon atlas, gap mining, and the Jul-2 'honest paper counts' commit cannot be reproduced, re-audited, or extended; if the local working state that produced it is gone, the research record is permanently lost.
confirmed.suggestion: Immediately export the populated DB (wherever it still exists) via load_literature() to data/literature_methods.json and commit it; add a CI assertion that restored row count >= last committed count so silent corpus loss fails the build.

confirmed.summary: All four LLM workflows rebuild an ephemeral data/abm_knowhow.db from a snapshot file that has never been committed, so every CI run (ingest, atlas_refresh, propose, methods_annotate) operates on an EMPTY literature corpus and accumulates nothing.
confirmed.evidence: ingest_arxiv.yml:61 `if [ -f data/literature_methods.json ]` (comment line 57: 'each Actions run can resume from the accumulated knowledge rather than starting empty'); atlas_refresh.yml:23/45, propose.yml:32 and methods_annotate.yml:33 all set `DB_PATH: data/abm_knowhow.db` and rebuild it fresh per run. But data/ is absent from the working tree, `git log -- data` is empty, and .gitignore line 11 ignores `data/*.db`. So the restore branch has never fired.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A scheduled atlas_refresh run regenerates the coverage matrix and canon atlas from 0 papers, opening a PR that silently replaces the 120-paper figures with empty ones; propose.yml generates proposals whose 'literature context' is empty while the prompt implies a corpus; each weekly ingest PR contains only that run's <=50 papers and merging two of them out of order discards one run's extractions.
confirmed.suggestion: Commit an initial snapshot, fail the workflow (instead of soft-continuing) when the snapshot is missing, and add a row-count regression guard before opening the PR.

confirmed.summary: The committed dashboard figures novelty_calibration.png / prediction_error_over_time.png / prediction_error_by_family.png (dashboard/assets, 2026-06-29 08:58) require executed rows in a `proposals` table that does not exist in the production DB, and the runs table contains no proposal-executed runs at all.
confirmed.evidence: analytics.py:53 `raise RuntimeError("no executed proposals with prediction_error")` — the plots read proposals with status executed. Production DB: `SELECT name FROM sqlite_master` has no `proposals` or `ideas` table; `SELECT MAX(created_at) FROM runs` -> '2026-06-27T04:53:18Z' (only the initial 126-row atlas population; zero later executed-proposal runs). The PNGs exist only as untracked local files (dashboard/assets/ is gitignored; `git ls-files dashboard` returns nothing).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Running the analytics/dashboard pipeline against the designated production DB raises RuntimeError; nobody can regenerate, extend, or verify the published prediction-error learning curves and novelty-calibration scatter — the proposal lifecycle data (status distribution, prediction_error history, orphan references) that this audit was asked to profile has ceased to exist.
confirmed.suggestion: Persist proposals/ideas in the durable DB (or dump them alongside the literature snapshot) and track dashboard/assets in git or a release artifact.

confirmed.summary: All 25 preference labels in the production runs table were bulk-written by a script in a 0.12-second window — they are the task-#14 'self-label pseudo-simulation' acceptance-test labels, indistinguishable from real human taste, and the ridge preference model will train on them by default.
confirmed.evidence: Query: all 25 non-NULL preference_labeled_at values lie between 2026-06-27T05:13:06.454502Z and 2026-06-27T05:13:06.567235Z with microsecond precision, in non-id order. update_preference()'s default timestamp format is `strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S") + "Z"` (no microseconds), so every label was written with an explicit scripted labeled_at. Distribution: {0.0: 8, 1.0: 13, 2.0: 4}, no negative Likert values despite VALID_LABELS spanning -2..+2 (label_cli.py:46).
confirmed.failure_scenario: The researcher runs `label_cli --db <prod> next`: load_runs(labeled=True) returns 25 rows (>= the 2 needed for the ridge fit), so the preference model fits on fabricated labels and the UCB acquisition ranks 'next runs to label/explore' by simulated taste — the human-in-the-loop novelty search silently optimises a test artifact's preferences.
confirmed.suggestion: Add a labeled_by/provenance column (human|simulation) or clear these 25 labels via clear_preference; have `label_cli summary` warn when all labels share one timestamp second.

confirmed.summary: The committed genealogy artifact lm_tree.html for cond-mat/9810262 (Lux–Marchesi 1999, one of the most-cited econophysics papers) contains only the root node — '1 papers, 0 citations' — i.e. the forward-citation walk silently returned nothing and the empty tree was published.
confirmed.evidence:
lm_tree.html:22: `arxiv:cond-mat/9810262 · forward citations, depth 2
1 papers, 0 citations.` (file committed, mtime Jul 1 03:25; 2.8 KB), while the sibling mg_tree.html shows '133 papers, 179 citations' (52 KB, Jun 29) — proving the pipeline works when the API walk succeeds. Lux & Marchesi (Nature 1999) has thousands of forward citations, so 0 is a fetch/matching failure, not reality.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A reader of the repo's genealogy trees concludes the Lux–Marchesi lineage has no retrievable descendants; any downstream canon/genealogy statistic that consumes these HTML/walk outputs undercounts an entire canon family. The generator produced a degenerate result without failing, and the artifact was committed unnoticed.
confirmed.suggestion: Have the tree builder exit non-zero (or refuse to write HTML) when the walk returns 0 citations for a root with known nonzero cited_by_count; regenerate lm_tree.html.

confirmed.summary: 36/126 runs (28.6%) have hill_tail_index pinned at the cap 20.0 (chiarella_iori 12, minority_game 12, gcmg 12); the 12 chiarella_iori fingerprints are two-point-degenerate series (excess kurtosis ≈ -1.999, the theoretical minimum -2; volatility ≈ 2e-4; hill_raw 199–302), and for MG/GCMG the uncapped diagnostic hill_raw is NULL exactly where the cap binds.
confirmed.evidence: Queries: chiarella_iori kurtosis values -1.9991..-1.9988, volatility ~0.0002, hill_raw MIN/MAX = 198.95/302.34, capped feature = 20.0 for all 12; `SELECT model_name, COUNT(*) FROM runs WHERE hill_raw IS NULL GROUP BY 1` -> gcmg 12, minority_game 12 (yet their stored hill feature is 20.0). adapters.py:89-92 acknowledges this deliberately: 'CI *is* a low-vol thin-tail specimen at default params, and the atlas should *show* that, not hide it.'
confirmed.failure_scenario: In the published atlas.png/features.png, three of eleven families share an identical saturated value on the hill axis, so distances among them on that axis are zero regardless of dynamics; chiarella_iori's 'position' in the map reflects a ±one-tick binary series (excess kurtosis -2 means returns take essentially two values), not CDA dynamics — and for the 24 attendance-series rows the hill_raw column that the schema calls the 'honest diagnostic' is missing, so cap-saturation cannot be distinguished from moderate thin tails after the fact.
confirmed.suggestion: Store hill_raw for attendance_excess series too, and add a `degenerate` flag (kurtosis <= -1.9 or hill_raw > cap) so atlas renderers can mark saturated specimens visually.

confirmed.summary: Measured coverage: the package is 47% covered overall and every entry point the 4 automation workflows execute lives in modules at 0–36% coverage; the branch's largest artifact, arxiv_cli.py (+2194 lines this branch), is 14% covered with 1171/1364 statements never executed by any test.
confirmed.evidence: pytest --cov=packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas (245 passed in 96s): TOTAL 6693 stmts, 3569 miss, 47%. Modules <40%: propose_cli.py 0% (367 stmts, incl. execute_proposal:246-337, cmd_auto, cmd_dump_md used by propose.yml), label_cli.py 0% (244), populate_refs.py 0% (99, run by propose.yml+atlas_refresh.yml), analytics.py 0% (98), populate.py 0% (74, run by both workflows), inverse_abm_cli.py 0% (69), real_refs.py 0% (57), arxiv_cli.py 14% (missing 509-667 cmd_extract_untagged loop, 1291-1405 cmd_canon_atlas, 1441-1705 all expand/enrich commands, 1868-2362 main() dispatch of ~34 subcommands), synthetic.py 16% (garch11/levy_walk/regime_switch generators 62-136 untested), idea_cli.py 17%, methods_cli.py 27% (draft-all/import-md CLI paths), atlas.py 33% (plot_atlas 130-168, main 219-237), arxiv_ingest.py 34% (ingest() 471-585: relevance-delete + retry loop; query_arxiv 335-360), code_links.py 36% (all fetch paths: fetch_pwc_repo 184-198, extract_github_from_pdf 242-263, fetch_repo_snapshot 380-399). Core logic modules are fine (db 85%, propose 87%, gap_finder 92%, dashboard 91%, canon_atlas 95%). Notably tests/test_propose.py:303 'test_end_to_end_execute_links_run_and_records_error' re-implements the execute logic inline ('Simulate the CLI execute path inline') instead of calling propose_cli.execute_proposal, leaving the real propose→run→measure function at 0%.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Any regression in an argparse default, subcommand wiring, or the untested ingest/execute loops (e.g. a renamed flag in cmd_ingest, an exception in execute_proposal's prediction-error math) passes the full green 245-test suite and only surfaces when a weekly cron run fails — or worse, when it silently writes wrong prediction_error/analytics into the research DB.
confirmed.suggestion: Add a parametrized CLI smoke layer (each subcommand against a tmp seeded DB with network/LLM stubbed) and a test that drives propose_cli.execute_proposal directly; consider a per-module coverage floor for the modules the workflows execute.

confirmed.summary: The unified LLM client retries rate-limit (429) errors immediately with no sleep/backoff — only a +0.1 temperature bump — so the retry budget is burned in milliseconds and the retry mechanism is ineffective for its primary target class; both provider call paths have 0% test coverage.
confirmed.evidence: `except Exception as exc: last_exc = exc; if attempt < max_retries and _is_transient(str(exc)): temperature = min(1.0, temperature + 0.1); continue` (identical in _call_openai:138-142 and _call_groq:181-185). _TRANSIENT_MARKERS includes "rate limit", "429", "overloaded" — all provider-side throttling signals that need elapsed time, not a temperature change, to clear. The module imports only `json, os` (lines 30-31); there is no time.sleep anywhere. Coverage run shows lines 106-144 and 149-187 (the entire _call_openai/_call_groq bodies incl. this loop) are never executed by the 245-test suite; only the pure helpers are tested (tests/test_llm_client.py).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Groq free-tier returns 429 during a 50-paper extract-untagged batch: three back-to-back requests fire within ~100ms, all 429, the exception propagates, and the paper is recorded as a failed extraction attempt — while a 2-4s backoff would have succeeded. Multiplied across the batch this wastes the run and inflates extraction_attempts.
confirmed.suggestion: Sleep with exponential backoff (and honor Retry-After) before retrying transient errors; add a mocked-client test for the retry loop.

confirmed.summary: One test dominates the suite (43.9s of ~67s wall = 66%) and, because ci.yml runs `pytest -m "not slow"` with no scheduled slow-lane, the SG parity go/no-go gate (spec 001 AC2) never executes in any CI run — it only runs when someone invokes pytest locally without the marker filter.
confirmed.evidence: Measured durations: 43.90s test_sg_parity.py::test_sg_findings_parity_within_5pct; next slowest 5.13s (test_fingerprint_atlas::test_every_registry_model_runs_and_fingerprints), 1.53s, 0.77s, 0.76s. ci.yml:24 `run: uv run pytest -m "not slow"` and no other workflow runs pytest; there is no cron/`-m slow` job. Also, the documented invocation `pytest -q` combines with pyproject addopts="-q" into -qq, which suppresses the final 'N passed' summary line entirely (observed: dot output ends at [100%] with no count).
confirmed.failure_scenario: A change to packages/abm_models or stylized_facts that breaks the 5%-relative parity with the recorded YH005 baseline merges green through CI; the regression is only discovered whenever a developer happens to run the full local suite, potentially weeks later with many suspect commits.
confirmed.suggestion: Add a weekly scheduled CI job running `pytest -m slow`, and drop one of the duplicate -q flags so summaries stay visible.

confirmed.summary: Generic single-word title_any tokens ('market', 'learning', 'trading', 'return', 'volatility') make gap views A/C assign papers to unrelated subfields via substring, so subfield membership is driven almost entirely by false positives.
confirmed.evidence: subfields.py:114 `"title_any": ["bounded rationality", "market"]` and :156 `"title_any": ["adaptive expectation", "learning", "expectation"]`. Reproduced against the real SUBFIELDS list: a paper tagged only ['market-making'] is claimed by 'Bounded rationality in markets'; ['deep-learning','gan'] is claimed by 'Adaptive expectations / learning'; ['returns-forecast'] is claimed by both 'Heavy-tailed returns' and 'Aggregational Gaussianity' (needle 'return'). Combined with the hyphenisation bug (multi-word needles dead), the specific needles never fire and ONLY these generic tokens do the matching.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A corpus heavy in ML trading papers inflates the 'Adaptive expectations / learning' and 'Bounded rationality' rows of view A; their row_totals pass min_row_total, so their zero cells outrank genuine gaps in log(row+col+1) salience, and gap_propose generates LLM research proposals citing paper counts that belong to a different subfield.
confirmed.suggestion: Match on word boundaries against individual tags (not a joined substring haystack), drop or qualify the generic tokens, and keep separate matcher vocabularies for the title-query use (canon_atlas) vs the tag-matching use (gap_finder).

confirmed.summary: build_coverage truncates row candidates at top_rows*3 by raw tag count before checking for canonical facts, silently dropping fact-bearing mechanisms when many higher-count fact-less tags exist.
confirmed.evidence: coverage.py:89 `candidate_tags = [t for t, _ in tag_counts.most_common(top_rows * 3)]` — tags ranked below 3x the row budget are never considered even when `kept` ends up shorter than top_rows. Reproduced: top_rows=1 with three 2-paper fact-less tags and one 1-paper tag targeting fat-tails -> row_labels == [] and n_papers_classified == 0 despite one classifiable paper.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A corpus where OA-concept fall-through creates 45+ multi-paper generic rows without canonical facts (common after broad OpenAlex walks) starves the matrix: specific mechanisms with real fact coverage silently vanish from both the heatmap and the markdown table.
confirmed.suggestion: Iterate all tags in most_common() order and stop only when len(kept) == top_rows.

confirmed.summary: matplotlib is imported by six fingerprint_atlas modules but is not declared in the package's dependencies (while `stylized-facts` is declared yet never imported), so a standalone install of the fingerprint-atlas wheel breaks every plotting/rendering feature.
confirmed.evidence: Line 6: `dependencies = ["numpy", "abm-models", "stylized-facts", "groq", "openai", "arxiv", "pypdf"]` — no matplotlib. AST scan of all 43 modules shows `import matplotlib` in analytics.py, atlas.py, coverage.py, inverse_abm.py, label_cli.py, literature_map.py (e.g. coverage.py:179-181 `import matplotlib / matplotlib.use("Agg") / import matplotlib.pyplot as plt`). Conversely `stylized_facts` is never imported anywhere in the package (only string literals like 'stylized_facts_targeted'). The package is built as its own wheel ([build-system] hatchling, [tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]).
confirmed.failure_scenario: `pip install fingerprint-atlas` (the built wheel) into a fresh env, then run any of: `arxiv_cli atlas`, `arxiv_cli coverage`, `label_cli` plotting, `inverse_abm_cli heatmap`, analytics learning-curve plots, or literature_map rendering -> ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'matplotlib' at the lazy import site. The repo's own CI never sees this because all workflows run `uv sync` at the workspace root, whose meta-package (root pyproject.toml line 11) happens to declare matplotlib.
confirmed.suggestion: Add `matplotlib` to fingerprint-atlas dependencies (or an extras group like `viz` mirroring stylized_facts' pattern) and drop the unused `stylized-facts` requirement.

confirmed.summary: Fingerprint vectors loaded from the DB are never length-validated against the v4 9-feature schema: _verbal_summary hard-unpacks 9 values and _format_row zips without strict, so any pre-v4 (6-D) run row crashes or silently truncates.
confirmed.evidence: label_cli.py:60: `vol, kurt, hill, acf_ret, acf_short, lev, acf_long, acf_decay, agg_kd = fp` (hard 9-way unpack); line 180-181: `"  ".join(... for n, v in zip(FEATURE_NAMES, fp))` (B905, silent truncation). db.py load_runs (line 809) does `"fingerprint": np.array(json.loads(r[4]), dtype=float)` with no check against len(FEATURE_NAMES). FEATURE_NAMES grew from 6 to 9 in v4 (fingerprint.py:19-31, comment 'v4: Cont-outside axes'). Reproduced: `_verbal_summary(np.zeros(6))` -> `ValueError: not enough values to unpack (expected 9, got 6)`.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Open any DB whose runs table predates the v4 feature addition (or archive/backup DBs from the v3 era) and run `label_cli next` or `cold-start`: _verbal_summary raises ValueError on the first v3 row. Mixed-era DBs also crash `np.vstack([r["fingerprint"] ...])` in propose_cli.py:298 with a ragged-array error. Latent today — read-only inspection of /home/user/test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db shows all 126 rows already length 9 — but there is no migration or guard, so the next feature-vector extension recreates the hazard.
confirmed.suggestion: Have load_runs validate (or pad/migrate) fingerprint length against FEATURE_NAMES and fail with an explicit schema-version message; store the feature list or a schema version per row.

confirmed.summary: Dead-code inventory: six never-referenced functions and several unused imports/locals survive in the package, some superseded by newer implementations.
confirmed.evidence: Verified by repo-wide grep (only the def line matches): canon_ingest.py:46 `_already_in_db` (superseded by `_existing_arxiv_ids` used at line 82), abm_families.py:281 `find_family`, db.py:104 `clear_preference`, openalex.py:60 `reset_rate_limit`, subfields.py:161 `validate_subfields`, techniques.py:414 `validate_techniques`. Plus pyflakes-confirmed unused: canon_ingest.py:77 local `now` (computed timestamp never written), idea_plan.py:31 `from .db import load_proposals, load_runs` unused, preference.py:32 `.fingerprint.distance_matrix` unused, arxiv_cli.py:347 `fetch_paper` unused in cmd_enrich_via_s2.
confirmed.failure_scenario: No direct runtime failure; risk is divergence — e.g. `_already_in_db` implements per-id SELECT semantics that no longer match the batched `seen`-set logic actually used, and `reset_rate_limit` suggests test isolation for openalex's global rate-limit state that no test performs; future maintainers may call the stale variants.
confirmed.suggestion: Delete or wire up each item; the validators are covered by a separate finding.

confirmed.summary: Module docstring promises the markdown table is 'sorted by row total — biggest mechanism cluster on top', but rows are now grouped into method-family bands (ABM/stat/ml/other) and ordered within-family by primary-tag paper count.
confirmed.evidence: Docstring line 6: "markdown table (sorted by row total — biggest mechanism cluster on top)". Actual ordering (lines 108-113): `kept_by_family: dict[str, list[str]] = {f: [] for f in _FAMILY_ORDER} ... for f in _FAMILY_ORDER: row_labels.extend(kept_by_family[f])` — a small ABM row precedes a much larger stat row.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A reader (or script) assuming the first markdown row is the biggest cluster picks the wrong 'dominant mechanism' when the largest tag is in the stat band.
confirmed.suggestion: Update the docstring to 'grouped by method family, by paper count within family'.

confirmed.summary: The cron workflows that actually execute (scheduled workflows run from the DEFAULT branch, i.e. main's copies) still open PRs with `base: claude/vibrant-ptolemy-fapuxw`; the fix to `base: main` exists only on this branch, so every weekly ingest/propose run keeps stacking data PRs onto a feature branch that is about to be merged and deleted.
confirmed.evidence: `git show origin/main:.github/workflows/propose.yml` line 86: `base: claude/vibrant-ptolemy-fapuxw` (same in main's ingest_arxiv.yml line 149). HEAD fixed both to `base: main` (propose.yml:98, ingest_arxiv.yml:149). Live proof: PR #3 (ingest-arxiv/28309101263) and PR #4 (propose/28374237859) both target claude/vibrant-ptolemy-fapuxw, created by github-actions[bot] on 06-28/06-29.
confirmed.failure_scenario: PR #7 stays open past Sunday 2026-07-05 21:00 UTC (ingest cron) or Monday 09:00 UTC (propose cron) -> another snapshot PR is opened against the feature branch; if the feature branch is deleted without GitHub's auto-retarget path (e.g. deleted via CLI before merging the cron PRs), the cron PRs are closed and their data commits stranded on orphan branches.
confirmed.suggestion: Merge PR #7 (which carries the base: main fix) before the next Sunday cron; delete the head branch via the GitHub merge UI so PRs #3/#4 auto-retarget to main.

confirmed.summary: Commit c0a648c (message: 'a') accidentally committed two near-duplicate LLM-scaffolded stub packages (_idea_self_organizing_speculation_game, _idea_speculation_game_self_organized) into the canonical, separately-audited packages/abm_models/abm_models/ package, plus regenerated dashboard HTML; the stubs ride into main via PR #7.
confirmed.evidence: `git show --stat c0a648c` lists dashboard/index.html, dashboard/markets.html, dashboard/research.html, lm_tree.html and 4 files under packages/abm_models/abm_models/_idea_*/. Both stub model.py files are auto-scaffold output ('"""auto-scaffold for idea #6 (SelfOrganizingSpeculationGame)...'). They are NOT registered in abm_models/__init__.py REGISTRY (explicit imports only), so they are inert, but they pollute the canonical model namespace and are the only junk commit message in an otherwise clean 89-commit history.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A future contributor (or the idea-pipeline scaffolder itself, which writes into this package) globs abm_models subpackages for model discovery or refactors REGISTRY to auto-discover, and the two half-implemented stubs surface as runnable 'models' in the atlas; meanwhile duplicate scaffolds for the same idea suggest the scaffold step ran twice without idempotency.
confirmed.suggestion: Drop both _idea_* directories from PR #7 (or move them to scratch/); make the scaffolder write outside the canonical package by default.

confirmed.summary: Spec-number collision across open branches: trusting-clarke-7tgj73 carries specs/002-minimum-viable-arena.md while yh007-policy-overhaul-h1rya4 (open PR #6) carries a different specs/002-yh007-kronos-microstructure.md; additionally, stacked PR #5 (laughing-bohr → yh007) has a verified content conflict on that same spec file against its own base.
confirmed.evidence: `git ls-tree origin/claude/trusting-clarke-7tgj73 specs/` -> 002-minimum-viable-arena.md; `git ls-tree origin/claude/yh007-policy-overhaul-h1rya4 specs/` -> 002-yh007-kronos-microstructure.md + 003-yh007-8-self-organized-book.md. Simulated merge of laughing-bohr into yh007: 'CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in specs/002-yh007-kronos-microstructure.md' (laughing-bohr forked at 530d77c, yh007 rewrote the spec across ~32 later commits, e.g. de1af33 retracting all ablation conclusions).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Merging both PR #6 and trusting-clarke puts two competing 'spec 002' ground-truth documents in specs/; README says specs are ground truth, so anyone citing 'spec 002' gets ambiguous provenance. PR #5's 2026-06-23 HF-infrastructure claims may also be stale relative to yh007's round-3..5 arbitration commits (06-24) and would need manual reconciliation.
confirmed.suggestion: Renumber trusting-clarke's spec to 004 if MVA is still pursued (it also conflicts on pyproject.toml/uv.lock after PR #7 — verified); close PR #5 or rebase it, checking whether its content was already superseded.

confirmed.summary: Generated artifacts are inconsistently tracked on this branch: genealogy outputs lm_tree.html / mg_tree.html sit tracked at the repo root and merge to main via PR #7, while the equivalent canon_atlas.html and dashboard/*.html were (attempted to be) gitignored, and notebooks/*.png analytics artifacts are committed too.
confirmed.evidence: `git ls-files | grep html` at HEAD includes lm_tree.html and mg_tree.html (repo root, vis-network-CDN interactive HTML from genealogy.py runs); .gitignore lines 2-3 ignore dashboard/assets/ and canon_atlas.html. PR #7 name-status also adds notebooks/inverse_abm_heatmap.png and notebooks/propose_analytics/*.png.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Every future genealogy CLI run dirties the working tree at the root; a reviewer diffing main sees stale binary-ish HTML churn; the artifacts also freeze one arbitrary run's output as if it were curated documentation, with no run-id provenance (unlike the atlas_refresh workflow which correctly namespaces outputs under notebooks/atlas/<run_id>/).
confirmed.suggestion: Either ignore *_tree.html like canon_atlas.html, or move them under a run-id directory as atlas_refresh does.

confirmed.summary: Post-merge NaN-bug status (the question from main's audit): merging PR #7 neither resurrects nor fixes the propose/preference NaN issues — preference.py is byte-identical between main and HEAD, and propose.py's branch diff touches only classify_references/_call_groq/propose_from_corpus, not the NaN paths — so main's audit findings there carry over to post-merge main unfixed.
confirmed.evidence: `git diff --quiet origin/main HEAD -- packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/preference.py` exits 0 (identical; std guard `self.std_ = np.where(std > 0, std, 1.0)` and novelty isfinite guard at lines 177-178 unchanged). propose.py diff (21+/54-) leaves `np.nanmean(fps_std[mask], axis=0)` (line 240) and `_feature_typical_ranges` NaN emission (line 286) untouched. Positive note: llm_client.py retains and extends main's f6fefde json_validate_failed retry ('insufficient_quota' now unrecoverable per a229a30), so no regression of main's last fixes.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Whoever fixes the main-audit NaN findings must apply them on top of post-merge main; if instead fixes were prepared against pre-merge main's propose.py, the _call_groq function they patch no longer exists (replaced by llm_client delegation), so a cherry-pick would conflict or patch dead code.
confirmed.suggestion: Land the main-audit NaN fixes AFTER PR #7 merges, against the merged tree.

confirmed.summary: Branch-inventory verdicts: the 1 commit this branch is behind (67b4e1c, a one-line README wording fix) merges cleanly (verified via merge-tree, 0 conflicts); stale-branch closures recommended: gallant-turing-ydrjo5 (personal university-math oral-exam notes, off-topic for the repo, stale since 06-23), ingest-arxiv/28287737193 (PR-less, 100% redundant snapshot), and laughing-bohr-el7mg7 (1 unique doc commit that conflicts with its own base); trusting-clarke-7tgj73 (MVA packages/atlas, stale since 06-18, superseded in direction by the yh007 line) needs an explicit keep-or-close decision.
confirmed.evidence: 67b4e1c changes only README.md line 3 (『…1つに収束させる器。』→『…1つに収束。』); `git merge-tree --write-tree origin/main HEAD` exits 0 with 0 CONFLICT lines. gallant-turing's entire diff vs merge-base is one file: notes/口述試験準備.md. ingest-arxiv/28287737193's snapshot has identical 30 arxiv_ids to PR #3's and no PR references it. trusting-clarke conflicts with post-PR#7 main on pyproject.toml + uv.lock (simulated).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Left open, the stale branches accumulate: the redundant snapshot branch invites an add/add conflict merge that would overwrite PR #3's (newer) extraction values; the off-topic notes branch pollutes the claude/* namespace making it harder to see which agent branches carry real work.
confirmed.suggestion: Delete ingest-arxiv/28287737193 and gallant-turing (salvage the notes file to a personal repo first); close PR #5 unless its HF-infra measurements are still load-bearing; timebox a keep/renumber/close decision on trusting-clarke.

confirmed.summary: delete-low-relevance and delete-rows perform irreversible bulk DELETEs with no backup/undo/export, preview capped at 30 rows, destroying user annotations and paid LLM/API enrichment, and leaving orphaned literature_code_snapshots rows (lead (a) confirmed).
confirmed.evidence: Lines 828-833: `with sqlite3.connect(args.db) as con: for r in victims: con.execute("DELETE FROM literature_methods WHERE arxiv_id = ?", (r["arxiv_id"],))` — no dump of deleted rows anywhere; the preview above shows only `for r in victims[:30]` (line 818). Same pattern in cmd_delete_rows lines 498-505. Deleted rows include `user_notes`/`user_tags` (hand-entered, db.py schema lines 236-237) plus S2/OA enrichment and LLM extractions that cost money to rebuild. Neither command deletes matching `literature_code_snapshots` rows (db.py:424-435, keyed by arxiv_id), leaving permanent orphans. The delete criterion `r["relevance_score"] < args.threshold` is an LLM-estimated score, so one bad extraction pass can put good papers below threshold corpus-wide.
confirmed.failure_scenario: User runs `delete-low-relevance --threshold 0.3 --yes` after a flaky extraction pass that scored several in-scope papers 0.2; 60 rows are deleted but only 30 were shown; hand-written user_notes on those rows are gone with no recovery path short of re-ingesting and re-annotating (a user already mass-deleted 85 rows once). Orphaned snapshot rows also linger under ids that no longer exist.
confirmed.suggestion: Before committing, dump the victim rows to a timestamped JSON file next to the DB (or add a deleted_at soft-delete column); print ALL victims or write the full list to a file; cascade the delete to literature_code_snapshots.
confirmed.verify_notes: Reproduced the mechanics offline (temp SQLite + real CLI): delete-low-relevance --yes deletes rows with no backup/export, preview capped at 30 (arxiv_cli.py:818), and leaves an orphaned literature_code_snapshots row (confirmed orphan count=1 after delete). Those literal code facts are correct. However the major-severity harm claims are unsupported: (1) 'destroying user annotations' cannot happen via any supported path — no code anywhere writes literature_methods.user_notes/user_tags (all UPDATE literature_methods statements at db.py:329/352/498/521/540/553/569 and arxiv_cli.py:894/961 touch only extraction/S2/OA/pdf/comment/code_url/arxiv_id; annotation workflows exist only for the methods and ideas tables), and the only DB present (/home/user/test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db) has 0 literature rows and 0 annotations; (2) 'paid enrichment' is essentially free to rebuild — S2/OpenAlex are free APIs and the code's own hint (arxiv_cli.py:664) prices full-backlog LLM re-extraction at ~¥20; deleted rows are recoverable by free arxiv re-ingest + cheap re-extract; (3) the 'user already mass-deleted 85 rows once' precedent has no evidence in the repo or git history — the only documented mass delete is 15 intentionally-removed wrong-paper rows (commit a96ce2b), the command's designed use case; (4) both commands are dry-run by default, require explicit --yes, and print the exact total victim count ('... and N more'), and commit dc66405 documents the eyeball-then-delete workflow — a deliberately destructive curation command behaving as documented is not a broken feature. What genuinely survives is minor: orphaned code-snapshot rows accumulate as dormant junk (snapshots are an explicitly best-effort cache, db.py:466-467, filtered by live ids and upserted on re-ingest), and the 30-row cap prevents enumerating victims 31+ from this command before committing. Latent/hygiene issues, not corrupted research data or a broken feature → minor, not major.

confirmed.summary: backfill-code's --force flag is a no-op — both branches compute the identical list — and the claimed 'idempotent semantics' of the default do not exist since no checked-stamp column is ever written.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 223-229: `if args.force: todo = [r for r in rows if not r.get("code_url")] else: ... todo = [r for r in rows if not r.get("code_url")]` — literally the same expression in both branches. The in-code comment claims 'Default keeps the idempotent semantics for subsequent runs' and --help says --force 're-checks papers that previously came back empty', but unlike pdf_scanned_at / s2_fetched_at there is no code_checked_at stamp, so the default already re-checks every empty row on every run.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A corpus of 300 papers where 200 have no code link: every `backfill-code` run (with or without --force, identically) re-hits the Papers-with-Code API 200 times and re-fetches arxiv author-comments at 3 s each for every paper whose comment is genuinely absent (fetch_arxiv_comment returns None, and line 245 `if comment is not None` means nothing is persisted) — ~10 minutes of redundant network traffic per run, while the user believes the default run skips already-checked rows.
confirmed.suggestion: Add a code_checked_at stamp (set even when nothing is found), make the default skip stamped rows, and make --force ignore the stamp — matching the documented semantics.
confirmed.verify_notes: Checked: (1) read cmd_backfill_code in full (arxiv_cli.py lines 210-270) — the `if args.force:` / `else:` branches at lines 223-229 are textually identical (`[r for r in rows if not r.get("code_url")]`), so --force is a genuine no-op; the else-branch comment even admits "--force vs default look the same" while claiming "Default keeps the idempotent semantics for subsequent runs". (2) Verified no neutralizing guard exists: db.py's literature_methods schema (dumped via PRAGMA table_info on a fresh ensure_literature_schema DB) has pdf_scanned_at, s2_fetched_at, oa_fetched_at but NO code-checked stamp column, and cmd_backfill_code is only reachable via the CLI dispatch (line 2336) with no pre-filtering caller. The argparse help (line 1998-1999: --force "re-check papers that previously came back empty") and the docstring both falsely imply the default skips previously-checked rows. (3) Verified the network-cost mechanics: code_links.fetch_arxiv_comment returns results[0].comment (None when absent, with arxiv.Client delay_seconds=3.0), and line 245's `if comment is not None` means an absent comment is never persisted, so it is re-fetched every run; resolve_code_url falls through to fetch_pwc_repo (Papers-with-Code HTTP) for every link-less row every run. (4) Offline repro (temp SQLite DB, 3 link-less rows, monkeypatched fetch_arxiv_comment/fetch_pwc_repo returning None with call counters): run 1 default = 3 comment + 3 PWC calls; run 2 default = identical 3+3 (no idempotency); run 3 --force = identical 3+3 (flag no-op). One nuance not changing the verdict: rows whose comment fetch returns a non-None string DO get it cached, so only genuinely-comment-less papers re-pay the 3s arxiv delay each run — but the PWC API is re-hit for ALL empty rows on every run regardless, and --force remains a no-op in all cases. Severity 'minor' is correct: results are never wrong, only redundant network traffic (~10 min per run on a 200-empty-row corpus) plus a misleading flag/doc — no data corruption, no broken results.

confirmed.summary: extract-untagged silently defeats ingest's --min-relevance-to-keep filter: papers whose extraction was deliberately dropped for low relevance are left extracted_by_model=NULL, so extract-untagged re-extracts them and stores the result unconditionally with no relevance threshold.
confirmed.evidence: cmd_extract_untagged selects `r for r in rows if not r.get("extracted_by_model") and _has_usable_abstract(r) and int(r.get("extraction_attempts") or 0) < max_attempts` (lines 559-564) and stores any non-empty payload via update_literature_extraction (lines 644-652) — there is no min-relevance option. Meanwhile ingest (arxiv_ingest.py:554-561) 'keeps metadata but skips extract' for below-threshold papers, leaving extracted_by_model NULL, i.e. exactly the state extract-untagged targets. Its docstring (lines 511-513) claims it targets rows that 'never went through the LLM step', which these rows contradict.
confirmed.failure_scenario: User ingests with `--min-relevance-to-keep 0.5` to keep tangential papers out of the coverage matrix; 40 papers are dropped. A later routine `extract-untagged` run (e.g. to pick up OA canon) re-extracts those 40, pays the LLM again for judgments already made once, and writes their low-relevance tags into the corpus — the coverage matrix and gap-miner now include papers the user explicitly filtered out, with no message that the filter was bypassed.
confirmed.suggestion: Either mark relevance-dropped rows distinctly (e.g. store relevance_score but not tags), or add a --min-relevance-to-keep option to extract-untagged and mention the interplay in both docstrings.
confirmed.verify_notes: Reproduced end-to-end offline (temp SQLite, mocked extract_paper_structured, no network). Chain verified: (1) arxiv_ingest.py:554-561 — below-threshold papers `continue` before update_literature_extraction and record_extraction_attempt is never called in ingest, so the row is left extracted_by_model=NULL, relevance_score=NULL, extraction_attempts=0; the inline comment claims 'mark with low score' but no score is ever written, making the dropped state indistinguishable from never-extracted. (2) arxiv_cli.py:559-564 — cmd_extract_untagged selects exactly that state; its argparse subparser (lines 1922-1952) has no relevance option, and lines 644-652 store any non-empty payload unconditionally. Docstring lines 512-513 ('never went through the LLM step') is contradicted. (3) No downstream neutralizer: cmd_coverage (arxiv_cli.py:428) and cmd_gap_mine (arxiv_cli.py:1238) load via load_literature(args.db) with no min_relevance; the word 'relevance' appears nowhere in coverage.py or gap_finder.py. Repro output: ingest(min_relevance_to_keep=0.5) with a 0.2-relevance mock payload dropped the paper (n_dropped=1); a subsequent cmd_extract_untagged re-called the extractor (2 total LLM calls for one paper), wrote relevance_score=0.2 + tags with extracted_by_model set, and build_coverage then included the paper as a coverage-matrix row. Severity stays minor: needs the opt-in flag plus a later extract-untagged run; the re-judged relevance_score IS persisted, so the pollution is detectable and reversible (list --min-relevance, delete-ids), and the impact is corpus hygiene and duplicate LLM spend in exploratory tooling, not corrupted research conclusions.

confirmed.summary: extract-untagged's retry-policy docstring is wrong for the --retry-empty-past path: 'empty payloads ... retried up to --max-attempts times, then parked' only holds for never-extracted rows; defective rows selected by --retry-empty-past ignore the attempts cap entirely, and ingest-created empty-but-marked-extracted rows are never parked at all.
confirmed.evidence: Docstring lines 520-526 promise parking after --max-attempts. The parked/untagged filters apply the cap only to `not r.get("extracted_by_model")` rows (lines 559-564, 586-590), while the --retry-empty-past selection (lines 566-568) is `[r for r in rows if _is_extraction_defective(r) and _has_usable_abstract(r)]` with no `extraction_attempts` check — such rows are retried on every invocation forever, with record_extraction_attempt (line 638) incrementing a counter that is never consulted for them. (Root cause of defective rows: arxiv_ingest.ingest lines 546-572 stores even all-empty payloads with extracted_by_model set, with no is_empty guard.) Note: lead (b) 'parked rows silently shrink the pool' is refuted — lines 591-593 print '(N row(s) parked — attempts ≥ M ...)'.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A paper whose abstract reliably yields an empty payload is retried (and billed) on every `extract-untagged --retry-empty-past` run indefinitely; the user reading the docstring expects it to park after 3 attempts. Meanwhile its 'attempt 7/3' style progress line (line 640-642) prints a nonsensical fraction.
confirmed.suggestion: Apply the extraction_attempts cap to the --retry-empty-past selection too, and fix the docstring; add the same is_empty guard to ingest() so defective rows stop being created.
confirmed.verify_notes: Read cmd_extract_untagged in full (arxiv_cli.py:509-667) plus record_extraction_attempt/update_literature_extraction (db.py:316-365) and the ingest path (arxiv_ingest.py:419-583). Static reading confirms all three sub-claims: (1) the attempts cap and the parked filter are both gated on `not r.get("extracted_by_model")` (lines 563, 588-589), while the --retry-empty-past selection (lines 566-568) has no extraction_attempts check and only matches rows WITH extracted_by_model set — so such rows can never be parked and are re-selected every run; (2) record_extraction_attempt bumps the counter but never clears extracted_by_model, so a repeatedly-empty defective row stays eligible forever; (3) ingest genuinely creates such rows: extract_paper_structured always sets extracted_by_model=model even for all-empty payloads, and ingest's only guard is the relevance filter, which an all-empty payload (relevance None) passes. No neutralizing guard exists elsewhere; the --max-attempts help text (lines 1938-1942, 'Prevents infinite retries') is also violated on this path. Reproduced offline with a temp SQLite DB and a monkeypatched extractor returning empty payloads: a defective row with attempts=6 was selected and 'billed' on 3 consecutive --retry-empty-past runs, printing 'attempt 7/3', 'attempt 8/3', 'attempt 9/3', attempts ending at 9, never counted as parked; a never-extracted control row with attempts=3 was correctly parked with the printed parked-count message (consistent with the claim's note refuting the 'silent shrink' lead). Severity 'info' is right: opt-in flag per run, small wasted LLM cost, no data corruption — a docstring/help-text vs behavior mismatch plus a nonsensical progress fraction.

confirmed.summary: Exit codes are inconsistent across LLM/ingest paths: gap-propose returns 0 even when every proposal generation FAILed, and all --auto-ingest paths (canon, canon-atlas, expand-via-s2/oa) ignore the ingest summary's n_errors, while plain ingest/ingest-ids return 1 on any error.
confirmed.evidence: cmd_gap_propose prints `FAIL: {g['view']} ...` for entries with `"error" in p` (lines 1210-1214) yet unconditionally ends `return 0` (line 1224). cmd_canon_atlas's auto-ingest (lines 1385-1405), cmd_canon (1114-1129), cmd_expand_via_s2 (1691-1705) and cmd_expand_via_oa (1572-1585) all print the ingest summary but never inspect summary["n_errors"], returning 0. Contrast cmd_ingest line 64: `return 0 if summary["n_errors"] == 0 else 1`.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A scheduled job runs `gap-propose --top 5` while the Groq key is expired: all 5 gaps print FAIL, zero proposals are inserted, exit code 0 → CI marks the step green and downstream steps (propose execute, dashboards) silently operate on stale data. Same for `canon-atlas --auto-ingest-missing` when every extraction errors.
confirmed.suggestion: Return non-zero from gap-propose when created contains only error entries (or any, matching ingest's convention), and propagate n_errors from auto-ingest summaries.
confirmed.verify_notes: Verified all cited sites in packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/arxiv_cli.py: cmd_gap_propose prints per-entry FAIL (1210-1214) and unconditionally returns 0 (1224); cmd_canon (1118-1129), cmd_canon_atlas (1388-1405), cmd_expand_via_oa (1576-1585), cmd_expand_via_s2 (1695-1705) all print the ingest summary with the errors key stripped and never check summary["n_errors"], returning 0; plain ingest (line 64) and ingest-ids (line 101) return 1 on any error. No neutralizing guards: main() (2362) returns the handler value straight to SystemExit, and propose_from_top_gaps (gap_propose.py 349-351) catches every per-gap exception into {"error": ...} entries so an expired/missing LLM key cannot escape as an exception (llm_client raises RuntimeError, which is swallowed per gap). Reproduced offline in a scratch dir with a temp SQLite DB and API keys unset: real propose_from_top_gaps → real call_llm raise path produced 2/2 FAIL lines, zero proposals inserted (proposals table never created), and cmd_gap_propose returned exit code 0. The only overstated detail is the failure scenario's framing: no repo workflow actually schedules gap-propose, and atlas_refresh.yml runs canon-atlas WITHOUT --auto-ingest-missing (and canon-atlas does return 2 for OpenAlex lookup failures — a different error class), so nothing in current CI is silently green today; the bug bites only when these subcommands are automated. That latency is exactly what severity "minor" encodes, so minor is the correct rating.

confirmed.summary: The --db requirement gate `_db_free = {"glossary"}` is stale: genealogy, diagnose-oa, diagnose-s2 and diagnose-concept never read args.db but the parser refuses to run them without --db.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 2329-2331: `_db_free = {"glossary"}; if args.cmd not in _db_free and not args.db: ap.error(...)`. AST scan confirms cmd_genealogy, cmd_diagnose_oa, cmd_diagnose_s2 and cmd_diagnose_concept contain zero `args.db` accesses (they are pure OpenAlex/S2 network tools). The --db help (lines 1870-1871) hard-codes the same claim: "required by every command except `glossary` (which is DB-free)".
confirmed.failure_scenario: `python -m fingerprint_atlas.arxiv_cli genealogy --root-concept 'Minority game'` errors with "--db is required for `genealogy`" even though no DB is touched; users must invent a dummy path (which, worse, ensure-* helpers in other commands would happily create as an empty DB file if they typo a real one).
confirmed.suggestion: Extend _db_free to {"glossary", "genealogy", "diagnose-oa", "diagnose-s2", "diagnose-concept"} and fix the --db help string.
confirmed.verify_notes: Claim verified in full. (1) Gate at arxiv_cli.py:2329-2331 is exactly as cited: _db_free = {"glossary"}, then ap.error if --db missing; --db (line 1869) has default=None and no env fallback. (2) Exhaustive grep of all 84 args.db occurrences shows none inside cmd_diagnose_concept (973-1008), cmd_genealogy (1011-1077), cmd_diagnose_oa (1588-1605), or cmd_diagnose_s2 (1608-1635); these four are pure OpenAlex/S2 HTTP tools and pass only explicit scalars to helpers, never the args namespace. (3) Reproduced offline (argparse errors before dispatch, no network): `python -m fingerprint_atlas.arxiv_cli genealogy --root-concept 'Minority game'` exits 2 with "error: --db is required for `genealogy`". Severity minor is correct: workaround is any dummy --db string (never opened by these commands, so no stray file created by them); the empty-DB-creation aside only applies to other commands that legitimately require --db. Help text at 1870-1871 also hard-codes the stale "except `glossary`" claim.

confirmed.summary: expand-via-oa fetches every seed paper from OpenAlex twice: once for the n_refs pre-count and again inside fetch_references.
confirmed.evidence: Line 1534: `seed_paper = fetch_paper(seed["arxiv_id"])` (used only for `len(seed_paper["referenced_works"])`), then line 1544 `refs = fetch_references(seed["arxiv_id"], ...)` whose first statement (openalex.py:722) is `paper = fetch_paper(arxiv_id)` — an identical second HTTP round trip.
confirmed.failure_scenario: `expand-via-oa --all-seeds-since 2000` on a 400-paper corpus issues ~400 redundant works lookups on top of the per-reference resolution calls, roughly doubling seed-level OpenAlex traffic under the shared anonymous rate limit and lengthening the run by n_seeds × (latency + sleep).
confirmed.suggestion: Let fetch_references accept a pre-fetched paper dict, or return (n_refs, refs) from a single call.
confirmed.verify_notes: Checked: (1) Read cmd_expand_via_oa in full (/home/user/financial-abm-lab/packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/arxiv_cli.py:1492-1585). Line 1534 `seed_paper = fetch_paper(seed["arxiv_id"])` is used only for `n_refs = len(seed_paper["referenced_works"])`; line 1544 calls `fetch_references(seed["arxiv_id"], ...)`. (2) Read openalex.py: fetch_references's first statement (line 722) is `paper = fetch_paper(arxiv_id)`, and fetch_paper (line 204) issues a fresh `_http_get_json` on `/works/doi:10.48550/arXiv.<id>` per call. Grepped the whole module for lru_cache/_cache/memo/functools — zero hits, and _http_get_json (lines 102-122) has no response cache, so nothing neutralizes the duplicate. fetch_references takes no pre-fetched-paper parameter, so the redundancy is unavoidable in the current API. (3) Offline repro (stubbed _http_get_json counting URLs, temp SQLite DB with 2 seed rows, real cmd_expand_via_oa via SimpleNamespace args, no network): each seed's /works/doi: URL was fetched exactly 2x — 2 redundant seed-level lookups for 2 non-empty seeds, matching the claim. Two minor imprecisions in the claim that don't change the verdict: (a) seeds with empty referenced_works `continue` at line 1541 before fetch_references, so only non-empty seeds are double-fetched ("every seed" is a slight overstatement; ~400 redundant lookups on a 400-paper corpus assumes most seeds non-empty); (b) the redundant call adds one HTTP round-trip latency per non-empty seed but no extra sleep — fetch_references's sleep is per-reference and the per-seed sleep_for_rate_limit at line 1558 runs once regardless, so "n_seeds x (latency + sleep)" overstates the wall-clock cost. Severity info is correct: pure efficiency/traffic issue, results are identical, and total traffic is dominated by the per-reference _resolve_oa_work calls anyway (1 extra call per seed vs up to refs_per_seed resolution calls per seed).

confirmed.summary: canon-atlas --auto-ingest-missing writes the HTML before ingesting, so the artifact advertised as closing "the canon-coverage gap ... in one batch — single command" always depicts the stale pre-ingest coverage.
confirmed.evidence: Line 1340 `render_html(atlas, args.out)` executes before the auto-ingest blocks at lines 1385-1404; the atlas dict's in_db/coverage fields are never recomputed after ingest_by_ids / ingest_canon_via_oa succeed. Docstring lines 1288-1290: "pulls every arxiv-hosted canon paper not yet in DB through arxiv_ingest in one batch — single command to close the canon-coverage gap across the whole atlas."
confirmed.failure_scenario: User (or the atlas_refresh workflow) runs `canon-atlas --auto-ingest-missing`, ingest succeeds for all missing canon, exit 0 — but the freshly written atlas.html (the deliverable committed to the PR) still shows the old sub-100% coverage cells; a second full OpenAlex sweep is needed to get a truthful artifact, which nothing in the output tells the user.
confirmed.suggestion: After a successful ingest, recompute in_db flags from the DB (no new OpenAlex calls needed) and re-render, or at least print "re-run canon-atlas to refresh the HTML".
confirmed.verify_notes: Read cmd_canon_atlas in full (arxiv_cli.py:1283-1405): render_html(atlas, args.out) at line 1340 runs before missing_canon (1370) and the auto-ingest blocks (1385-1404); the function returns 0 at 1405 with no re-render, no recomputation of in_db/coverage, and no stdout hint that the HTML is stale. Reproduced offline (temp dir, stubbed build_atlas/render_html/ingest_by_ids, no network): call order build_atlas -> render_html -> missing_canon -> ingest_by_ids; written HTML contains pre-ingest coverage (0.5, 1/2) after a fully successful ingest; exit 0; stdout contains no staleness/re-run warning. The repo itself admits the defect: docs/canon_atlas_next_actions.md lines 75-76 ("After ingestion, rebuild coverage from the DB before writing the final HTML. The current flow writes HTML before ingestion, leaving stale coverage") and line 99 lists "post-ingestion HTML reflects the updated DB" as a test yet to be added. Docstring lines 1288-1290 advertise the single-command gap-closing exactly as quoted, and docs line 27 confirms a second run needs a full OpenAlex sweep. One overstatement that does not change the verdict: .github/workflows/atlas_refresh.yml step D (lines 103-107) runs canon-atlas WITHOUT --auto-ingest-missing, so the workflow-committed PR artifact is not affected; only manual use of the flag hits the scenario. Severity info is correct: DB ingest succeeds, no data corruption, artifact is one-run stale, and the fix is already a documented next action.

confirmed.summary: find_canon_papers' plain-search fallback omits `sort=cited_by_count:desc`, so when the phrase filter returns empty the function returns relevance-ranked works instead of the highest-cited — contradicting its own contract ('Top-N highest-cited papers about a topic') and skewing canon membership/coverage for any subfield served by the fallback.
confirmed.evidence: url = (f"{_OA_BASE}/works?search={q_safe}" f"&filter={retry_filter}" f"&per-page={n_per_page}&select={select}") — the primary path (line 351-352) and the seed path (line 494) both append &sort=cited_by_count:desc; the retry does not, and OpenAlex's default ordering for ?search= is relevance_score desc.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A narrow subfield query (e.g. 'aggregational Gaussianity') returns zero phrase-filter hits and falls into the retry: the atlas entry's 'canon' becomes the 8 most-relevant works with >9 citations rather than the 8 most-cited, so coverage % and the missing-canon ingest list are computed against a different paper set than every other subfield, with no marker distinguishing the two rankings.
confirmed.suggestion: Append &sort=cited_by_count:desc to the retry URL (or record fallback ranking mode in the result).
confirmed.verify_notes: Read find_canon_papers in full (openalex.py:302-384) plus find_canon_papers_by_seed (472-508) and all callers. Verified factually: primary path (lines 351-352) and seed path (line 494) both append &sort=cited_by_count:desc; the plain-search retry (lines 365-367) builds the URL with only search=, filter=cited_by_count:>9[,publication_year:<=Y], per-page, select — no sort. Checked for neutralizing guards: none exist. find_canon_papers slices raw["results"][:n] in server order with no client-side sort; build_atlas (canon_atlas.py:81-153) consumes the list as-is to compute coverage and does not set any marker for this retry (fallback_used only flags the separate seed-concept fallback, so the search-retry is invisible to callers exactly as claimed); cmd_canon (arxiv_cli.py:1095) prints/auto-ingests in returned order; cmd_genealogy --root-concept (arxiv_cli.py:1037-1043) takes canon[0] as 'the top-cited paper'. per-page=min(n,200) compounds the issue: the truly most-cited work may not even be in the fetched page. Author intent is unambiguous — the test docstring at tests/test_genealogy.py:148 says the fallback 'must fall back to /works?search=...&sort=cited_by_count', but that test never exercises the retry branch (its mock returns non-empty phrase-filter results), so the omission is untested. OpenAlex's documented default sort under ?search= is relevance_score:desc; even if it were different, it is not cited_by_count:desc, so the 'Top-N highest-cited' contract is violated regardless. Reproduced offline in mktemp -d by monkeypatching oa._http_get_json: phrase-filter call returned empty, retry fired, retry URL contained no sort= param, and find_canon_papers('aggregational Gaussianity', n=2) returned the two most-relevant works (12 and 150 citations) while excluding the 4000-citation paper. Severity minor is correct: only the fallback path (narrow queries with zero phrase hits) is affected, the >9 citation floor bounds the noise, and mainline subfields are unaffected — a latent/edge defect that skews canon membership and coverage % for fallback-served subfields only.

confirmed.summary: search_by_title returns the first of 3 relevance-search hits that matches the year and carries an arxiv id, with no title-similarity check at all, and cmd_fix_arxiv_ids uses that result to UPDATE literature_methods.arxiv_id — the corpus primary key — so a wrong hit rewrites a row's identity; conversely the strict year equality rejects correct journal twins whose publication_year is offset by one from the arxiv year.
confirmed.evidence: for work in raw["results"]: if year and work.get("publication_year") != year: continue; arxiv_id = _arxiv_id_from_work(work); if arxiv_id: return {...} — contrast _fetch_work_by_title (lines 194-200) which requires exact normalized title equality. Consumer arxiv_cli.py:891-896 executes UPDATE literature_methods SET arxiv_id = ? on the returned id, guarded only by `if "/" not in canonical: skip`.
confirmed.failure_scenario: fix-arxiv-ids runs on a truncated row titled 'Scaling in financial data' (year 2001): OpenAlex full-text search ranks the true paper's journal record first but with publication_year 2002 (skipped by the year check), and hit #2 is a different 2001 old-style arxiv paper mentioning the phrase — its id is written over the row's arxiv_id, silently re-pointing the literature row (and all downstream enrichment/coverage) at the wrong paper.
confirmed.suggestion: Require normalized-title equality (reuse _fetch_work_by_title's comparison) and tolerate +/-1 year before returning a match.
confirmed.verify_notes: Verified all three legs of the claim. (1) search_by_title (openalex.py:513-541) does a relevance search (?search=...&per_page=3), skips hits where publication_year != year via strict equality (line 530), and returns the FIRST surviving hit with an arxiv id — zero title-similarity checking; the cited contrast is accurate: _fetch_work_by_title (openalex.py:194-200) in the same file requires exact normalized title equality. (2) Consumer cmd_fix_arxiv_ids (arxiv_cli.py:837-899) executes UPDATE literature_methods SET arxiv_id=? WHERE arxiv_id=? (lines 891-896) guarded only by the `"/" not in canonical` skip; it never checks that the recovered id's 7-digit tail matches the original truncated id, and the command runs LIVE by default (--dry-run is opt-in store_true, arxiv_cli.py:2038; no confirmation prompt). (3) Reproduced the exact failure scenario offline (temp SQLite + monkeypatched _http_get_json, no network, repo untouched): row ('0103089','Scaling in financial data',2001); mocked hit#1 = true paper with publication_year 2002 (journal twin, arxiv id present) → skipped by the year check; hit#2 = different 2001 old-style paper → returned; cmd_fix_arxiv_ids rewrote the DB primary key to cond-mat/0112999 (wrong paper, digits don't even match the original) with exit 0. Control with year=None picked the true hit, proving the strict-year skip is the diverting mechanism. The year-offset premise is realistic: OpenAlex merges preprint+journal versions and reports the journal publication_year. Severity 'minor' is honest: one-shot maintenance command, narrow suspect set (^\d{7}(v\d+)?$ ids only), wrong hit must itself be an old-style pre-2007 paper, and the old→new mapping is printed (an attentive operator could catch the digit mismatch) — but when it fires the corpus PK is silently corrupted. Repro: /tmp/claude-0/-home-user/0bb0fcda-825b-5170-9011-76eb3dee386c/scratchpad/repro/repro.py.

confirmed.summary: fetch_references returns [] for rate-limit/network failures (indistinguishable from 'paper has no references') and never paginates past S2's 100-per-page cap despite the `limit` parameter, so expand-via-s2 silently reports 'no new arxiv-hosted candidates' under throttling and silently truncates reference walks for limit>100.
confirmed.evidence: page_size = min(limit, _REFS_PAGE_LIMIT); url = (...f"?fields={_REF_FIELDS}&limit={page_size}"); raw = _http_get_json(url); if not raw or not isinstance(raw.get("data"), list): return [] — no offset loop, and _http_get_json collapses 429-after-retry/404/5xx into None. cmd_expand_via_s2 (arxiv_cli.py:1665-1681) treats [] as 'no refs'. The sibling module's own _http_get_json_with_status docstring (lines 49-54) says the status is exposed so callers can distinguish these cases, but fetch_references does not use it — while the enrich path was explicitly rewritten to (arxiv_cli.py:355-358).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Unauthenticated user runs `expand-via-s2 --top-seeds 10` right after an enrich pass burned the 100-req/5-min budget: every fetch_references gets 429 twice, returns [], and the command prints 'no new arxiv-hosted candidates discovered.' — the researcher concludes the citation neighborhood is exhausted. Separately, `--refs-per-seed 150` silently fetches only the first 100 references of each seed.
confirmed.suggestion: Return status alongside data (or log per-seed HTTP status like the enrich command); either paginate with offset or clamp/warn when limit > 100.
confirmed.verify_notes:
Set out to refute; could not — every element of the claim checks out against the code, and I reproduced both failure modes offline with monkeypatched HTTP (no network, temp SQLite, repo untouched).

What I checked:
1. /home/user/financial-abm-lab/packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/semantic_scholar.py:136-167 — fetch_references builds one URL with page_size = min(limit, _REFS_PAGE_LIMIT=100), makes a single _http_get_json call (line 148), and returns [] when raw is None. There is no offset loop and the response's "next" pagination token is never read. _http_get_json (lines 72-83) collapses 429-after-one-retry, 404, 5xx, and network errors into None.
2. Caller: arxiv_cli.py cmd_expand_via_s2 (lines 1638-1705). Line 1665 calls fetch_references; there is zero status/429 handling anywhere in the function (grep confirmed). All-seeds-failed and all-seeds-genuinely-empty both fall into the `if not candidates:` branch at 1679-1681, printing "no new arxiv-hosted candidates discovered." and returning 0.
3. The asymmetry evidence is accurate: _http_get_json_with_status's docstring (lines 49-54) exists precisely so callers can distinguish these cases, and cmd_enrich_via_s2 (arxiv_cli.py:336-421) was rewritten to use it with per-status counters — its own comment at lines 355-358 calls the identical pattern "the silent-429 swallowing bug". fetch_references still uses the legacy wrapper.
4. No neutralizing guards: sleep_for_rate_limit only paces between seeds (irrelevant once throttled; S2's unauthenticated pool is shared, so throttling need not be self-inflicted), and no test pins the failure-mode behavior.

Repro (script in scratchpad, executed): (A) with _http_get_json_with_status stubbed to always return (429, None), fetch_references returned [] — bytewise identical to the 200-with-empty-data case; driving cmd_expand_via_s2 against a temp DB seeded with one paper produced exit code 0 and output "walking references of 1909.03185 … / no new arxiv-hosted candidates discovered." with no warning token (no '429'/'rate'/'error'/'fail' anywhere), after exactly 2 HTTP attempts (initial + one retry, both 429). (B) with a stub returning 100 refs plus a "next": 100 token, fetch_references("…", limit=150) made exactly 1 request with &limit=100 (no offset param) and returned 100 refs — silent truncation as claimed. The docstring "Return up to `limit` references" makes limit>100 look supported.

Severity: minor is correct. The primary command path only prints candidates for human review (--auto-ingest is opt-in), no data is corrupted or stamped wrongly (unlike the fixed enrich bug), a later re-run recovers, and the >100 truncation requires a non-default --refs-per-seed (default 50). The real cost is a misleading "citation neighborhood exhausted" signal under throttling — a latent/edge-condition UX trap, exactly the minor tier. The one caveat against the auditor: fetch_references' own docstring says "Empty list on any failure", so the library function behaves as documented — but the defect stands because the caller consumes [] as data and the maintainers themselves classified this exact swallowing pattern as a bug when they fixed it in the enrich path.

confirmed.summary: Two advertised escape hatches are unreachable: reset_rate_limit() is never called anywhere (its docstring cites a 'canon-atlas retry loop' that does not exist), and _top_concept_id_from_seed's skip_concepts parameter ('extends this deny-list at call time') cannot be reached because its only caller find_canon_papers_by_seed neither accepts nor forwards it.
confirmed.evidence: def reset_rate_limit() -> None: """Public escape hatch for callers who want to force-clear the lock (e.g. a canon-atlas retry loop after fixing the query).""" — grep over the package shows no caller; resolved = _top_concept_id_from_seed(seed) at line 484 passes no skip_concepts, and find_canon_papers_by_seed's signature has no corresponding parameter.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A user whose seed keeps resolving to an unhelpful concept follows the docstring and looks for the call-time deny-list extension — there is no way to supply it through any public function or CLI flag; likewise nothing in the sweep ever clears a tripped rate lock early, so the documented recovery paths are fictional.
confirmed.suggestion: Either plumb skip_concepts through find_canon_papers_by_seed (and a CLI flag) and call reset_rate_limit from the canon-atlas retry/hint path, or delete both and fix the docstrings.
confirmed.verify_notes:
Checked /home/user/financial-abm-lab/packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/openalex.py (branch claude/vibrant-ptolemy-fapuxw confirmed), all callers via repo-wide grep, canon_atlas.py in full, and the cmd_canon_atlas CLI surface in arxiv_cli.py; also ran an offline monkeypatched repro (no network).

Facts confirmed: (1) reset_rate_limit (openalex.py:60) appears exactly once in the entire repo — its definition; no package code, CLI, or test calls it. The docstring's example caller, 'a canon-atlas retry loop after fixing the query', does not exist: canon_atlas.build_atlas (canon_atlas.py:80-102) catches OpenAlexQueryError, tries find_canon_papers_by_seed once per subfield, records the error string, and moves on — no retry, and nothing anywhere clears a tripped _RATE_LIMITED lock early; only the 90 s auto-cooldown inside _http_get_json (lines 107-110) does. (2) _top_concept_id_from_seed's skip_concepts deny-list extension is unreachable through any public conduit: AST check confirms the sole call site (openalex.py:484) passes 1 positional arg and zero keywords; inspect.signature shows find_canon_papers_by_seed is (seed, *, n=30, year_max=None) — passing skip_concepts raises TypeError (demonstrated); build_atlas and the canon-atlas CLI expose no corresponding option; repo-wide grep shows skip_concepts only inside openalex.py. Runtime repro also showed the deny-list logic itself works when the private function is called directly (seed resolving to 'Unhelpful concept' is skipped), so the parameter is functional but has no public path — exactly the claimed failure scenario.

Adversarial counterpoints considered and rejected: (a) 'reset_rate_limit is a public API so zero internal callers is fine' — true in isolation, and the docstring's 'e.g.' phrasing is arguably hypothetical, but the auditor's substantive assertions (no caller anywhere, no canon-atlas retry loop, nothing clears the lock early) are all factually accurate; a user following the docstring finds no such loop to hook into. (b) 'users can call the private _top_concept_id_from_seed directly or pass a concept id to find_canon_papers' — workarounds exist, but the claim only asserts no public function or CLI flag reaches skip_concepts, which is correct. Severity 'info' is right: documentation/dead-parameter cruft with functional workarounds (90 s auto-cooldown, manual concept-id path), no wrong results or broken feature in realistic use.

confirmed.summary: fetch_work_full's docstring promises a 'primary_category' key that the returned dict never contains (it returns an undocumented 'arxiv_id' instead), and the module header says oa_concepts stores 'top 3' concept names while _normalise_work/fetch_work_full take 5.
confirmed.evidence: Docstring: "{oa_paper_id, title, authors (str), year, published_date, doi, abstract (reconstructed), cited_by_count, concepts (list[str]), primary_category (None — OA doesn't have arxiv categories)}" vs the actual return dict (lines 618-629) which has no 'primary_category' key and adds 'arxiv_id'. Module docstring line 13: 'oa_concepts ← top 3 concept display_names' vs concepts[:5] at lines 149 and 615 (canon_ingest joins all 5 into oa_concepts while enrich-via-oa stores [:3], so the DB column mixes 3- and 5-name rows).
confirmed.failure_scenario: A future caller trusts the docstring and does work["primary_category"] -> KeyError (today's only consumer, canon_ingest.py:136, happens to hard-code primary_category=None); analyses splitting oa_concepts by comma see systematically longer concept lists for source_kind='openalex' rows than for arxiv rows.
confirmed.suggestion: Fix the docstring, document arxiv_id, and standardize the concept count between the two writers of oa_concepts.
confirmed.verify_notes:
Tried to refute; every element of the claim holds on direct reading plus an offline repro with a mocked HTTP layer.

(1) Docstring vs return dict: /home/user/financial-abm-lab/packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/openalex.py lines 592-596 promise the returned dict contains "primary_category (None ...)". The actual return (lines 618-629) has keys {oa_paper_id, title, authors, year, published_date, doi, abstract, cited_by_count, concepts, arxiv_id} — no 'primary_category', and 'arxiv_id' (line 628, via _arxiv_id_from_work) is undocumented. Reproduced offline: monkeypatched openalex._http_get_json with a fake work, called fetch_work_full → 'primary_category' in work == False, work["primary_category"] raises KeyError, 'arxiv_id' in work == True.

(2) Module docstring line 13 says "oa_concepts ← top 3 concept display_names" but both _normalise_work (line 149) and fetch_work_full (line 615) slice concepts[:5]. Reproduced: 8 fake concepts → both return 5.

(3) The 3-vs-5 DB mixing is real: cmd_enrich_via_oa (arxiv_cli.py line 1462) stores ", ".join(paper["concepts"][:3]) for arxiv rows, while canon_ingest.py line 148 stores ", ".join(work.get("concepts") or []) — all 5 — for source_kind='openalex' rows. So the same oa_concepts column holds up to 3 names for arxiv-enriched rows and up to 5 for canon-ingested rows; consumers that split on comma (literature_map.py:60,122; coverage.py:69; gap_finder.py:109,246; gap_propose.py:303) see systematically longer lists for openalex rows, exactly as claimed.

(4) No neutralizing guard needed or present: the only caller of fetch_work_full is canon_ingest.py:96, and it hard-codes primary_category=None at line 136, so there is no runtime failure today — which the claim itself already states. The failure scenario (future caller trusting the docstring → KeyError) is accurately framed as latent.

Severity: 'info' is honest. Pure documentation drift plus a mild cross-source concept-count inconsistency; nothing currently crashes and no research output is corrupted (concepts are used only as fuzzy tag haystacks, where 5-vs-3 names slightly increases match surface for openalex rows but does not produce wrong values). Not worth upgrading to minor since the only concrete failure requires a hypothetical new caller.

confirmed.summary: The min_relevance_to_keep drop path stores nothing at all — contradicting its own comment '(do NOT delete row; mark with low score)' — so every re-run re-pays the LLM extraction for the same rejected papers, and cmd_extract_untagged later re-extracts and stores them unconditionally, silently bypassing the relevance gate.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 554-561: `# apply relevance filter (do NOT delete row; mark with low score)` followed by `if (ext["relevance_score"] is not None and ext["relevance_score"] < min_relevance_to_keep): n_dropped += 1; ... continue` — the extraction result including the relevance score is discarded; neither update_literature_extraction nor record_extraction_attempt is called, leaving extracted_by_model NULL. The next ingest's `already_extracted` check (line 538-543) keys on extracted_by_model, so the paper is re-sent to the LLM every run. arxiv_cli.py cmd_extract_untagged (lines 559-563, 643-652) explicitly targets rows with extracted_by_model NULL ('any rows that skipped extract=True' per its docstring) and stores whatever extraction comes back with no relevance filter.
confirmed.failure_scenario: .github/workflows/ingest_arxiv.yml:125 runs scheduled ingests with `--min-relevance-to-keep 0.3`. A preset whose 50-result window contains 15 sub-0.3 papers burns 15 LLM calls (and 15×~2k tokens of TPM budget) on every scheduled run, forever, since nothing marks them. Separately, one maintenance run of `extract-untagged` fully extracts and stores those same below-threshold papers, so they enter the coverage matrix/atlas the gate was supposed to keep them out of — and note ext['relevance_score'] is None also bypasses the gate entirely (line 556).
confirmed.suggestion: Store the extraction (or at minimum the relevance_score + extracted_by_model) for below-threshold papers and filter at read time (load_literature already supports min_relevance), or bump record_extraction_attempt and teach extract-untagged to respect a relevance threshold. Fix the comment either way.
confirmed.verify_notes:
Checked: full ingest() function (packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/arxiv_ingest.py:459-593), cmd_extract_untagged (arxiv_cli.py:509-665), db.py (record_extraction_attempt:316, update_literature_extraction:346, load_literature:368), .github/workflows/ingest_arxiv.yml, query_arxiv sort behavior, downstream consumers (coverage, literature_map, propose), git history of the comment, and tests/. Then ran an offline runtime repro (temp SQLite, stubbed extractor, no network).
