Repro results (script in scratchpad, ran clean): (1) Two ingest() runs over the same paper with stub relevance 0.2 and min_relevance_to_keep=0.3 called the extractor TWICE (n_dropped_below_relevance=1 both runs, n_skipped_already_extracted=0), and the row ended with extracted_by_model=NULL, relevance_score=NULL — the drop path at arxiv_ingest.py:554-561 just `continue`s, calling neither update_literature_extraction nor record_extraction_attempt, directly contradicting its own comment '(do NOT delete row; mark with low score)'. The already_extracted skip (lines 538-543) keys on extracted_by_model, so the LLM cost is re-paid every run. (2) cmd_extract_untagged then selected that row (its filter at arxiv_cli.py:559-563 is extracted_by_model NULL + usable abstract + attempts<3; the dropped row matches all three) and stored the full extraction with relevance_score=0.2 via update_literature_extraction (lines 643-652) — no relevance check anywhere in that path. Row now carries extracted_by_model='stub-model', rel=0.2, tags.

Corroborating context: ingest_arxiv.yml line 125 hardcodes `--min-relevance-to-keep 0.3` on the weekly scheduled run (cron '0 21 * * 0', preset financial_abm_strict, max 50); query_arxiv (arxiv_ingest.py:332-346) defaults to SubmittedDate descending with no date restriction, so rejected papers remain in the 50-result window across many consecutive weekly runs. cmd_coverage (arxiv_cli.py:428) and literature_map load literature with NO relevance filter, so extract-untagged-stored sub-threshold papers do enter the coverage matrix; propose.py only partially protects (drops rel=None, ranks by rel). The rel-is-None bypass at line 555 is also real as stated (explicit `is not None` guard), though that half may be an intentional don't-drop-on-missing-score choice. Git history (commit 7a31f10) shows the comment/code contradiction existed from day one — not a regression. No test covers the min_relevance_to_keep drop path (zero grep hits in tests/), so nothing codifies the current behavior as intended.

Refutation attempts that failed: record_extraction_attempt exists but is never called from ingest(), and ingest's skip check ignores extraction_attempts anyway; no workflow runs extract-untagged automatically (manual maintenance only, matching the claim's phrasing); no downstream guard neutralizes the coverage-matrix contamination. Only overstatement in the claim: 'forever' — a rejected paper ages out of the top-50 submittedDate window as newer submissions accumulate, so the repeated cost lasts weeks-to-months per paper, not literally forever. That does not change the finding. Severity major is correct: recurring wasted LLM quota on every scheduled ingest plus a silently bypassable relevance gate is a broken feature in realistic use; not critical because coverage contamination needs a manual extract-untagged run and propose partially filters by score.

confirmed.summary: TPM-vs-TPD 429 classification depends on exact Groq message substrings ('tokens per day'/'TPD'); Groq's requests-per-day (RPD) and requests-per-minute (RPM) 429s match neither, so daily request-quota exhaustion is misclassified as TPM-recoverable and burns 2×65s sleeps per paper for the rest of the run.
confirmed.evidence: `tpd = "tokens per day" in msg or "TPD" in msg` followed by `if attempt < max_retries and rate_limited: ... time.sleep(65)`. rate_limited matches any 'rate_limit_exceeded'/'429' message, so an RPD 429 ('... on requests per day (RPD) ...') falls through to the TPM branch even though it cannot recover within 65s. The classification also breaks silently if Groq rewords its message (the comment at 389-391 admits classification should be 'by explicit error CODE, not message keywords' — but these are still message keywords).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Groq free-tier RPD (e.g. 1k requests/day) is exhausted mid-ingest of 100 papers: each remaining paper sleeps 65s twice before erroring — ~3.6 hours of pointless sleeping in a CI job instead of the immediate bail the TPD branch performs.
confirmed.suggestion: Match '(TPD)'/'(RPD)'/'per day' together for the bail-out branch, or parse Groq's structured error type/code and the 'Please try again in Xs' hint for the sleep duration.
confirmed.verify_notes: Verified against packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/arxiv_ingest.py lines 363-416 plus caller ingest() lines 546-552 and llm_client.py. (1) Code matches claim: tpd classification at line 404 requires 'tokens per day'/'TPD' substrings; Groq RPD/RPM 429 bodies ('Rate limit reached ... on requests per day (RPD): ...', code rate_limit_exceeded) match none of them but do match the rate_limited markers ('Rate limit reached'/'rate_limit_exceeded'/'429'), so they take the 65s-sleep TPM branch. (2) No neutralizing guards: call_llm is invoked with max_retries=0 (bypassing llm_client's own retry table); ingest() catches each paper's exception and continues with no circuit breaker, so exhausted RPD repeats the penalty per paper; arxiv_cli.py:662's TPD hint is post-run stderr text only. CI runs this path (.github/workflows/ingest_arxiv.yml). (3) Offline repro (monkeypatched call_llm raising realistic Groq 429 strings, monkeypatched time.sleep): RPD and RPM each cost 3 failed calls + sleeps [65,65] = 130s before raising; TPD bails immediately with 0 sleeps; 100 remaining papers = 3.6h of pointless sleeping, exactly the stated scenario. The auditor's aside is also correct: the comment at lines 387-390 claims classification 'by explicit error CODE, not message keywords' while lines 395/404 remain message-keyword substring checks, so the branch silently degrades if Groq rewords. Severity minor is right: edge condition (daily request quota exhausted mid-run), consequence is wasted wall-clock time only — no data corruption, errors recorded, reruns skip extracted papers.

confirmed.summary: A client-side json.JSONDecodeError from json.loads(content) matches no transient marker, so malformed/truncated JSON bodies are never retried — inconsistent with the module contract that retries the analogous shape errors (JSON-list, non-object, empty choices) and Groq's server-side json_validate_failed.
confirmed.evidence: `parsed = json.loads(content)` inside _normalize_json_response; _TRANSIENT_MARKERS (lines 76-83) contains 'json_validate_failed', 'JSON list instead of an object', 'non-object JSON' etc., but JSONDecodeError messages are 'Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)' / 'Unterminated string ...', which match nothing, so _call_groq/_call_openai raise on the first attempt while a same-shape server-reported failure would be retried with temperature jitter.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A response truncated at the token limit (finish_reason='length') or a provider hiccup yields invalid JSON; the call fails immediately even with max_retries=2, whereas a retry with jitter (the exact remedy the module was built for) would likely succeed.
confirmed.suggestion: Add 'Expecting value'/'Unterminated'/'JSONDecodeError' to _TRANSIENT_MARKERS, or catch json.JSONDecodeError in _normalize_json_response and re-raise a RuntimeError containing an existing transient marker.
confirmed.verify_notes: Reproduced offline with a stubbed groq SDK: a malformed JSON body ('{"a": "trunc...') raised json.JSONDecodeError from json.loads(content) at llm_client.py:58 after exactly 1 attempt despite max_retries=2, while the same-shape server-visible failure ('[1,2,3]' -> "JSON list instead of an object") was retried and succeeded on attempt 2. _is_transient() returns False for both canonical decode messages ("Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)", "Unterminated string starting at: ...") because no _TRANSIENT_MARKERS entry (lines 76-83) matches them. No guard neutralizes it: the one caller with its own retry loop, arxiv_ingest._call_groq_for_extraction (lines 374-415), passes max_retries=0 to call_llm and only retries rate-limit-marked messages (rate_limit_exceeded / "Rate limit reached" / 429), so JSONDecodeError re-raises there too; all other callers (gap_propose, propose, idea_judge, idea_plan, methods_draft) delegate directly with no outer retry. Severity minor is right: with response_format=json_object, Groq surfaces invalid JSON as the server-side json_validate_failed 400 (which IS retried), so the client-side decode path realistically fires only on OpenAI truncation at the token limit (finish_reason='length') or provider hiccups; when it fires, the ingest loop skips the paper (arxiv_ingest.py 547-552) and other CLI paths fail one re-runnable command — latent/edge, no data corruption.

confirmed.summary: _call_groq_for_extraction passes max_retries=0 to call_llm, disabling the generic transient retry (json_validate_failed, empty choices, JSON-list) entirely for the extraction path, while its own loop only retries rate-limit-classified messages — contrary to its docstring's framing that the 65s backoff is 'more aggressive than' (i.e. in addition to) the generic retry.
confirmed.evidence: `return call_llm(EXTRACTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT, paper_payload, model, temperature=temperature, max_retries=0, generate_japanese=False)` and the outer classifier `rate_limited = ("rate_limit_exceeded" in msg or "Rate limit reached" in msg or "429" in msg)` — a Groq 'json_validate_failed' (a documented-transient error per llm_client's own marker list) matches neither, so `raise` fires with zero retries.
confirmed.failure_scenario: During a 100-paper ingest, one call returns json_validate_failed (common with structured output on open-weight models): the paper is logged to `errors`, cmd_ingest exits 1 (failing the CI step) even though a single retry would almost certainly have succeeded. The row stays un-extracted until a later run picks it up.
confirmed.suggestion: Use max_retries=1 in the call_llm call (the outer wrapper's unrecoverable/TPD checks still preempt hopeless cases), or replicate the transient-shape retry in the outer loop.
confirmed.verify_notes:
Checked: (1) read _call_groq_for_extraction in full (packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/arxiv_ingest.py:363-416) and llm_client.py in full; (2) traced callers extract_paper_structured -> ingest -> arxiv_cli.cmd_ingest and the CI workflow .github/workflows/ingest_arxiv.yml; (3) ran an offline monkeypatch repro; (4) checked git line-history for a neutralizing rationale.

Mechanics confirmed: call_llm is invoked with max_retries=0 (line 381-383), so llm_client's generic transient retry (whose _TRANSIENT_MARKERS at llm_client.py:76-83 explicitly include "json_validate_failed") never fires. The outer loop's only retry branch requires rate_limited = ("rate_limit_exceeded" in msg or "Rate limit reached" in msg or "429" in msg) (line 395-397, 410); a Groq json_validate_failed is HTTP 400 and matches none, and is not caught by the unrecoverable/TPD branches, so `raise` at line 415 fires on attempt 0.

Reproduced offline (project .venv, monkeypatched call_llm to fail once with the Groq 400 json_validate_failed message then succeed; code_links.resolve_code_url stubbed to stay offline): llm_client._is_transient(msg) returned True, yet _call_groq_for_extraction made exactly ONE call_llm invocation (max_retries passed = 0) and raised — the retry that would have succeeded never happened. Through ingest(): n_extracted=0, n_errors=1, and cmd_ingest returns 1 (arxiv_cli.py:64). The CI step "Ingest new arxiv papers" in ingest_arxiv.yml has no continue-on-error, so exit 1 fails the workflow AND skips the subsequent snapshot-dump/PR steps, discarding that run's successful extractions (the runner DB is ephemeral) — slightly worse than the claim states.

Git history refutes the "intentional design" defense: before refactor commit 8cba812, this function's own docstring said "Retries on the 'json_validate_failed' 400 — same gpt-oss-120b JSON-mode quirk as propose._call_groq" and its except block had `if attempt < max_retries and transient_json: temperature += 0.1; continue`. The refactor to call_llm(max_retries=0) silently dropped that branch while keeping only the 429 sleep — a behavioral regression, and inconsistent with every sibling caller (propose.py, methods_draft.py, idea_judge.py, gap_propose.py all pass max_retries=2). The auditor's docstring reading ("more aggressive than" implying in-addition-to the generic retry) matches the historical intent. json_validate_failed is realistic on this path: the workflow documents Groq routing (openai/gpt-oss-120b) as a supported model input, and propose.py:42 documents the error as a known gpt-oss-120b JSON-mode quirk.

Severity stays minor: operational/latent — a weekly cron job fails and rows stay unextracted until a later idempotent re-run; no data corruption or wrong research results.

confirmed.summary: ingest()'s guard around resolve_code_url catches only (OSError, ValueError, KeyError, TypeError), but code_links._http_get_json can leak http.client.HTTPException subclasses (IncompleteRead during resp.read(), BadStatusLine from urlopen), which would abort the entire ingest loop mid-run.
confirmed.evidence: `except (OSError, ValueError, KeyError, TypeError) as e:` at arxiv_ingest.py:524, while code_links.py:164-166 catches `(urllib.error.URLError, urllib.error.HTTPError, TimeoutError, json.JSONDecodeError, OSError)` — http.client.IncompleteRead/BadStatusLine inherit HTTPException (not OSError) and escape both nets; urllib's do_open only wraps OSError into URLError, letting getresponse()-stage HTTPExceptions propagate raw.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Papers with Code returns a truncated chunked response for paper 12 of 50: IncompleteRead propagates through resolve_code_url past ingest()'s catch, the run dies, and papers 13-50 get neither metadata nor extraction in that run (summary/errors never printed).
confirmed.suggestion: Broaden the code_url guard to `except Exception` (it is explicitly a best-effort side quest), or add http.client.HTTPException to _http_get_json's catch tuple.
confirmed.verify_notes: Checked: (1) full ingest() body (arxiv_ingest.py:459-593) and its callers (arxiv_cli.py cmd_ingest:52, cmd_ingest_ids:89, ingest_by_ids:611, cmd_canon --auto-ingest:1118) plus main() dispatch (arxiv_cli.py:2362) — no try/except anywhere above ingest(), so an escaping exception kills the CLI before the summary prints; (2) code_links._http_get_json (code_links.py:156-166) and resolve_code_url chain; (3) exception hierarchy on the environment's Python 3.11: http.client.IncompleteRead and BadStatusLine subclass HTTPException only (not OSError), while RemoteDisconnected IS an OSError (the auditor correctly excluded it). Reproduced offline with a loopback socket server (no repo modification, no external network): truncated Content-Length body and truncated chunked body both leak IncompleteRead out of _http_get_json (its catch tuple URLError/HTTPError/TimeoutError/JSONDecodeError/OSError misses HTTPException); a garbage status line leaks BadStatusLine raw from urlopen (urllib's do_open wraps only OSError from the send phase into URLError, not getresponse()-stage HTTPExceptions). The full chain resolve_code_url(arxiv_id, None, None) against the truncating server escaped the exact except tuple at arxiv_ingest.py:524 (OSError, ValueError, KeyError, TypeError). Control case (server closes with zero bytes → RemoteDisconnected) is correctly swallowed, confirming only the HTTPException-not-OSError subset escapes. Guard elsewhere: cmd_backfill_code wraps the same resolve_code_url in `except Exception` (arxiv_cli.py:254), so backfill is safe, but the ingest path is not. Severity minor is right: requires a rare malformed/truncated response from the PWC endpoint on the fallback path only; papers before the crash are persisted and re-run is idempotent (no data corruption), but the run aborts mid-loop with a traceback and remaining papers get neither metadata nor extraction that run, exactly as the failure_scenario states.

confirmed.summary: ingest() reloads the entire literature_methods table (including a full ensure_literature_schema executescript + index creation + ALTER sweep) once per paper inside the loop, making ingestion O(papers x corpus).
confirmed.evidence: `for p in papers:` then `before_rows = load_literature(db_path)` at line 493; db.load_literature (db.py:368-421) unconditionally calls ensure_literature_schema and fetches every row, building a 32-key dict per row — repeated for each of up to --max 200 papers just to answer three point lookups (had_id, existing_code_url, already_extracted).
confirmed.failure_scenario: A 5,000-row corpus and --max 200: 200 full-table loads = 1M dict constructions plus 200 schema/executescript/commit round-trips per ingest run; grows quadratically as the corpus and preset count expand, dominating the non-LLM portion of scheduled CI runs.
confirmed.suggestion: Load once before the loop and maintain the in-memory index across iterations, or replace the three checks with indexed point SELECTs on arxiv_id.
confirmed.verify_notes: Verified by reading full context and by offline repro. (1) Structure: arxiv_ingest.py:491-493 calls load_literature(db_path) inside `for p in papers:`; db.py:368-421 confirms load_literature unconditionally runs ensure_literature_schema (executescript + 3 CREATE INDEX + PRAGMA column sweep + commit, db.py:251-278) and builds a 32-key dict per row; before_rows is used only for three point lookups (had_id line 494, existing_code_url 514-518, already_extracted 538-541). No guard or cache neutralizes it. (2) Repro in mktemp -d with a 5,000-row temp DB: 200 x load_literature = 11.9s (~60ms/call) vs 54ms for one hoisted load — ~12s of redundant work per run, exactly the claimed O(papers x corpus); isolated ensure_literature_schema is only 0.04s/200 calls, so the claim slightly overweights the schema round-trips but they do occur. (3) Scale caveats consistent with 'minor': the live DB at /home/user/test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db has 0 literature_methods rows and CI (ingest_arxiv.yml) defaults MAX=50, so the 5,000x200 scenario is hypothetical; per-paper PWC HTTP lookups and arxiv 3s page delays rival the waste at realistic scale, so 'dominating the non-LLM portion' holds only asymptotically. No incorrect results are produced (the per-iteration reload even handles duplicate arxiv_ids within a batch; a per-paper point SELECT would preserve that in O(1)). Real latent performance defect, severity minor as filed.

confirmed.summary: The volume_volatility_corr preset embeds an unparenthesized AND inside an OR chain ('abs:"trading volume" AND abs:"volatility clustering"'), relying on undocumented arxiv-API operator precedence — every other preset parenthesizes such groups explicitly.
confirmed.evidence: `'abs:"volume volatility" OR abs:"volume-volatility" OR abs:"trading volume" AND abs:"volatility clustering" OR abs:"activity-volume" OR abs:"MDH" ...'` — compare prospect_theory_returns (lines 145-149) and llm_agent_stylized_facts (132-137), which wrap every AND-joined group in parentheses. arxiv's API docs do not specify AND/OR precedence; under left-to-right evaluation the AND would bind the whole preceding OR chain to 'volatility clustering', excluding most 'volume volatility' papers. (Network egress to export.arxiv.org timed out in this environment, so the live count comparison could not be run.)
confirmed.failure_scenario: If the arxiv parser evaluates left-to-right, the preset returns a much narrower (or differently-shaped) set than intended, and the volume-volatility coverage cell this preset was created to fill stays sparsely populated while appearing 'swept'.
confirmed.suggestion: Parenthesize: `... OR (abs:"trading volume" AND abs:"volatility clustering") OR ...`, matching the house style of the other presets.
confirmed.verify_notes: Checked: (1) read the full DEFAULT_QUERIES dict in packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/arxiv_ingest.py — the volume_volatility_corr preset (lines 151-160) does embed an unparenthesized 'OR abs:"trading volume" AND abs:"volatility clustering" OR' while all sibling presets parenthesize AND groups, as claimed; (2) traced the caller path (arxiv_cli.py:42-47 → query_arxiv() at arxiv_ingest.py:332 → arxiv.Search) — the query string is passed verbatim, no guard or normalization; (3) network to export.arxiv.org works in this environment, so I ran the live comparison the auditor could not. Results (stable over 3 rounds): preset as written = 16 hits; explicitly parenthesized intended form = 22; auditor's left-to-right form = 11. The as-written count and ID set exactly match a fourth grouping, A OR B OR (C AND (D OR E OR F OR G)) — arxiv's parser makes the unparenthesized AND right-greedy over the rest of the OR chain, so abs:"activity-volume"/"MDH"/"mixture of distributions hypothesis" cease to be independent alternatives and must co-occur with "trading volume". The as-written results are a strict subset of the intended set, silently missing 6/22 papers (arxiv 1304.6006, 1409.0118, 1511.08997, 2004.00550, 2107.05923, 2201.03213 — realized-volatility/information-flow/MDH papers, precisely the literature the preset's comment says it targets). Verdict reasoning: the auditor's summary (undocumented-precedence reliance, inconsistent with sibling presets) is correct, and the hedged failure scenario ("differently-shaped set than intended, coverage cell under-populated while appearing swept") is empirically real — though the specific left-to-right mechanism is refuted (a simple discriminator query showed A OR B AND C == A OR (B AND C), and the preset behaves as right-greedy AND, dropping the MDH tail rather than the volume-volatility phrases). Since the defect is no longer speculative but a demonstrated, deterministic 27% recall loss on every run of this preset, info understates it; it is a real wrong-result in realistic use but limited to degraded literature-sweep recall in one preset (no data corruption, ingested papers remain valid), so minor is the honest severity. Reproduced via live arxiv API counts + ID diffs (read-only HTTP, no repo modification).

confirmed.summary: Paper identity is solely the raw arxiv_id string (UNIQUE) with no DOI/title dedup anywhere, so the same paper can exist twice as an arxiv row plus an 'oa:W' synthetic row whenever OpenAlex does not link the journal work to its arxiv preprint.
confirmed.evidence: `arxiv_id TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE, -- e.g. '2412.01234' or '2412.01234v2'` is the only identity column; literature_methods stores no DOI. canon_ingest.py:89 dedups only on the synthetic id itself (`if synthetic in seen: skip`), and canon_atlas.build_atlas's dual match (canon_atlas.py:119-122) relies on OpenAlex reporting the paper's arxiv_id or on oa_paper_id having been backfilled onto the arxiv row — neither holds when OA indexes the journal version as a separate unlinked work.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Cont 2001-style paper exists in DB via arxiv ingest ('cond-mat/...'). OpenAlex's journal-version work W-id has no arxiv location and the arxiv row's oa_paper_id is NULL (OA enrichment not run). canon-atlas marks it not-in-DB, missing_canon routes it to ingest_canon_via_oa, which inserts a second row 'oa:Wxxxx' with the same title/DOI. literature_map, coverage counts and LLM corpus context then double-count the paper, and the atlas coverage percentage is inflated.
confirmed.suggestion: Add a doi column (OpenAlex provides it, canon_atlas already carries p['doi']) plus a normalized-title index, and check both before inserting an oa: synthetic row.
confirmed.verify_notes: Verified against packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/{db.py,canon_ingest.py,canon_atlas.py,arxiv_cli.py}. arxiv_id UNIQUE (db.py:220) is the sole paper identity: no DOI column in literature_methods (schema + ALTER migrations db.py:263-277), upsert_literature_metadata dedups only on exact arxiv_id (db.py:299-304), and ingest_canon_via_oa dedups only on the synthetic 'oa:W' id (canon_ingest.py:82-94). build_atlas's dual match (canon_atlas.py:117-122) depends on OA reporting the arxiv location OR db_oa_ids containing the work id; cmd_canon_atlas (arxiv_cli.py:1299-1306) fills db_oa_ids only from the oa_paper_id column, which is NULL on arxiv rows unless the separate oa-enrich command ran (arxiv_cli.py:1460-1484). No title/DOI dedup exists anywhere (only arxiv-version normalization at arxiv_cli.py:908ff and a genealogy-rendering title merge that never touches the DB). Reproduced offline in a temp SQLite DB with monkeypatched OA fetchers (scratchpad/repro_dup.py): arxiv row 'cond-mat/0105191' with NULL oa_paper_id + canon work W2145023731 with arxiv_id=None -> build_atlas in_db=False, missing_canon routes to oa_only, ingest_canon_via_oa added=1, DB ends with two rows (arxiv + oa:W) for the identical title; load_literature returns both, so literature_map/coverage/LLM corpus double-count. Severity minor is right: requires an OpenAlex data gap (journal work unlinked to preprint) plus no enrichment (or split OA works) plus the opt-in --auto-ingest-missing flow; harm is duplicate corpus rows/count inflation, not corrupted conclusions. One claim nuance: post-ingest coverage is not really 'inflated' (the paper genuinely is in DB afterward) — the pre-ingest coverage is understated and the durable harm is the duplicate row; this does not change the verdict.

confirmed.summary: ensure_code_snapshot_schema is the only ensure_* function that does not create the parent directory, so it fails with 'unable to open database file' if it is the first call against a fresh path.
confirmed.evidence: `def ensure_code_snapshot_schema(db_path):\n    with sqlite3.connect(db_path) as con:` — every sibling (ensure_runs_schema:73-74, ensure_proposals_schema:148-149, ensure_literature_schema:253-254, ensure_ideas_schema:606-607) does `os.makedirs(parent, exist_ok=True)` first.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A script calls upsert_code_snapshot (which calls ensure_code_snapshot_schema first, line 447) with db_path='newdir/atlas.db' before any other ensure_* has run -> sqlite3.OperationalError instead of creating the DB, unlike every other entry point.
confirmed.suggestion: Add the same makedirs guard.
confirmed.verify_notes: Checked /home/user/financial-abm-lab/packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/db.py: ensure_code_snapshot_schema (lines 438-441) goes straight to sqlite3.connect(db_path) with no os.makedirs, while every sibling ensure_* does `parent = os.path.dirname(db_path) or "."; os.makedirs(parent, exist_ok=True)` first (ensure_runs_schema 73-74, ensure_proposals_schema 148-149, ensure_literature_schema 253-254, ensure_ideas_schema 606-607). Reproduced offline in mktemp -d: calling upsert_code_snapshot (which calls ensure_code_snapshot_schema at line 447) with db_path='newdir/atlas.db' on a fresh path raises sqlite3.OperationalError: unable to open database file, exactly as claimed; running ensure_literature_schema first succeeds. Guards elsewhere: both in-repo call paths are shielded — arxiv_cli.py cmd_fetch_code_snapshots calls ensure_literature_schema(args.db) at line 173 before load_code_snapshots/upsert_code_snapshot, and idea_plan.py make_plan (line 153) only runs against a DB already populated with literature matches — so no shipped code path hits the failure; it only bites external/direct API users on a fresh path. That makes it a latent API-consistency defect, which matches the filed severity of info. Claim, evidence, line numbers, and failure scenario are all accurate as stated.

confirmed.summary: _citation_count mixes OpenAlex total citations with Semantic Scholar *influential* citation counts on one scale, and exports both under the CSV column name 'cited_by_count'.
confirmed.evidence: for k in ("oa_cited_by_count", "s2_influential_citation_count"): ... return int(v) — influential-citation counts are typically an order of magnitude smaller than total cited_by counts, yet both feed marker size (sizes = 20 + 6 * math.log(1 + _citation_count(r))), the 'K most-cited' label selection, and the CSV column labeled cited_by_count.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A landmark paper enriched only via Semantic Scholar (OA fetch failed/404) shows influential_citation_count=40 vs another paper's oa_cited_by_count=400; the top-labels selection and dot sizes systematically demote S2-only papers, and downstream consumers of literature_map.csv treat the mixed column as one metric.
confirmed.suggestion: Fall back to a comparable S2 field (total citationCount) if stored, or emit a source column alongside the count.
confirmed.verify_notes: Checked _citation_count in full context (literature_map.py:127-136) plus all callers and data-population paths. (1) The two fields are genuinely incommensurable: semantic_scholar.py documents influentialCitationCount as "S2's curated impact metric" (a small curated subset of total citations); fetch_paper also returns S2's total citation_count but cmd_enrich_via_s2 (arxiv_cli.py:384-390) persists only the influential count and db.py:270 has no S2-total column, so the fallback cannot be a comparable total. (2) The S2-only state is reachable by design: cmd_enrich_via_oa (arxiv_cli.py:1476-1482) writes oa_cited_by_count=None on an OpenAlex miss and stamps oa_fetched_at so re-runs skip the row, while S2 enrichment independently sets s2_influential_citation_count; load_literature returns both keys to render_literature_map via cmd_atlas. (3) Reproduced offline with the repo's actual functions: for a row {oa_cited_by_count: None, s2_influential_citation_count: 40} vs {oa_cited_by_count: 400}, _citation_count returned 40 vs 400, marker sizes 42.3 vs 56.0, top-labels sort demotes the S2-only paper, and the CSV wrote 40 and 400 under the single 'cited_by_count' header (also confirmed OA is preferred when present — the second row's s2 value 500 was ignored). The live DB at /home/user/test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db has an empty literature_methods table, so no in-the-wild mixed rows were observable, but the code path is unconditional. Severity: impact is limited to log-scaled dot sizes, label selection in a PNG, and a human-consumed CSV artifact (atlas_refresh.yml uploads it; nothing in-repo machine-reads its cited_by_count column — gap_propose.py reads oa_cited_by_count from the DB directly). No stored data or research conclusions corrupted; the fallback is even documented as intentional ("Pull whichever citation count is present"). info is the correct severity as filed.

confirmed.summary: gap_mine proposals cannot be executed: the module docstring promises downstream can 'list / execute it like any other proposal', but propose_cli.execute_proposal hard-rejects any proposal_type != 'param_sweep', and even if it didn't, the '_gap' key injected into params would be passed to the model constructor.
confirmed.evidence: gap_propose.py docstring: "inserted into the `proposals` table with `proposal_type='gap_mine'` so downstream (propose CLI, dashboard) can list / execute it like any other proposal." propose_cli.py:265-268: `if p["proposal_type"] != "param_sweep": raise ValueError(f"executor only knows param_sweep; got {p['proposal_type']!r}")`. insert_gap_proposal (gap_propose.py:267-269) does `params.setdefault("_gap", proposal["_gap"])` with the comment 'the executor sees full context; downstream filters can read params[_gap] if needed' — but execute_proposal never strips it before `build_model(p["target_model"], p["params"])` -> `cls(**p)` (adapters.py:175); only idea_cli's promote path filters `k != "_gap"`.
confirmed.failure_scenario: User runs `gap-propose` (proposals inserted as gap_mine, predicted_fingerprint mandatory precisely so prediction_error can be computed), then `propose_cli execute --id N` -> ValueError, exit 1. The whole predicted-vs-actual fingerprint loop that gap_propose's validation is built around (mandatory 9-component predicted_fingerprint) is unreachable; the only working path is promote-to-idea, which never runs the model.
confirmed.suggestion: Either accept proposal_type='gap_mine' in execute_proposal (stripping '_gap' from params before build_model), or fix the docstring and stop persisting predicted_fingerprint as 'mandatory' for a proposal type that can never be executed.
confirmed.verify_notes:
Checked all three legs of the claim against source and reproduced the failure offline with a temp SQLite DB (no network/LLM).

1) Docstring promise: gap_propose.py:10-12 says gap_mine rows are inserted "so downstream (propose CLI, dashboard) can list / execute it like any other proposal" — verbatim as cited.

2) Hard rejection: propose_cli.py:265-268 raises ValueError for any proposal_type != "param_sweep". Repro: inserted a proposal via gap_propose.insert_gap_proposal (status defaults to 'proposed' per db.py:129, so it passes the status gate at propose_cli.py:261 and hits the type gate) → execute_proposal raised "ValueError: executor only knows param_sweep; got 'gap_mine'" and cmd_execute returned exit code 1 — exactly the stated failure scenario. Grepped the whole repo: propose_cli.execute_proposal is the ONLY executor (callers: cmd_execute, cmd_auto, idea_cli cmd_run's auto-execute which is itself gated on impl == "param_sweep"); no workflow (.github/workflows) or dashboard path executes gap_mine rows.

3) Secondary '_gap' sub-claim: insert_gap_proposal (gap_propose.py:267-269) injects "_gap" into params; adapters.build_model (line 175) does cls(**p) without filtering. Repro: build_model("zero_intelligence", params_with_gap) → TypeError: ZeroIntelligence.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument '_gap'; same params without '_gap' build fine. So even removing the type gate wouldn't make these rows runnable as stored. Only idea_cli.cmd_promote_proposal (idea_cli.py:274-275) filters k != "_gap".

4) Unreachable prediction loop: propose_from_gap makes the 9-component predicted_fingerprint mandatory (gap_propose.py:214-221), and gap_propose.py:328-329 comments "Only surface families that actually have a registry impl ... so the LLM can't pick targets the executor can't run" — i.e., executor compatibility was the design intent, not just stale docs. The only working downstream is promote-to-idea → plan → scaffold_param_sweep (idea_plan.py:202-243), which inserts a NEW param_sweep proposal from a fresh LLM plan; the gap proposal's own predicted_fingerprint never gets a prediction_error computed, so the predicted-vs-actual loop for gap_mine rows is indeed unreachable.

Minor nit only: the CLI syntax is `execute <id>` (positional), not `execute --id N` — immaterial to the finding. Severity 'major' is right: a documented realistic flow (arxiv_cli gap-propose → propose_cli execute) fails loudly with exit 1 and a core feature (gap-proposal prediction scoring) is dead; not 'critical' since no data is corrupted and the error is immediate and visible, with list/dashboard/promote unaffected.

confirmed.summary: Param validation checks only that keys are in model_bounds, never that values lie within the advertised ranges, although the prompt tells the LLM ranges will be enforced ('範囲も尊重', '違反すると validate で reject される').
confirmed.evidence: `allowed = set(model_bounds[target].keys()); bad = [k for k in params if k not in allowed]` — the (lo, hi) tuples shown to the LLM as params_allowed are never compared against the proposed values. Non-numeric values (e.g. "N": "large") also pass.
confirmed.failure_scenario: LLM returns {"N": 500000, "T": 10**7} for minority_game — both keys allowed, values far outside (51,151)/(2000,3000) — proposal validates and persists; anything that later runs it (executor once the gap_mine gate is fixed, or code scaffolded from the promoted idea) hangs or produces runs incomparable with the LHS-swept atlas.
confirmed.suggestion: Clamp or reject numeric params outside bounds in propose_from_gap.
confirmed.verify_notes: Checked: (1) Read gap_propose.py in full. The validation block at /home/user/financial-abm-lab/packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/gap_propose.py:206-213 does exactly what the claim says: `allowed = set(model_bounds[target].keys()); bad = [k for k in params if k not in allowed]` — key membership only; the (lo, hi) tuples serialized into the prompt via _summarise_families (lines 100-103, "param key と範囲") are never compared against proposed values, and value types are never checked. The prompt does advertise ranges: line 50 '範囲も尊重' and line 60 header '絶対に守る制約 (違反すると validate で reject される)'. One small nuance: the enumerated reject-list item 1 under that header mentions only keys, so 'ranges will be enforced' is a mild overstatement of the prompt's literal promise — but the summary's factual core (values never range-checked despite ranges being advertised) is exact. (2) Guards elsewhere: none neutralize it. insert_gap_proposal/db.insert_proposal (gap_propose.py:257-281, db.py) persist params with no bounds checks. execute_proposal (propose_cli.py:261-268) currently refuses non-param_sweep types, so direct execution of gap_mine is gated — consistent with the claim's own caveat. The promote path copies the invalid params verbatim into idea_text (idea_cli.py:273-277), scaffold_param_sweep (idea_plan.py:214-219) again checks only key completeness, and build_model (adapters.py:151+) instantiates with params as-is, no clamping. (3) Reproduced offline with dry_run_response (no LLM/network): the claim's exact payload {"N": 500000, "T": 10**7} for minority_game (bounds N=(51,151), T=(2000,3000)) validates and returns a persistable proposal; non-numeric {"N": "large"} also validates; control case with an unknown key ('memory_exponent') is correctly rejected, confirming only the key check fires. Severity: 'minor' is honest — the failure requires the LLM to disobey an explicit instruction, the direct executor path is currently gated, and the damage (persisted invalid proposal, later hang or atlas-incomparable run) is latent rather than corrupting existing results.

confirmed.summary: propose_from_top_gaps mixes error records ({'_gap':..., 'error':...}) into its return list while the docstring says it returns 'created proposal dicts'; the sole caller handles the 'error' key but its summary line counts failures as 'generated proposal candidate(s)', and insert failures print as '(dry)'.
confirmed.evidence: `except Exception as exc: created.append({"_gap": gap_dict, "error": str(exc)}); continue` and later `proposal["insert_error"] = str(exc)`. arxiv_cli.cmd_gap_propose: `print(f"generated {len(created)} proposal candidate(s)")` counts error rows; `id_str = f"#{pid}" if pid is not None else "(dry)"` renders a row whose insert failed (insert_error set, no id) as '(dry)' even in non-dry runs.
confirmed.failure_scenario: All 5 LLM calls fail (e.g. missing GROQ_API_KEY per-call) -> CLI prints 'generated 5 proposal candidate(s)' and exits 0; a DB insert failure in a real run is displayed as if it were an intentional dry-run.
confirmed.suggestion: Return (created, failed) or document the error shape; make the CLI count and exit-code reflect failures.
confirmed.verify_notes:
I set out to refute but the claim survives; I reproduced all three parts offline with a temp sqlite DB and the real cmd_gap_propose/propose_from_top_gaps code (only the LLM call and the insert boundary stubbed).

What I checked:
1. packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/gap_propose.py:316-360 — docstring says "Returns the list of created proposal dicts", but line 350 `created.append({"_gap": gap_dict, "error": str(exc)})` mixes pure error records into that list, and line 358 sets `proposal["insert_error"]` without setting `id`. Grep confirms `insert_error` is written once and NEVER read anywhere in the repo, so an insert failure's message is silently swallowed.
2. packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/arxiv_cli.py:1208 counts error rows in "generated N proposal candidate(s)", and line 1216-1217 (`pid = p.get("id"); id_str = f"#{pid}" if pid is not None else "(dry)"`) renders a failed insert in a non-dry run as "(dry)". cmd_gap_propose returns 0 unconditionally (line 1224). llm_client.py:153-155 confirms missing GROQ_API_KEY raises RuntimeError per call, caught by the broad `except Exception` at gap_propose.py:349.
3. Repro (scratchpad, venv /home/user/financial-abm-lab/.venv): Scenario A — all 5 LLM calls raise "GROQ_API_KEY environment variable not set." → CLI prints "generated 5 proposal candidate(s)" followed by 5 FAIL rows, exits 0, 0 rows persisted. Scenario B — dry_run=False with insert_gap_proposal raising sqlite3.OperationalError("database is locked") → every row prints "(dry)" (output also shows "dry-run: False" two lines above), the lock error appears nowhere, exit 0. Direct call of propose_from_top_gaps returns a list containing only {'_gap':..., 'error':...} records despite the docstring.

Mitigations that keep this at info (not minor/major): each LLM failure IS visibly printed as a "FAIL: ... └─ <error>" row, so an interactive operator sees the errors — only the summary count, exit code, and "(dry)" label mislead; grep shows gap-propose is not invoked from any CI workflow or script (manual CLI only), so the exit-0-on-total-failure has no automation consumer today; no data is corrupted (nothing is persisted on failure). The failure scenario is real exactly as stated; severity "info" is honest for a reporting/UX defect on error paths of a manually run research tool. The worst sub-issue is the swallowed insert_error rendered as "(dry)" — if the tool is ever wired into automation, this should be revisited.

confirmed.summary: The system prompt's family-selection guidance steers herding gaps toward 'kirman_ant', which exists in neither ABM_FAMILIES nor MODEL_BOUNDS, and the prompt hardcodes '9 成分' for feature_names in three places.
confirmed.evidence: `- 群集行動 → kirman_ant (将来) or lux_marchesi` — kirman_ant is not a key in abm_families.ABM_FAMILIES (only kirman-ant-switching exists as a technique) so a proposal targeting it is rejected by `target not in family_keys`; lines 43/53/65 hardcode the 9-component count that must track len(FEATURE_NAMES).
confirmed.failure_scenario: An LLM that follows the guidance literally for a herding-related gap returns target_model='kirman_ant' and the gap becomes an error row; if FEATURE_NAMES grows to 10 in a v5 fingerprint, the prompt still demands exactly 9 components while validation demands 10, guaranteeing rejects.
confirmed.suggestion: Remove the not-yet-implemented family from the guidance (available_families already carries the authoritative list) and interpolate len(feature_names) into the prompt.
confirmed.verify_notes: Verified all factual predicates. (1) ABM_FAMILIES (abm_families.py) contains 9 keys, none of which is 'kirman_ant'; MODEL_BOUNDS (adapters.py:53-115) also lacks it; 'kirman_ant' exists only as a subfield (subfields.py:61) and as technique 'kirman_ant_switching' (techniques.py:196), neither of which feeds validation's family_keys. (2) The system prompt hardcodes '9 成分' at gap_propose.py lines 43, 56, 65 (auditor cited 43/53/65; line 53 opens the block containing 56 — same substance), while FEATURE_NAMES (fingerprint.py:19-32) has exactly 9 entries today and validation (gap_propose.py:215-221) requires every listed name. (3) Offline repro via the dry_run_response path: target_model='kirman_ant' raises ValueError('target_model 'kirman_ant' not in available families ...'), which propose_from_top_gaps (lines 349-351) converts to an error row — exactly the claimed failure. Mitigations noted but not refuting: the prompt hedges kirman_ant with '(将来)' and constraint 4 restricts to available_families (filtered to MODEL_BOUNDS at line 329), so a compliant LLM picks lux_marchesi and the worst case is a graceful error row plus a wasted LLM call, not data corruption; the '9→10 guarantees rejects' part is slightly overstated since the payload carries the literal feature_names list and the operative instruction says to fill each key in it — the stale '9' is a real but latent contradiction. All of this is consistent with the claimed info severity: a prompt-hygiene/maintenance defect with graceful degradation.

confirmed.summary: fallback_used is recorded in the atlas dict but never rendered by _render_cell/_render_section, so the HTML artifact (the only output the user/PR reviewer sees) presents concept-anchored seed-fallback canon as if it were phrase-derived canon, and missing_canon() from a fallback run feeds --auto-ingest-missing which permanently ingests those concept-neighbour papers with no relevance gate on the OA path.
confirmed.evidence: build_atlas stores `"fallback_used": fallback_used` (line 149), but grep shows it is consumed only in arxiv_cli.py stdout (lines 1335, 1356, 1361: `marker = " (seed-fallback)" if entry.get("fallback_used") else ""`). _render_cell (242-258) and _render_section (261-303) read coverage/error/papers only. The fallback path deliberately skips title_any filtering (line 108 `if title_terms and not fallback_used`) and anchors on a heuristic deny-list concept (openalex._top_concept_id_from_seed, lines 441-455). cmd_canon_atlas then routes `missing_canon(atlas)` into ingest_by_ids/ingest_canon_via_oa (arxiv_cli.py 1385-1402); ingest_canon_via_oa inserts rows unconditionally with no relevance scoring (canon_ingest.py 129-149).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Weekly atlas_refresh runs anonymously (no OPENALEX_API_KEY in the workflow env) during a transient OpenAlex /works?search 503 affecting the two seeded subfields; both silently pivot to concept-filter canon (e.g. Lux-Marchesi seed resolving to a broad econophysics concept whose top-cited works are not chartist-fundamentalist canon). The PR's canon_atlas.html shows normal-looking cells and paper lists with no marker; the reviewer per the workflow instructions only skims artifacts. A user then running --auto-ingest-missing permanently inserts up to n_per_subfield off-topic journal papers into literature_methods, polluting the corpus feeding proposals/idea-novelty.
confirmed.suggestion: Render a 'seed-fallback' badge in the cell and section meta (and the anchoring concept name), and either exclude fallback entries from missing_canon or require confirmation before auto-ingesting them.
confirmed.verify_notes:
Attempted to refute; every load-bearing element of the claim held up. Verified by reading full context and by an offline end-to-end repro (monkeypatched network layer, temp SQLite in scratchpad; repo untouched).

Checked: (1) /home/user/financial-abm-lab/packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/canon_atlas.py — fallback_used is stored at line 149 but _render_cell (242-258), _render_section (261-303), and render_html (314-336) never read it; grep across the repo shows the only consumers are arxiv_cli.py stdout lines 1335/1356/1361 and tests. dashboard.py just copies canon_atlas.html verbatim. (2) Guards do NOT neutralize it: the fallback clears error (canon_atlas.py:99), so the "auto-ingest was skipped" guard at arxiv_cli.py:1343-1368 never trips for fallback subfields; title_any filtering is deliberately bypassed on the fallback path (line 108); ingest_canon_via_oa (canon_ingest.py:63-159) inserts rows unconditionally with no relevance scoring; the arxiv path's --min-relevance-to-keep defaults to 0.0 (arxiv_cli.py:2176) and even when set only skips extraction while keeping the metadata row (arxiv_ingest.py:554-561). (3) Repro (scratchpad/repro_fallback.py): simulated 503 on phrase search for the exactly-two seeded subfields (minority_game, lux_marchesi — subfields.py:44,53); build_atlas returned fallback_used=True/error=None; rendered HTML contained no fallback indication and no ERROR — cells render as ordinary coverage cells; missing_canon routed all 8 journal-only fallback papers to the OA path; ingest_canon_via_oa permanently inserted 8/8 off-topic rows into literature_methods in a temp DB. (4) Operational context is accurate: .github/workflows/atlas_refresh.yml runs weekly (cron Fri 23:00 UTC), step D runs canon-atlas with no OPENALEX_API_KEY in env (arxiv_cli.py:1349-1357 itself anticipates anonymous 503s), the PR carries only the artifacts and its body instructs the reviewer to skim them; stdout markers land only in Actions logs. Bonus support: subfields.py:52 notes Lux-Marchesi's top concept is 'Scaling' (0.79), which is on the deny-list at openalex.py:449, so that subfield's fallback anchor is an unverified next-best concept — making the off-topic-drift scenario more, not less, plausible.

Softeners considered (insufficient to downgrade): fallback cells may show a coverage drop (repro showed 0%), a weak anomaly signal, but indistinguishable in the HTML from legitimate phrase-derived low coverage; and a user running --auto-ingest-missing interactively does see the "(seed-fallback)" stdout marker in that run, but it is purely informational — nothing gates or confirms before ingest in the same invocation. Severity major is correct: silently misrepresented artifact plus unguarded permanent corpus pollution in realistic use, but it requires the transient-outage + auto-ingest conjunction and pollutes downstream proposal/novelty context rather than directly corrupting recorded research results.

confirmed.summary: build_tree cannot distinguish 'paper has no citers' from 'OpenAlex call failed' because find_citing_papers returns [] on every failure mode, so a mid-walk 429/5xx (which also latches the module-level rate-limit lock for 90s, failing all subsequent calls) silently truncates the citation tree and the HTML renders normally with no warning.
confirmed.evidence: Line 54: `children = find_citing_papers(parent_id, n=per_node, min_cited_by=min_cited_by)` — openalex.find_citing_papers (openalex.py 664-666) does `raw = _http_get_json(url); if not raw or not isinstance(raw.get("results"), list): return []`, and _http_get_json returns None for 429/5xx/network errors and for the entire 90s `_RATE_LIMITED` cooldown after a single double-429 (openalex.py 107-122). Contrast: find_canon_papers was given OpenAlexQueryError to surface exactly this failure class for the canon atlas, but the genealogy walk has no equivalent.
confirmed.failure_scenario: genealogy --depth 2 --per-node 20 issues ~21 sequential /works?filter=cites: calls; an anonymous run under OpenAlex load gets one 429 at call 3, the latch makes calls 4-21 return None -> [] -> next_frontier empties; the written mg_tree.html shows a tree of ~40 nodes instead of ~400, presented as the complete forward-citation genealogy of the subfield. The researcher draws gap/influence conclusions from a silently amputated graph.
confirmed.suggestion: Mirror the canon-atlas pattern: raise or collect per-parent errors in the tree dict and render a visible 'N lookups failed — tree incomplete' banner in the HTML.
confirmed.verify_notes: Verified all four links in the claim's chain against source and reproduced the failure offline. (1) genealogy.py:54/build_tree: find_citing_papers()==[] just empties next_frontier and the loop breaks (lines 75-77); no status check, no exception, no warning in the module. (2) openalex.py:664-666: find_citing_papers returns [] whenever _http_get_json returns None, so 'no citers' and 'API failure' are indistinguishable. (3) openalex.py:102-122: _http_get_json returns None on any non-200; a double-429 (initial + 5s-retry both 429) latches module-global _RATE_LIMITED, and all calls within the 90s cooldown (_RATE_LIMIT_COOLDOWN_S=90.0, line 57) return None without hitting the network — with default sleep=0.5 the whole remaining walk fits inside the cooldown. (4) Caller cmd_genealogy (arxiv_cli.py:1011-1077) never inspects _LAST_HTTP_STATUS/_RATE_LIMITED, prints counts, writes HTML, returns 0. Contrast confirmed: find_canon_papers raises OpenAlexQueryError (openalex.py:354-355, 369-370) and canon_atlas.py:83,100 catches/surfaces it; genealogy has no equivalent. Offline repro (monkeypatched _http_get_json_with_status, no network, script in scratchpad): root call succeeds with 3 children, next call double-429s -> latch True, remaining depth-2 calls never reach the wire (3 total wire calls), build_tree returns 4 nodes instead of ~63 with no exception, render_html writes a normal HTML with no warning/error/incomplete text. One precision note that does not refute: the failure_scenario's 'one 429 at call 3' strictly requires the 5s retry to also 429 to latch (the claim's evidence section states this correctly as 'a single double-429'); a persisting 429 is the normal shape of an OpenAlex anonymous rate-limit event (acknowledged in the module's own _api_key docstring, openalex.py:45-48), and even non-latching 503/timeout failures silently amputate individual branches. Severity major is right: realistic use produces a silently truncated research visualization presented as complete; not critical since no persisted data is corrupted and the printed node count gives a faint anomaly cue.

confirmed.summary: title_any filtering is applied after find_canon_papers has already truncated to the top n_per_subfield hits, so the coverage denominator silently varies per subfield (qualifying canon at rank n+1.. is never considered), making coverage percentages non-comparable across cells and letting a 1-2 paper filtered set render as 100% green.
confirmed.evidence: openalex.find_canon_papers requests `per-page={min(n, 200)}` and returns at most n works; build_atlas then drops non-matching titles: `papers = [paper for paper in papers if any(term in (paper.get("title") or "").casefold() for term in title_terms)]` (lines 109-113), and `n_canon = len(annotated)` (line 134) becomes the coverage denominator. Fallback rows additionally skip this filter entirely (line 108), so fallback subfields always have n_canon=n while phrase subfields have n_canon<=n.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Subfield 'kirman_ant' (query 'Kirman herding', title_any ['kirman','herding']): 6 of the top-8 phrase hits mention the phrase only in the abstract and are filtered; n_canon=2 and both happen to be in DB -> the cell shows 100% dark green ('lab well-equipped') even though canon papers ranked 3-8 under the filter were never fetched or evaluated, while a neighbouring subfield's 6/8=75% cell used a genuinely larger canon set.
confirmed.suggestion: Fetch ~3x n from OpenAlex, apply the title filter, then truncate to n so every subfield's denominator is n whenever enough filtered canon exists; or render n_requested vs n_kept in the cell.
confirmed.verify_notes: Read canon_atlas.build_atlas in full plus its only data source openalex.find_canon_papers and its only production caller arxiv_cli.cmd_canon_atlas. Every structural assertion in the claim is accurate: (1) find_canon_papers fetches per-page=min(n,200) and slices results[:n] (openalex.py:326,352,375), so at most n_per_subfield=8 works are considered and qualifying canon at rank n+1.. is never fetched; (2) the title_any filter runs AFTER that truncation (canon_atlas.py:108-113) and only shrinks the list; (3) n_canon=len(annotated) (line 134) becomes the coverage denominator (line 136); (4) fallback rows skip the filter via `and not fallback_used` (line 108, deliberate per tests/test_canon_atlas.py:182-184), so fallback subfields always have n_canon=n while phrase subfields have n_canon<=n. No neutralizing guard exists anywhere: no over-fetch before filtering, no minimum-n_canon threshold, no re-query on shrinkage; the CLI passes args.n straight through. Reproduced offline with monkeypatched find_canon_papers (scratchpad script, no network): a kirman_ant-shaped subfield where 6/8 phrase hits match only in the abstract yields n_canon=2, coverage=1.0, and _render_cell emits '>100%<' on background rgb(44,160,44) (full-saturation green), while a neighbouring 6/8 subfield renders 75% at rgb(96,183,96) and a fallback subfield keeps n_canon=8 unfiltered — exactly the claimed failure. The one soft spot in the claim is the word 'silently': the cell and CLI do print the '2 / 2 canon' fraction in small text, so the tiny denominator is discoverable; and the specific live-OpenAlex kirman ranking is illustrative rather than verified. Neither weakens the core defect — the heatmap's primary signals (percentage and color) are non-comparable across cells as claimed. Severity minor is correct: triage dashboard, no data corruption, fraction visible to careful readers, but realistic (title_and_abstract phrase search routinely matches abstract-only, and title_any is title-only).

confirmed.summary: The literature-snapshot restore step calls upsert_literature_metadata without source_kind and never calls set_oa_metadata, so in a CI-rebuilt DB any 'oa:W…' journal-canon rows get source_kind='arxiv' and lose oa_paper_id — the weekly canon-atlas step then reports all journal canon as 'missing', and downstream source_kind guards for synthetic ids are defeated.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 55-60 restore rows with `upsert_literature_metadata(DB, arxiv_id=r["arxiv_id"], title=..., abstract=r["abstract"],)` — no source_kind (defaults 'arxiv', db.py:285) and no oa_paper_id/oa_cited_by_count restore. cmd_canon_atlas builds db_oa_ids exclusively from the oa_paper_id column (arxiv_cli.py 1302-1306), and canon_ingest.py's own header warns 'Downstream tools that build arxiv URLs or scan PDFs must check source_kind'.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Once data/literature_methods.json is committed containing previously-ingested oa:W rows (the snapshot file the workflow already probes for), the weekly refresh rebuilds the DB with empty db_oa_ids; step D's canon_atlas.html shows every journal-only canon as red 'missing' although it was ingested, prompting redundant re-ingestion and misrepresenting lab coverage in the reviewed PR. Currently latent because the snapshot file does not exist in the repo.
confirmed.suggestion: Restore source_kind (r.get('source_kind','arxiv')) and oa_* columns in the snapshot loop, and include them in whatever exporter produces the snapshot.
confirmed.verify_notes: Checked: (1) /home/user/financial-abm-lab/.github/workflows/atlas_refresh.yml:46-71 — the restore loop calls upsert_literature_metadata without source_kind and never calls set_oa_metadata; db.py:285 defaults source_kind='arxiv'. (2) The snapshot producer (ingest_arxiv.yml:130-141) dumps load_literature(), which DOES serialize source_kind, oa_paper_id, oa_cited_by_count (db.py:374-420) — so the fields are present in the JSON and are dropped purely by the restore code. (3) arxiv_cli.py:1299-1306 (cmd_canon_atlas): db_arxiv_ids excludes 'oa:W…' ids via is_openalex_synthetic_id, and db_oa_ids is built exclusively from the oa_paper_id column; canon_atlas.py:116-122 marks a journal-only canon paper (arxiv_id=None) in_db only via db_oa_ids. No other matching path (no title/DOI match) neutralizes the loss. Repro (temp SQLite, exact workflow code copied verbatim): original DB with a canon_ingest-shaped row (arxiv_id='oa:W2091653681', source_kind='openalex', oa_paper_id set) → snapshot dump preserves both fields → atlas_refresh restore yields source_kind='arxiv', oa_paper_id=None → db_oa_ids=set() → in_db=False for the ingested journal canon, while the original DB gives in_db=True. So step D's canon_atlas.html would report ingested journal canon as 'missing' exactly as claimed. Latency confirmed: `git ls-files data/` is empty (no data/literature_methods.json committed), atlas_refresh step D runs canon-atlas WITHOUT --auto-ingest-missing, and atlas_refresh only commits notebooks/atlas/** — so the bug fires only after a snapshot containing oa:W rows is committed (realistic: task history shows a live 'auto-ingest all OA-only canon' run produced such rows locally, later cleaned per docs/canon_atlas_next_actions.md). One sub-claim is overstated: 'downstream source_kind guards are defeated' — repo-wide grep shows NO production code branches on source_kind (only canon_ingest.py sets it, db.py stores/loads it, tests assert it); the only operative synthetic-id guard is is_openalex_synthetic_id(arxiv_id) at arxiv_cli.py:1301, which survives the restore since arxiv_id round-trips intact. So the documented source_kind convention (canon_ingest.py:15-16) is violated in the rebuilt DB, but no existing code guard is actually defeated today (cmd_scan_pdfs_for_code ignores source_kind either way). This doesn't change the main failure scenario. Severity 'minor' is right: latent (snapshot file absent), CI-ephemeral DB only, wrong coverage reporting rather than data corruption — atlas_refresh never writes the snapshot back. Same lossy restore also exists in ingest_arxiv.yml:83-89 and methods_annotate.yml (out of scope for this claim, but ingest_arxiv's version WOULD rewrite the committed snapshot with the oa/source_kind fields nulled, which is the more damaging sibling).

confirmed.summary: filter_tree silently ignores exclude_keywords when keywords is empty (`if not terms: return tree`), so an exclusion-only invocation is a no-op with no warning; the CLI compounds this by only calling filter_tree inside `if args.keywords:`.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 90-91: `if not terms:\n        return tree` — the `excluded` list built at lines 85-89 is discarded. arxiv_cli.py:1064-1067 wraps the call in `if args.keywords:`, so `--exclude-keywords` without `--keywords` is dropped without notice, though the flag help (arxiv_cli.py:2089) only says 'reject even when --keywords matches'.
confirmed.failure_scenario: User runs `genealogy --root-arxiv-id ... --exclude-keywords 'racism,addiction'` expecting noise branches pruned; the rendered tree is identical to the unfiltered one and nothing tells them the flag did nothing.
confirmed.suggestion: Either honor exclude-only filtering (keep everything not excluded) or error out when --exclude-keywords is passed without --keywords.
confirmed.verify_notes: Read filter_tree in full (packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/genealogy.py:81-139) and its only production caller (packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/arxiv_cli.py:1064-1067). Both halves of the claim are exact: (1) genealogy.py builds `excluded` at lines 85-89 then hits `if not terms: return tree` at 90-91, discarding it; reproduced with a pure-function repro in mktemp — exclusion-only invocation returns the identical tree object with the excluded node still present (also true for whitespace-only keywords, since terms are stripped before the check). (2) The CLI wraps the call in `if args.keywords:`, so `--exclude-keywords` without `--keywords` never invokes filter_tree; grep confirms no warning anywhere and no argparse constraint linking the flags (the required mutually-exclusive group at arxiv_cli.py:2067 covers only root-source flags). Help text at 2089 ('reject even when --keywords matches') implies but never states the dependence. Refutation angles checked: the early return is a sensible guard (without it, empty keywords would prune all non-root nodes since matches() requires a keyword hit), and the help text partially documents the interaction — but neither neutralizes the silent no-op, and the failure scenario is real as stated: exclusion-only run yields an unfiltered tree with no message (the 'topical filter: kept X/Y' line is simply absent). Severity 'info' is correct: no wrong results under documented usage, purely a silent-no-op UX/documentation gap on a plausible invocation. Tests (tests/test_genealogy.py:104) cover only the combined case.

confirmed.summary: The verdict is never schema-validated beyond arxiv ids: 'category' is not checked against the four-value enum, so a malformed category silently disables the plan stage's category→implementation_type hard constraints (which live only in the prompt) and persists garbage into the ideas table.
confirmed.evidence: Line 265 `verdict = _call_groq(VERDICT_SYSTEM_PROMPT, payload, groq_model)` — llm_client only guarantees a dict. The only post-check is `_filter_verdict_arxiv_ids`. Nothing verifies category ∈ {trivial_variant, incremental_novelty, novel_combination, genuinely_novel}, confidence ∈ [0,1], closest_method ∈ candidate_methods, or closest_proposal_id exists. PLAN_SYSTEM_PROMPT constraints 1-3 (idea_plan.py:111-117) branch on the category string, enforced only by the second LLM.
confirmed.failure_scenario: LLM returns category='novel' or 'Novel_Combination' (casing/paraphrase drift). judge stores it, cmd_list shows it, and at plan time none of hard constraints 1-3 match, so the plan LLM free-picks implementation_type — the user's novelty-tier→strategy mapping (the core contract of the pipeline) silently stops applying, with no warning anywhere.
confirmed.suggestion: After the verdict call, validate category against the enum (case-normalized) and raise/flag on mismatch; clamp confidence; check closest_method/closest_proposal_id against the candidate lists.
confirmed.verify_notes: Checked idea_judge.py (judge_idea lines 244-275, _filter_verdict_arxiv_ids lines 278-321), llm_client.py, idea_cli.py (cmd_judge/cmd_run/cmd_plan/cmd_list), idea_plan.py (PLAN_SYSTEM_PROMPT lines 111-117, make_plan, scaffold), db.py (insert_idea/load_ideas), and grepped the whole repo for the four enum values. Findings: (1) llm_client uses JSON mode (response_format={"type":"json_object"}), not schema-constrained output — it guarantees only a dict; (2) the only verdict post-check is the arxiv-id filter; nothing anywhere validates category against the four-value enum, confidence in [0,1], closest_method in candidate_methods, or closest_proposal_id existence — the enum strings appear only in prompt text, docstrings, and test fixtures; (3) the category→implementation_type mapping exists solely as prose in PLAN_SYSTEM_PROMPT; make_plan does no Python branching on category and scaffold() validates implementation_type but never cross-checks it against the judgment category. Reproduced offline via the pipeline's own dry_run hooks with a temp SQLite DB: a verdict with category='Novel_Combination', confidence=3.7, closest_method='does_not_exist', closest_proposal_id=99999 flowed through judge_idea → insert_idea (persisted verbatim) → cmd_list display path → make_plan → scaffold with zero exceptions and empty verdict_warnings; scaffold happily wrote files for a free-picked implementation_type='new_method'. The failure scenario is real exactly as stated. Severity 'minor' is honest: the trigger needs enum drift from an LLM given an explicit enum in JSON mode (edge/latent), and the consequence degrades a constraint that is only LLM-prompt-enforced even in the happy path, with mitigations downstream (scaffolds are marked REVIEW BEFORE RUNNING, param_sweep plans get hard Python validation of target_model/params/rationale, --force-implementation manual override exists); no run/fingerprint research data is corrupted — one garbage metadata string lands in the ideas table.

confirmed.summary: Re-running `scaffold --id` on a param_sweep idea inserts a duplicate proposals row on every invocation and downgrades an 'executed' idea back to 'scaffolded', losing the executed marker.
confirmed.evidence: Line 130 allows re-scaffold for status in ('planned', 'scaffolded', 'executed'); line 145 `update_kwargs = {"status": "scaffolded"}` is unconditional; scaffold_param_sweep (idea_plan.py:227) calls insert_proposal unconditionally, so each re-run appends a fresh 'proposed' row (line 151-155 appends the new id to proposal_ids).
confirmed.failure_scenario: User scaffolds idea #3 (param_sweep), executes it (status='executed'), then re-runs `scaffold --id 3` (e.g. after tweaking the plan or by accident): a second identical proposal row appears in the proposals table (polluting rank_proposals context for future judgments and the dashboards), and the idea's status regresses to 'scaffolded' even though a linked run exists — `list --status executed` no longer shows it.
confirmed.suggestion: For re-scaffold of param_sweep, either update the existing linked proposal or require an explicit flag; never downgrade status from 'executed'.
confirmed.verify_notes: Verified statically and with a runtime repro (temp SQLite, no network/LLM — the param_sweep scaffold path makes no LLM calls). Checked: (1) idea_cli.py:130 explicitly permits `scaffold --id` when status is 'executed'; (2) idea_cli.py:145 `update_kwargs = {"status": "scaffolded"}` is unconditional; (3) idea_plan.py:227 scaffold_param_sweep calls insert_proposal unconditionally, and db.py:118-137 shows the proposals table has no unique constraint and db.py:157-183 insert_proposal has no dedup guard — no guard anywhere neutralizes the issue. Repro (drove the real CLI `python -m fingerprint_atlas.idea_cli` end-to-end): scaffold idea #1 -> proposal row 1, status 'scaffolded'; simulate the only executed-setting path (cmd_run --auto-execute, idea_cli.py:236-237: update_proposal_status + update_idea(status='executed')); `list --status executed` shows the idea; re-run `scaffold --id 1` -> proposals table now has 2 rows with statuses ['executed','proposed'] and IDENTICAL target_model/params/rationale, idea.status regressed to 'scaffolded', proposal_ids=[1,2], and `list --status executed` prints "no ideas." Also confirmed the duplicate 'proposed' row enters future judgment contexts: idea_judge.py:192-197 rank_proposals loads all non-'rejected' proposals, and the repro shows both rows returned with equal scores. One nuance vs the claim: "polluting the dashboards" is only partially true — analytics.py:50/82/115/154 filter status='executed' so executed-only analytics are unaffected; only n_proposals_total (analytics.py:155) and the rank_proposals/judge context are polluted. The comment at idea_cli.py:149-151 shows re-scaffold is a deliberately supported flow (provenance-preserving append), which makes the missing dedup/status guard a real latent defect rather than an unreachable path. Severity 'minor' is accurate: requires a manual re-run, no data corruption (the executed proposal row and its run survive intact), consequences are duplicate context rows and a recoverable idea-status regression.

confirmed.summary: cmd_run persists the judgment WITHOUT the 'warnings' key that cmd_judge persists, so arxiv-hallucination provenance (in_db_only/hallucinated) is silently lost for all ideas created via `run`.
confirmed.evidence: cmd_judge (lines 73-78) stores `judgment={"verdict": verdict, "matches": matches, "warnings": warnings}`; cmd_run (lines 176-181) stores `judgment={"verdict": judgment["verdict"], "matches": judgment["matches"]}` — warnings are only printed to stdout (line 182-184), never saved.
confirmed.failure_scenario: An idea judged via `run` had two citations dropped as hallucinated; a week later the user inspects it with `show --id N` to audit why closest_literature_arxiv_ids is thin — the stored judgment contains no warnings key, so the drop is invisible and the verdict looks like the LLM's raw output.
confirmed.suggestion: Mirror cmd_judge: include `"warnings": judgment.get("verdict_warnings") or {}` in cmd_run's insert_idea call.
confirmed.verify_notes: Checked idea_cli.py in full: cmd_judge (lines 73-78) persists judgment with a 'warnings' key; cmd_run (lines 176-181) persists only verdict+matches, printing warnings to stdout via _print_judgment (lines 182-184) and never storing them. No guard neutralizes it: cmd_run's later update_idea calls (lines 203, 226, 237) never touch the judgment JSON, and a repo-wide grep shows no code re-adds or programmatically reads judgment['warnings'] — only humans via cmd_show's raw dump. The scenario's premise is real: _filter_verdict_arxiv_ids (idea_judge.py:278-321) mutates the verdict in place, silently deleting hallucinated ids from closest_literature_arxiv_ids, and its docstring explicitly says the warnings dict is for the caller to 'persist alongside the judgment' — cmd_judge does, cmd_run doesn't. Reproduced offline in a temp SQLite DB using judge_idea's dry_run hooks and both insert paths copied verbatim: with a verdict citing two ids absent from the DB, judge-path row stored warnings {'hallucinated': ['2101.99999','1905.88888']} while run-path row had keys ['matches','verdict'] only and closest_literature_arxiv_ids=[] with no trace of the drop — exactly the stated failure. Severity 'minor' is right: provenance/audit-trail loss only; cmd_plan requires only verdict+matches, so no downstream computation or research result is affected.

confirmed.summary: cmd_run returns exit code 0 even when --auto-execute fails, so scripts/CI cannot detect that the end-to-end pipeline did not actually complete.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 239-242: `except Exception as exc: print(f"  ! execute failed: {exc}", file=sys.stderr); traceback.print_exc()` falls through to `return 0`. Judge/plan/scaffold failures all return 1, but the execute leg does not.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A CI job runs `idea_cli run --auto-execute ...`; execute_proposal raises (e.g. the extra-param TypeError from the unvalidated plan) and the proposal is marked rejected, yet the job exits green and the idea stays 'scaffolded' while downstream steps assume an executed run exists.
confirmed.suggestion: return 1 (or a distinct code) from the except branch, matching the other stages.
confirmed.verify_notes: Verified in idea_cli.py cmd_run (lines 161-242): judge/plan/scaffold exceptions each return 1, but the --auto-execute leg (lines 234-241) catches Exception, prints to stderr, and falls through to `return 0` at line 242; update_idea(status="executed") is inside the try so the idea stays 'scaffolded'. main() returns cmd_run's value via `raise SystemExit(main())`, so 0 becomes the process exit code — no guard elsewhere. propose_cli.execute_proposal (lines 280-282) marks the proposal status='rejected' and re-raises on model-run errors, matching the claimed scenario. Reproduced offline in a scratch dir with a temp SQLite DB and stubbed judge/plan/scaffold plus a raising execute_proposal: cmd_run returned 0, process exited 0, ideas table showed status='scaffolded'. The '[BONUS] EXECUTE' header could suggest best-effort intent, but that does not negate the stated CI-invisibility of the failure. Severity 'minor' is accurate: no data corruption (proposal correctly marked rejected in DB), only exit-code detectability in automation is affected.

ideas.title: Backup/undo layer for all destructive literature_methods operations
ideas.detail: delete-rows, delete-low-relevance, strip-arxiv-versions (conflict deletes) and fix-arxiv-ids all mutate/destroy rows containing paid LLM extractions, S2/OA enrichment, and hand-typed user_notes/user_tags, with nothing recoverable afterward — and a user has already mass-deleted 85 rows once. Cheapest path: a shared helper that dumps the affected rows as timestamped JSON next to the DB before any DELETE/UPDATE commit, plus a `restore-rows <dump.json>` subcommand. A step up: SQLite `.backup` of the whole file before --yes operations (the DB is small).

ideas.title: Referential-integrity 'doctor' command for arxiv_id-keyed tables
ideas.detail: literature_code_snapshots is keyed by arxiv_id but no delete/rename path maintains it, and the two id-migration commands can leave vN/base duplicates or orphans. A `doctor` subcommand that reports (a) snapshot rows with no matching literature row, (b) literature pairs differing only by vN suffix, (c) rows with suspect 7-digit ids, and (d) 'defective' extracted-but-empty rows would surface silent corruption cheaply; add --fix to cascade renames/deletes.

ideas.title: Write-time validation of LLM extraction payloads against the enum contract
ideas.detail: mechanism_tags and stylized_facts_targeted are stored as ", "-joined strings straight from the LLM with no enum validation, no lowercase normalization, and no guard against tags containing commas (which corrupt the round-trip split in load_literature). The entire stylized-fact-other command exists to audit 'other' dumping after the fact; a validator at update_literature_extraction time (reject/normalize non-enum facts, non-ASCII or comma-containing tags, out-of-range relevance) would prevent the drift instead of repairing it, and would have prevented the JA-token leak the code comments describe.

ideas.title: Scaled confirmation for bulk deletes
ideas.detail: delete-low-relevance previews only the first 30 victims and a single --yes flag deletes everything corpus-wide. Add: full victim list written to a file when >30, a required typed confirmation (or --force) when deleting more than, say, 20 rows or >10% of the corpus, and a printed diff of which coverage-matrix cells lose their last paper — the metric the atlas actually cares about.

ideas.title: Idempotency stamp for backfill-code (code_checked_at)
ideas.detail: pdf-scan and S2 enrichment both stamp rows so re-runs skip them; backfill-code has no stamp, so every run re-fetches arxiv author-comments (3 s each, un-persistable when the comment is absent) and re-queries the PWC API for every link-less row, and --force is currently indistinguishable from the default. Add a code_checked_at column set on every attempt, default-skip stamped rows, and make --force the re-check path — restoring the documented semantics and cutting minutes of redundant network traffic per run.

ideas.title: CLI smoke test that drives every subcommand end-to-end through argparse
ideas.detail: The three empirically confirmed crashes (canon-atlas NameError, gap-mine/gap-propose missing-runs OperationalError) all live in handler paths no test executes — tests/test_canon_atlas.py stops at build_atlas. A parametrized pytest that calls main() via monkeypatched sys.argv for all 33 subcommands against a fixture DB (network functions monkeypatched to canned responses, including the error/outage variants) would catch import errors, Namespace-attr regressions (which the team has already hit twice with --sleep/--top-n) and schema assumptions in one sweep. My ~60-line AST checker that cross-references every subparser dest against args.X reads per handler is a zero-network variant that could run in ci.yml in <1s.

ideas.title: Documented exit-code contract + machine-readable summaries for workflow gating
ideas.detail: Exit statuses are currently ad hoc (0/1/2 meaning different things per subcommand; gap-propose and all --auto-ingest paths return 0 on total failure), and CI workflows like atlas_refresh.yml can only scrape stdout. Define a small contract (0 = ok, 1 = partial errors, 2 = usage/upstream failure), enforce it in one place, and add a --summary-json PATH flag writing the summary dict every command already builds — then the scheduled workflows can fail or annotate PRs deterministically instead of shipping green runs with zero proposals ingested.

ideas.title: Source-priority and audit trail for code_url mutations
ideas.detail: set_literature_code_url is an unconditional UPDATE; nothing prevents an automated scanner from clobbering manual curation (demonstrated empirically via scan-pdfs --rescan), and there is no record of what a link was before it changed. Add a priority lattice (manual/manual_clear > abstract > comment > pwc > pdf) enforced in db.py, plus a tiny code_url_history table (arxiv_id, old_url, old_source, new_url, new_source, changed_at). This makes scanner-improvement rescans safe and lets a researcher audit why an idea-pipeline scaffold pulled a particular repo.

ideas.title: Post-ingest atlas refresh so the committed HTML reflects the closed gap
ideas.detail: canon-atlas --auto-ingest-missing mutates the DB after rendering, so the artifact the atlas_refresh PR commits is stale by construction. Since in_db membership can be recomputed purely from the DB (db_arxiv_ids/db_oa_ids) without new OpenAlex calls, a cheap fix is: after ingest, reload literature, re-annotate the already-fetched atlas structure, and re-render. This also enables a coverage-delta line in the run output ('coverage 72% → 96%') which is exactly the metric the workflow exists to move.

ideas.title: On-disk OpenAlex response cache + batched work resolution for expand/canon walkers
