ideas.detail: Three agent branches independently claimed spec numbers (trusting-clarke: 002-minimum-viable-arena; yh007: 002-yh007-kronos-microstructure + 003; future branches will collide again). Add specs/INDEX.md on main as the single allocation point (number -> title -> status: draft/active/superseded), and a CI structure-check line that fails when two specs share a number prefix. Cheap, and it turns the 'specs are ground truth' claim in README into something machine-checked.

area_summaries.summary: Audited arxiv_cli.py lines 1-1250 (all command implementations from ingest through gap-mine) plus their call chains into db.py, arxiv_ingest.py, and code_links.py, with temp-DB repros for the key claims. The read/report commands (list/show/search, coverage/atlas, S2 enrichment, snapshots) are largely sound, with careful rate-limit and stamping logic; the danger is concentrated in the destructive flows: stylized-fact-other --retag can wipe existing extractions on empty LLM payloads and report success (empirically reproduced), the --summary view prints a truncated-but---yes delete command that deletes only a prefix subset (reproduced), and the bulk-delete commands have no backup/undo while destroying user annotations and leaving orphaned snapshot rows — lead (a) and (d) confirmed, lead (b) refuted as stated (the parked pool prints a count; only docstring drift remains), lead (c) verified correct. Migrations (fix-arxiv-ids) lack the conflict handling their sibling (strip-arxiv-versions) has, and one flag (backfill-code --force) is a literal no-op.

The assigned slice (arxiv_cli.py 1150-2366: canon/canon-atlas/gap-mine/gap-propose/glossary/dashboard/genealogy/diagnose-*/enrich-expand commands, parser wiring, dispatch) is structurally sound: an AST cross-check confirms all 33 subparsers map 1:1 to the handlers dict and every args.X attribute a handler reads (including one via getattr) is defined by its subparser, so the historical --sleep/--top-n wiring bug class is fully fixed. However, error paths are undertested and three defects were reproduced empirically: canon-atlas crashes with a NameError (missing `import os`) on exactly the OpenAlex-outage path it was written to explain, gap-mine/gap-propose crash on any DB lacking the runs table (dashboard guards the identical query), and scan-pdfs-for-code --rescan silently overwrites manually curated code_url values despite set-code-url's documented survival guarantee. Secondary issues are exit-code inconsistency across LLM/auto-ingest paths (failures exit 0), a no-op --force flag, a swallowed insert_error, and a stale --db requirement for four DB-free commands.

openalex.py is a thoughtfully layered integration (status-aware low-level helper, phrase quoting, seed/concept fallbacks, generic-concept deny-list) but it is broken against today's live API in two places I reproduced end-to-end: the `publication_year:<=` / `cited_by_count:>=` filter operators are rejected with HTTP 400 (killing --year-max canon queries and silently emptying --min-cited-by genealogy trees), and arxiv-id extraction relies on an `ids.arxiv` field the API never returns plus arxiv.org/abs landing pages that old-style arXiv-DOI twin records don't have. The pervasive best-effort None/[] philosophy re-introduces on the OpenAlex path the exact silent-429 stamping bug the S2 path was explicitly fixed for: during the module-global 90 s rate lock, healthy papers get permanently stamped 'not found on OpenAlex'. semantic_scholar.py is smaller and sounder (old-style slash ids verified working; enrich CLI properly status-aware), with only silent-throttle and pagination-cap gaps in fetch_references; the unit tests for both modules mock invented API shapes and therefore certify the broken contracts.

arxiv_ingest.py and llm_client.py are thoughtfully commented but the comments have drifted ahead of the code in several load-bearing places. The three biggest issues: (1) llm_client's "billing" unrecoverable-marker defeats the entire retry mechanism for Groq 429 rate limits — a false-positive the repo itself documents in arxiv_ingest.py yet left unfixed at the shared layer used by propose/gap_propose/idea_judge/methods_draft; (2) the extraction path never validates stylized_facts_targeted against taxonomy.CANONICAL_FACTS despite the prompt's "choose ONLY from this fixed list" contract, so off-enum labels are stored and then silently dropped by the coverage matrix, corrupting coverage counts and gap mining; (3) the min_relevance_to_keep drop path stores nothing (contradicting its own comment "mark with low score"), so scheduled CI ingests re-pay LLM extraction for the same rejected papers every run, and extract-untagged later resurrects them with full extraction, bypassing the gate. A cluster of smaller robustness gaps (NaN relevance clamped to 1.0, stale token-budget arithmetic behind the 6s pacing, brittle TPM/TPD substring classification, broken o1/o3/o4 routing) rounds out the picture.

db.py is a clean thin SQLite layer: all user/LLM-supplied values are bound as parameters (no SQL injection anywhere — the only f-string SQL uses internal table/column constants), individual statements are correctly committed via the connection context manager, and the additive migrations are sensible. The real weaknesses are structural: (1) the per-call open/commit design makes multi-write flows non-atomic, and the proposal execute path demonstrably strands committed orphan runs (reproduced) that duplicate on retry and poison novelty distances; (2) comma-joined list columns (mechanism_tags, scaffold_paths, etc.) corrupt silently when an element contains a comma (reproduced); (3) status columns are free-text with COALESCE-only updates, allowing impossible/invisible states (reproduced); and (4) the CI snapshot restore contract loses a dozen enrichment columns per weekly cycle because db.py exposes no full-row restore. Concurrency (check-then-act migration and upsert races) and LIKE-wildcard tag matching are confirmed but lower-impact edge cases.

The three modules are functional for happy paths and reasonably unit-tested, and the taxonomy consolidation is a genuine improvement — but the consolidation stopped one module short: literature_map.primary_tag never received the normalisation/alias/deny-list fixes, so the branch's flagship 2D map still fragments mechanisms the way the coverage matrix used to, while both modules' docstrings falsely claim they mirror each other. The most concrete defect is empirically confirmed: render_markdown's column-totals row is off by one cell, so every published coverage.md shows column totals under the wrong fact headers, and the same table's row/column/grand totals disagree due to asymmetric excluded-cell handling. Secondary issues cluster around counting-rule divergence ('other'-suppression applied in coverage but not gap_finder, no extraction-era marker) and classification heuristics (ML-before-ABM substring ordering, deny-list bypass in the OA-concept fall-through), which distort the family bands and rows the atlas is meant to make trustworthy.

gap_finder.py and gap_propose.py are well-structured and have mocked-LLM tests, but the module's core outputs are quietly wrong in realistic use: the subfield matcher can never match hyphen-normalised tags for 5 of 25 subfields (empirically reproduced), view C can only ever cover 7 of 30 techniques because DOI refs have no join key, the 'other' catch-all inflates view A densities into spurious top gaps, and the salience scales are incommensurable so view B (family blind spots) is structurally starved out of the top-N that feeds proposal generation. On the propose side, the documented gap-to-execute loop is broken (executor rejects proposal_type='gap_mine' and would choke on the injected '_gap' param), and validation gaps (no value-range or numeric-type checks, empty-corpus scoping with silent reference stripping) degrade proposal quality without any signal. The existing tests encode rather than catch these flaws (hand-crafted salience, assertions on cells that are zero for the wrong reason).

The canon-atlas/genealogy area is well-tested at the unit level (mocked-network tests cover the happy paths, dual arxiv+OA matching, and the seed-fallback state machine) and the core per-subfield coverage math is correct. However, the aggregate layer and failure paths are weaker: the HTML's headline 'overall coverage' still uses a stale on-arxiv denominator (empirically renders 300%), the seed-fallback's provenance never reaches the HTML artifact the user actually reviews (and fallback canon flows ungated into auto-ingest), and the genealogy walk silently truncates on any OpenAlex failure including the module-level 429 latch. Secondary issues are edge-case bugs (filter_tree's stale duplicate remap drops edges, fallback never fires when all phrase hits are title-filtered, placeholder abstracts are permanent despite comments claiming otherwise) and a dead validation hook. Nothing found corrupts existing user data, but several defects can materially mislead the coverage/genealogy conclusions the atlas exists to support.

dashboard.py and i18n.py are in solid shape: all interpolated strings (curated catalogs, gap labels, error text, hrefs) are consistently html.escape'd, DB free-text (titles/abstracts/tags) never reaches these pages so the stored-XSS lead does not materialize here, the gap-mine section correctly consumes gap_finder's current (views, top)/Gap shape, the coverage matrix and db_path are wired correctly from cmd_dashboard, and all 13 dashboard tests pass. The verified defects are second-order: a canon-atlas candidate search that lets a stale copy permanently shadow a freshly regenerated atlas (reproduced), a misleading 'DB has no mechanism-tagged rows yet' message that masks swallowed render exceptions (reproduced), stale user-facing '6-feature' prose contradicting the 9-dim feature vector on the same page, i18n/glossary drift on gain-loss-asymmetry plus a dead herding entry, and a latent basename collision in the asset copier. Nothing found that corrupts data or research conclusions outright, but the staleness and silent-degradation paths deserve fixing before this dashboard is trusted as a daily research surface.

The idea pipeline's library core (judge -> plan -> scaffold) is competently built, with real safeguards: llm_client guarantees dict-shaped JSON with retry, judge post-filters hallucinated arxiv ids, and scaffold_param_sweep checks target_model and missing keys. However, the trust boundary for LLM output thins out exactly where consequences are largest: scaffolds write LLM-derived strings into Python files with no identifier/compile validation and silently overwrite existing (possibly human-completed) modules at name-keyed paths; param values and extra keys pass unchecked into the proposals/runs tables; and the module docstring promises a validation-and-logging step that was never implemented. The CLI layer is the weakest area — the promote-proposal feature is a 100%-reproducible dead-end, `list` crashes on legal DB states, and none of this is caught because the 30-file test suite never exercises a single cmd_* handler. Path-traversal via _camel_to_snake was checked and is NOT exploitable, generated code is never auto-imported (REGISTRY is static), and the cmd_run-vs-cmd_scaffold proposal_ids divergence flagged in the leads is benign (cmd_run always operates on a freshly inserted row).

The propose→execute→analytics loop and the preference/labelling loop are thoughtfully engineered at the prompt/UX layer (template-rationale blacklists, citation hallucination flags, per-provider retry logic), but the numeric core is brittle around exactly the states a long-lived research DB accumulates: NaN fingerprints and Optional measurement fields. I empirically reproduced four defects on this branch — (1) execute_proposal crashes with IndexError after committing the run row whenever any NaN-fingerprint run exists, orphaning and duplicating runs on retry; (2) cmd_auto reports successfully executed proposals as failures (exit 1) when prediction_error/novelty is None; (3) the UCB ranker puts NaN-fingerprint rows at the top of every labelling session; (4) zero-label 'next' is deterministic newest-k, not random as documented. Additionally, real-market replays in label_cli re-fetch Yahoo anchored at now(), so preference labels for real rows are progressively assigned against the wrong data, and the proposal validator checks only key presence, letting out-of-bounds or non-numeric LLM params flow into unattended execution. All three legacy leads from the main-branch audit are still present in this branch's code.

The core geometry stack (fingerprint -> standardize -> distance -> atlas/inverse-ABM) is coherent, well-commented, and covered by passing tests; MODEL_BOUNDS matches REGISTRY exactly (enforced by a test), the LHS sampler stratifies correctly, and the nested-params split matches the CIParams/FWParams dataclasses. However, two empirically-confirmed majors corrupt the production geometry: series_for_fingerprint reconstructs the GCMG series with a fabricated player count via a dead 'actions' fallback (all 12 production GCMG fingerprints are distorted, sign-flipped leverage/ACF features verified against the stored DB rows), and _aggregational_kurt_decay's near-zero-denominator ratio stores values like +21.4 and -12.7 against a documented ~[-2,+1] range, manufacturing spurious geometric outliers. A third structural issue — compute_hill=False writing a fabricated alpha=20.0 into every attendance-model fingerprint — biases inverse-ABM queries against MG/GCMG by construction. The remainder is minor robustness debt (NaN-unsafe nansum distances, CSV loader silently ingesting wrong columns/dropping BOM lines, non-strict gate JSON on failure paths) and stale 6-vector documentation from before the v4 9-feature upgrade.

The core methods store (methods.py) is clean, well-tested, and its CRUD semantics (None=don't-touch patching, idempotent seed) are correct. The risk concentrates at the boundaries: the markdown edit-file format has no escaping, so LLM drafts or user text containing '## word' lines silently truncate, misattribute, or destroy sections (reproduced end-to-end), and import-md will wipe all five commentary columns from a malformed file with rc 0; the daily methods_annotate workflow is structurally broken because its CI DB is re-seeded every run, so queue-next drafts the same method forever. code_links' extraction heuristics have four empirically demonstrated false-positive families (window truncation, missing host boundary, generic 'open-source' hint, prose-welding after '/\\n'), and its Papers-with-Code fallback is permanently dead (site now redirects to HuggingFace) while degrading silently to None.

The four curated-catalog modules are the package's ground-truth layer, and their code (simple data + helpers) is largely sound — subfields.py in particular checks out: both seeds verified correct (adap-org/9708006 is Challet-Zhang; W1537415400 is Lux-Marchesi Nature 1999), and the canon-atlas W-id fallback path is correctly implemented. The factual layer is much weaker: three curated arxiv ids are empirically confirmed wrong papers (a silicon-fracture paper still shipped as Lux-Marchesi in two files after being fixed in a third, a conformal-gravity paper as the Poisson-order-arrival reference, a Boltzmann-equation paper as the ABC reference), and five of nine ABM family reference cards describe idealized textbook models (LOBs, β-switching, Poisson arrivals, cancellation rates) that the actual registry implementations do not contain — with one fidelity claim being the exact inverse of the code. The validators that could institutionalize checking exist but are never called, DOI-format refs are structurally dead in gap-mining view C, and minor drift (6-dim vs 9-dim fingerprint, banned 富動 coinage back in i18n, phantom `glossary add` CLI, dangling your_impl paths) shows the catalogs are not regression-tested against the systems they describe.

The 30-file test suite is genuinely strong where it exists: 244 tests, all passing fully offline (verified with proxies stripped, zero skip markers), with excellent regression hygiene (version-suffix stripping, LLM-output coercion, hallucination filtering, canon fallback paths all pinned). The two systemic weaknesses are (1) coverage topology — nine modules including the proposal executor and the analytics that produce the project's headline learning curves have zero tests, and the one 'end-to-end execute' test is a hand-rolled mirror of the real executor, under which I empirically reproduced two crashes (NameError in cmd_canon_atlas's OpenAlex-failure path, IndexError in execute_proposal with any NaN-fingerprint run); and (2) CI — ci.yml does run the suite on Python 3.11, but lint is neutered with '|| true' while ruff currently reports 76 errors including the F821 that flags the canon-atlas crash, and push CI never fires on working branches. Tautologies are rare but real (a literal `len(...) >= 0` assertion, a 'non-empty' docstring guarded by `<= 5`), and fixtures mostly reflect the current multi-label extraction contract, with gap_finder/dashboard still testing only the legacy comma-string tag form.

The five workflows are well-commented and every CLI flag they call exists on this branch (verified against argparse definitions), and secrets are handled cleanly (never echoed, header-based OpenAlex auth, no fork exposure). However, the automated data loop has several broken joints: the weekly ingest snapshot round-trip silently wipes enrichment columns, propose.yml never restores the literature snapshot it is documented to use, the canon-atlas HTML artifact is gitignored so it silently vanishes from the atlas PR, the daily annotation queue can never advance in CI, and hard exit-1 semantics on flaky network dependencies (Yahoo, OpenAlex anonymous pool, single LLM errors) kill entire weekly runs including already-paid LLM work. No workflow has a concurrency group. The idea_plan scaffold (writes .py into packages/) is not reachable from any workflow.

The taxonomy core is genuinely in sync: both LLM prompts (arxiv_ingest, idea_judge) enumerate exactly the 10 CANONICAL_FACTS with the herding demotion, the coverage/gap_finder re-export shims are identity-true and test-pinned, every fact resolves to a Japanese label, and all 32 vocabulary-related tests pass. The problems sit one ring out from the vocabulary: gap mining has two reproduced correctness/design defects (multi-word subfield tokens never match the hyphen-normalised haystack, blanking market_impact/systemic_risk/santa_fe_market/el_farol and misattributing papers; and incomparable cross-view salience scales that bury all view-B structural blind spots), the coverage markdown totals row is misaligned by one column, and a cluster of stale comments/labels around the recently added facts (FACT_TO_FINGERPRINT exclusion comment, gain-loss-asymmetry glossary bypass, herding remnants, '6-feature' dashboard text) shows the mirrors that lack tests are exactly the ones drifting. Nothing found corrupts stored data; the risk concentrates in derived research artifacts (gap tables, coverage.md, literature map) quietly disagreeing with each other.

The analysis layer (coverage, gap_finder, fingerprint, atlas, preference) is well-documented and honest in intent — the commit trail shows real responsiveness to external review — but the statistical machinery under the figures has serious defects. Empirically confirmed: 20% of the subfield axis is unreachable due to a hyphen-vs-space matching bug, view B (family blind spots) can never appear in any ranked gap list due to incommensurable salience scales, the 'sigma' units in view B are fabricated under realistic baselines (std=1.0 fallback, pooled SPX+BTC spread), the salience formula inverts evidence ordering relative to independence-expected counts, and the user-facing markdown coverage table prints column totals under the wrong facts. Cross-cutting, there is no uncertainty quantification anywhere (top-15 gaps are point estimates over ~1,000 screened cells), no extraction-era marker despite a mid-corpus prompt change that the authors themselves proved changes per-column counts, and the fingerprint geometry has one unclipped feature (agg_kurt_decay, production range -12.7..+21.4 vs documented [-2,+1]) muting the axis v4 was built around. Fixes are mostly small and local; the bootstrap, prompt-version stamps, and Poisson-surprise salience would together address most of what a quantitative referee would raise.

The LLM-contract surface across the 6 call sites (arxiv_ingest extraction, propose.from_corpus, gap_propose, idea_judge aspect+verdict, idea_plan, methods_draft) is functionally wired but validation is uneven and lets malformed model output through. generate_japanese flags are mostly correct — False for the structured-EN extraction contract (arxiv_ingest, good), True for the Japanese-rationale consumers (propose/gap_propose/methods_draft/idea_plan/verdict) — with one wrong case: idea_judge.extract_aspects uses True for an English-keyed DB-matching payload. All DEFAULT_*_MODEL constants agree on 'openai/gpt-oss-120b' (no cross-module drift); CI intentionally overrides to gpt-4o-mini. The concrete defects: (1) llm_client's shared transient-retry is defeated for the common Groq billing-URL 429 (major, hits 5 consumers); (2) extraction writes out-of-enum facts and JA tags straight to the DB, silently dropping papers from the coverage matrix (major, the exact issue flagged); (3) promote-proposal->plan is a dead pipeline because the promoted idea carries no judgment (major); (4) predicted_fingerprint values aren't type-checked so a null crashes execute after orphaning a run (major); plus a null-category list crash, a JA-aspect matching degradation, unsanitized scaffold code injection, and a gap-prompt field the schema never requests. Category-1 (prompt promises a field the validator never checks) also covers propose.py constraint 6: references are 'mandatory when literature is non-empty' in the prompt but optional in _validate_proposal.

The SQLite layer is a single-file, single-table-per-write design with no relational integrity: every lifecycle (paper ingest, extraction, proposals, ideas, runs) is stitched together by exact-string ids and unguarded status columns, and the two migrations only touch literature_methods. The worst breaks are in the automated pipelines: proposal execution has a reproducible partial-failure window that strands orphan run rows and permits duplicate executions (also reachable via approve-after-execute); the gap-mine→idea→scaffold path is dead-ended twice (executor refuses gap_mine, promote-proposal creates unplannable ideas); and the weekly CI ingest re-pays LLM extraction for every below-relevance paper forever. Cross-source dedupe between arxiv and OpenAlex ingestion is exact-id only, so the same physical paper can occupy two rows and double-count in every coverage/gap statistic, while the canon atlas headline coverage uses a mismatched numerator/denominator (reproduced 400%). Read paths and the enrichment writers are otherwise solid and defensively coded; most bookkeeping columns (extraction_attempts, pdf_scanned_at, source_kind) exist but are inconsistently honored by their consumers.

The designated production DB (/home/user/test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db) is healthy only for its oldest layer: 126 fingerprint runs (all created 2026-06-27, valid JSON, no NaN/dupes) and 11 seed methods. Everything the 89-commit branch actually built on top of it is absent: literature_methods has 0 rows, and the proposals, ideas, literature_code_snapshots and techniques tables do not exist at all — so every assigned hygiene question (tag case-duplicates, stylized-fact enum violations, arxiv vN/oa:W hygiene, relevance survivors, orphan proposal refs, empty abstracts) is empirically vacuous, while branch commits and committed artifacts quote counts from a ~120-paper corpus that is persisted nowhere (the CI 'source of truth' snapshot was never committed, and I reproduced that its restore path would corrupt source_kind and drop OA/S2/user fields even if it existed). Secondary data-quality issues in what does survive: 25 preference labels are scripted test artifacts, methods annotations are 100% empty, 28.6% of runs sit at the Hill cap with the chiarella_iori family fully degenerate, and a committed genealogy tree for Lux–Marchesi is an empty single-node artifact.

Live measurement of the branch's test suite and CI: 245 tests, 245 passed / 0 failed / 0 skipped, ~67s wall (5 identical outcomes across repeated runs, reverse file order, and a fully network-disabled `unshare -n` run — the suite is deterministic and genuinely network-clean, with all arxiv/OpenAlex/S2/LLM calls monkeypatched or dry-run). The new test files are generally high quality (regression-pinned, contract-oriented), but coverage is sharply two-tier: core logic modules sit at 85-97% while the entire CLI/automation surface the 5 workflows execute is at 0-36% (package TOTAL 47%; arxiv_cli, the branch's biggest artifact, at 14%). The two empirically confirmed workflow defects — the literature-snapshot round-trip that permanently strips OA/S2 enrichment from the declared source-of-truth JSON, and atlas_refresh failing outright on the committed repo state — mean the scheduled automation would not survive contact with production even though the test suite is green.

Live smoke test of every offline arxiv_cli/propose_cli/idea_cli subcommand against a 15-row edge-case fixture (case-variant/alias tags, other+real fact combos, out-of-enum facts, vN ids, oa: synthetic rows, NULL relevance). Core read paths (list/show/search, tag aliasing, case collapsing, strip-versions conflict handling, delete dry-runs, proposals/ideas listing) are solid and the coverage PNG matched hand-computed ground truth exactly. However, four defects crash or corrupt first-class outputs: the gap-mining tensor structurally zeroes out ≥5 of 25 subfields (space-vs-hyphen needle mismatch), gap-mine/gap-propose crash on literature-only DBs, coverage.md's column totals are shifted one column left, and canon-atlas's failure path dies with a NameError. Several smaller issues (idea list bricked by empty idea_text, broken copy-paste delete hint, unvalidated LLM fact enum) reduce trust in the artifacts researchers actually read.

Core numerics are sound: I built a 12-paper hand-computable corpus plus synthetic run sets and verified build_coverage cell-by-cell (matrix, row/col totals, row_papers, mean_facts_per_paper=1.3, other-suppression, alias/case collapse, off-enum fact drop, herding-row vanishing, diagonal exclusion) and view B z-distances to 1e-9 against paper arithmetic — everything matched, and salience ranking (dense-row zero above sparse-row zero, ln(14) vs ln(10), top-3 verified by hand) is defensible as designed. The defects live at the edges: the markdown totals row is misaligned by one column (empirically confirmed, misattributing every per-fact total), the gap_finder subfield matcher is broken in both directions (multi-word needles can never match hyphenised tags, leaving 5 of 25 subfields permanently gap-invisible, while generic tokens like 'market'/'learning' absorb unrelated papers), and view B's degenerate-scale handling silently converts unmeasurable cells into 'no gap' and raw-unit deviations into false sigma claims. Since gap views feed the LLM proposal pipeline, the matcher fixes are the highest-value repairs.

Static health of the fingerprint_atlas branch work is good overall: all 43 package modules and 30 test files py_compile cleanly, every module imports in a fresh subprocess in under 0.5s with no ImportError or import cycles, all 5 workflow YAMLs and their 4 inline python heredocs parse, CLI dispatch tables are internally consistent (33 subparsers = 33 handlers in arxiv_cli), and the only TODOs are intentional TODO(human) scaffold markers. However, the sweep found one genuine crash bug (undefined `os` in cmd_canon_atlas's OpenAlex-failure diagnostic path, empirically reproduced, reachable from the atlas_refresh workflow) that CI's own ruff run flags but discards via `|| true`; an undeclared matplotlib dependency that breaks all plotting features for standalone installs of the wheel; and a cluster of dead safeguards (never-called catalog validators whose docstrings claim callers) plus a 6-D-vs-9-D docstring drift at the package front door. Production DB inspection (read-only) shows no live data damage from any of these.

The fingerprint_atlas analysis layer (coverage, gap_finder, gap_propose, canon_atlas, dashboard) is well-engineered per-module with honest inline caveats, but the evidentiary chain a thesis defense needs is largely missing: of the five external-reviewer demands, only 'other'-bucket auditing (partial) and multi-label extraction (shipped, but verified only on toy fixtures — the sole version-controlled corpus snapshot has 9 extracted rows at 1.0 facts/paper) are addressed; the row-normalized heatmap, the extraction spot-check protocol, and a rendered missing-not-at-random caveat are all still absent. The most serious structural issue is reproducibility: the literature corpus that every coverage/gap/novelty figure is built from exists only in an untracked local SQLite DB (0 rows in this checkout), while CI workflows promise a data/literature_methods.json 'source of truth' that is committed nowhere, so the weekly atlas-refresh cannot even produce its promised artifacts. Verified promised-vs-actual drifts include a canon-atlas headline coverage stat that renders 200% with journal-only canon (stale denominator), a misaligned totals row in the emitted coverage.md, the still-unimplemented canon cache/offline/post-ingest-rerender requirements documented in docs/canon_atlas_next_actions.md, and widespread stale '6-feature/8-family' text after the 9-feature/9-family expansion — including contradictory dimensionality inside a single user-facing dashboard panel. No generated figure carries any provenance (db hash, git SHA, timestamp), no gap claim carries uncertainty or a statistical null, and novelty verdicts consult only the local corpus — the 15 ideas target exactly these defense-critical absences.

Branch health: PR #7 (claude/vibrant-ptolemy-fapuxw, 89 commits, +15.6k/-193, CI green, mergeable_state=clean) is only 1 trivial README-wording commit behind main and merges conflict-free; its commit clusters are (1) LLM-client unification/robustness, (2) ideas pipeline + scaffolding, (3) code-link extraction, (4) S2/OpenAlex enrichment + arxiv-id hygiene, (5) canon atlas/genealogy/dashboard, (6) gap mining + i18n/glossary, (7) taxonomy + coverage-matrix cleanup, plus one junk commit ('a', c0a648c) that leaked generated dashboards and two LLM-scaffold stubs into packages/abm_models. The operational risk is not code conflicts but the cron ecosystem: main's LIVE scheduled workflows still open data PRs against this feature branch (proof: PRs #3/#4), the literature-snapshot accumulation loop has never closed (identical 30-paper snapshots twice, one stranded without a PR), the branch's .gitignore fix is corrupted ('*.csvdashboard/*.html'), and the new daily methods_annotate cron will re-draft the same method forever. MERGE PLAN: (0) on the branch, fix the .gitignore line and optionally drop the _idea_* stubs/root HTML artifacts; (1) merge PR #7 with a MERGE COMMIT — never squash: squash empirically converts PR #3 into 6 conflicts and PR #4 into 20 — and do it BEFORE Sunday 2026-07-05 21:00 UTC so the next ingest/propose crons pick up base:main; (2) delete the head branch via the GitHub merge UI so PRs #3/#4 auto-retarget to main, then merge PR #3 (seeds data/literature_methods.json, finally starting accumulation) and merge-or-regenerate PR #4; (3) delete stranded ingest-arxiv/28287737193 (100% redundant, add/add-conflicts with #3); (4) merge speculation-game-cleanup-4inq82 (audit docs, add-only) anytime; (5) merge PR #6 (yh007) — clean in either order relative to #7, only .gitignore overlaps and auto-merges; (6) close or rebase PR #5 (conflicts with its own base on specs/002; content likely superseded); (7) decide trusting-clarke's MVA fate (renumber spec 002→004 and resolve pyproject/uv.lock conflicts, else close) and close gallant-turing (off-topic personal notes); (8) before letting the newly-activated daily/weekly crons run unattended, fix the annotate-queue and snapshot-restore state loops. Post-merge NaN-bug status: preference.py is byte-identical to main and propose.py's changes avoid the NaN paths, so merging resurrects nothing — but main's audited propose/preference NaN bugs remain unfixed and should be patched against the post-merge tree (note propose._call_groq is now a thin llm_client shim, so pre-merge patches to it won't apply).
